CS 521.name: Foundations of Blockchain
CS 521.description: This course explores the fundamental concepts of cryptography and blockchain technology, tracing their evolution from early financial systems and cryptographic primitives to modern decentralized applications and advanced consensus mechanisms. It emphasizes both theoretical understanding through lectures and practical application via pair programming projects.
objectives: Analyze the historical development of money and cryptographic primitives that underpin blockchain technology.
Explain the architecture and functionality of major blockchain platforms like Bitcoin and Ethereum, including smart contracts.
Compare and contrast various consensus mechanisms used in distributed ledger technologies.
Evaluate the security vulnerabilities and attack vectors present in blockchain systems.
Implement core blockchain concepts or related cryptographic techniques in a software project.
Critically assess modern developments in decentralized finance, NFTs, and alternative consensus protocols.

topics.name: Basic Cryptographic Primitives
topics.description: Introduces fundamental cryptographic building blocks such as hashing, digital signatures, and symmetric/asymmetric encryption essential for secure communication and data integrity.

topics.name: Beyond Blockchains: Weak Consensus and Scalable Settlement
topics.description: Weak Consensus and Scalable Settlement: Explores advanced distributed ledger technologies and consensus models that deviate from traditional blockchain structures, focusing on scalability and settlement.

topics.name: Bitcoin
topics.description: Covers the foundational principles, architecture, transaction mechanisms, and proof-of-work consensus of the Bitcoin blockchain.

topics.name: Consensus Mechanisms
topics.description: Analyzes different algorithms for achieving agreement in a distributed network, including Proof-of-Work, Proof-of-Stake, and others.

topics.name: DeFi, NFTs, and Applications
topics.description: Covers the practical applications of blockchain technology in decentralized finance (DeFi), non-fungible tokens (NFTs), and other emerging use cases.

topics.name: Ethereum and Smart Contracts
topics.description: Explores the Ethereum platform, its virtual machine, and the concept and implementation of smart contracts for decentralized applications.

topics.name: History of Money
topics.description: Examines the historical evolution of monetary systems and their underlying principles, providing context for digital currencies.

topics.name: Scaling and Layer 2 Solutions
topics.description: Investigates techniques and architectures designed to improve the transaction throughput and scalability of blockchain networks, such as lightning networks and sidechains.

topics.name: Security, Attacks, and Formal Verification
topics.description: Details common security threats, attack vectors against blockchain systems, and methods for formally verifying the correctness and security of smart contracts and protocols.

topics.name: Zero-Knowledge Proofs (zk-SNARKs/zk-STARKs)
topics.description: Studies advanced cryptographic techniques that enable verifiable computation without revealing the underlying data, crucial for privacy and scalability.

CS 568.name: User Centered Machine Learning
CS 568.description: This course explores the principles, processes, and systems for creating user experiences that effectively integrate machine learning models. It focuses on the unique design challenges posed by data-driven products, including balancing automation with user control, and personalization with privacy concerns. Students will engage with interdisciplinary readings and complete a team-based research project focused on developing or analyzing data-driven experiences.
objectives: Analyze design trade-offs specific to user-centered machine learning applications, such as automation vs.
control and precision vs.
Apply guidelines and frameworks for designing effective human-AI interactions.
Evaluate methods for needfinding and data collection in the context of machine learning product development.
Critically assess ethical considerations, including data privacy and algorithmic bias, in user-centered AI systems.
Develop and present a multi-week research project focused on creating or auditing a data-driven user experience.

topics.name: AI vs. IA (Artificial Intelligence vs. Intelligent Assistance)
topics.description: Differentiates between fully automated artificial intelligence and intelligent assistance systems that augment human capabilities.

topics.name: Algorithmic Ethics
topics.description: Analyzes potential ethical harms, biases, and fairness issues that can arise from the design and deployment of algorithms.

topics.name: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)
topics.description: Discusses concepts and progress related to the development of Artificial General Intelligence, which possesses human-like cognitive abilities.

topics.name: Creativity Support with AI
topics.description: Explores the use of AI in augmenting human creativity, including applications in art generation and intellectual property considerations.

topics.name: Crowdsourcing Datasets
topics.description: Investigates techniques for collecting and labeling large-scale datasets using crowdsourcing platforms and gamification.

topics.name: Data Ethics
topics.description: Considers the ethical implications related to the collection, use, and documentation of datasets for machine learning.

topics.name: Decision-Making Support with AI
topics.description: Investigates the design and impact of AI systems intended to assist humans in complex decision-making processes.

topics.name: Design Guidelines for Human-AI Interaction
topics.description: Examines established and emerging guidelines for creating intuitive and effective interactions between humans and AI systems.

topics.name: Evaluation Metrics for ML Systems
topics.description: Addresses the challenges and appropriate selection of performance metrics for evaluating machine learning models in real-world applications.

topics.name: Feedback Loops in Interactive ML
topics.description: Examines the role and design of feedback mechanisms in systems where humans and machine learning models collaborate and learn from each other.

topics.name: Generating Datasets with LLMs
topics.description: Covers the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) for generating synthetic data to train and evaluate machine learning models.

topics.name: LLM-powered Interactions
topics.description: Explores how Large Language Models can be integrated to create novel and controllable human-AI interaction experiences.

topics.name: Model Interpretability
topics.description: Investigates methods and challenges in understanding and explaining the decision-making processes of machine learning models.

topics.name: Needfinding with Prototypes
topics.description: Explores methods for identifying user needs and validating design concepts through iterative prototyping.

topics.name: Prompt Engineering
topics.description: Focuses on the techniques and best practices for crafting effective prompts to guide the behavior of generative AI models, particularly LLMs and text-to-image models.

topics.name: Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)
topics.description: Studies architectures that combine retrieval mechanisms with generative models to produce more informed and contextually relevant outputs.

topics.name: User-Centered AI Design
topics.description: Introduces guidelines, processes, and systems for designing effective user experiences powered by machine learning models.

CHEM 102.name: General Chemistry I
CHEM 102.description: Chemistry 102 is the first semester course in introductory chemistry. The course covers the following topics: Principles governing atomic structure, bonding, states of matter, stoichiometry, and chemical equilibrium.
CHEM 102.instructors: Hummel, T, Marville, K, Gunasekera, B
objectives: How does the atomic structure of particles give rise to periodic trends.
How/why do atoms come together to form molecules in a variety of different ways bonding and structure.
How do molecules exist in three dimensions molecular shape.
How do gas particles interact with each other and the surroundings gasses and kinetic molecular theory.
What causes gasses to condense into other forms of matter intermolecular forces.
Why do reactions take place with defined ratios of reactants stoichiometry.
why do some reactions 'work' and others don’t thermodynamics.
why does a reaction stop when it does equilibrium and thermodynamics.
To apply the lessons we’ve learned in previous chemistry classes as well as the material wewill learn in this class to relevant real-world applications.
To develop critical thinking and problem solving skills that will be useful in this course as well as other courses that you will take throughout your college career.
We will do this by working through problems and experiments in class, the lab, and perhaps most importantly, onour own outside of class.
To gain an appreciation for the chemistry that is everywhere in the world around us (even if it still isn’t your favorite subject).

topics.name: Atomic structure and isotopes
topics.description: Atomic structure consists of a dense nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons in specific energy levels. The number of protons (atomic number) defines the element's identity. Isotopes are atoms of the same element with identical proton numbers but different neutron counts, resulting in different mass numbers. Isotopes have nearly identical chemical properties but differ in

topics.name: Aufbau principle
topics.description: The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill atomic orbitals starting from the lowest available energy level and progressing to higher levels. Electrons occupy orbitals in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, and so on. This principle, combined with Pauli's exclusion principle and Hund's rule, determines the electron configuration of atoms and explains the periodic table's st

topics.name: Balancing chemical reactions
topics.description: Balancing chemical equations ensures that the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides of the equation, satisfying the law of conservation of mass. Coefficients are adjusted to achieve balance without changing subscripts within chemical formulas. A balanced equation provides the molar ratios necessary for stoichiometric calculations. The process requires systematic adjustment and che

topics.name: Balancing equations
topics.description: Balancing chemical equations is the process of adjusting stoichiometric coefficients to ensure equal numbers of each type of atom appear on both sides of the equation, reflecting the law of conservation of mass. This involves systematic trial-and-error or algebraic methods to find the lowest whole-number coefficient set. Properly balanced equations are essential for all quantitative chemical calcu

topics.name: Bohr's hydrogen model
topics.description: Bohr's model describes the hydrogen atom with electrons orbiting the nucleus in fixed, quantized circular paths called energy levels or shells. Electrons can only exist in these specific orbits, each with a defined energy value. When electrons transition between levels, they absorb or emit photons with energy exactly equal to the difference between levels. This model successfully explained hydroge

topics.name: Bond energy & enthalpy
topics.description: Bond energy (bond enthalpy) is the energy required to break one mole of bonds in gaseous molecules, always an endothermic process. Average bond energies allow estimation of reaction enthalpies using: ΔH°rxn ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed). Breaking bonds requires energy input (positive), while forming bonds releases energy (negative). Net enthalpy change depends on the balance between bonds br

topics.name: Calorimetry & hess's law
topics.description: Calorimetry measures heat changes during reactions using calorimeters, which isolate systems to ensure heat exchange only between reaction and calorimeter components. Heat is calculated using q = mcΔT or q = CΔT. Hess's Law states that total enthalpy change for a reaction equals the sum of enthalpy changes for individual steps, because enthalpy is a state function. This allows calculation of ΔH fo

topics.name: Chemical bonding
topics.description: Chemical bonding involves the attractive forces that hold atoms together in molecules and compounds. The three primary types are ionic bonds (transfer of electrons between metals and nonmetals), covalent bonds (sharing of electron pairs between nonmetals), and metallic bonds (delocalized electrons among metal atoms). Bond formation releases energy and creates more stable arrangements than isolated

topics.name: Chemical equilibrium
topics.description: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in no net change in concentrations of reactants and products over time. Equilibrium is dynamic because reactions continue in both directions but at equal rates. The position of equilibrium (relative amounts of reactants and products) depends on reaction conditions but eventually reaches a characte

topics.name: Chemical laws
topics.description: Chemical laws are fundamental principles that govern chemical reactions and the behavior of matter. Key laws include the Law of Conservation of Mass (matter is neither created nor destroyed), the Law of Definite Proportions (compounds always contain elements in fixed mass ratios), and the Law of Multiple Proportions (when elements form multiple compounds, their mass ratios form simple whole-number

topics.name: Combustion analysis
topics.description: Combustion analysis is a laboratory technique for determining empirical formulas of organic compounds by burning a sample in excess oxygen and measuring the masses of combustion products (typically CO₂ and H₂O). The mass of carbon in the sample is calculated from CO₂ produced, hydrogen from H₂O, and other elements by difference or additional measurements. This method is particularly useful for det

topics.name: Common ion effect in solubility equilibria
topics.description: The common ion effect describes the decrease in solubility of an ionic compound when a soluble compound containing a common ion is added to the solution. This occurs because the added common ion shifts the dissolution equilibrium toward the solid (Le Chatelier's principle), reducing the concentration of other ions that can dissolve. For example, adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution decreases A

topics.name: Dalton's atomic theory
topics.description: Dalton's atomic theory proposes that all matter is composed of indivisible atoms, which are the fundamental building blocks of elements. Each element consists of identical atoms with characteristic mass, while atoms of different elements differ in mass and properties. Chemical reactions involve the rearrangement of atoms, not their creation or destruction, and compounds form when atoms combine in

topics.name: Dalton's law
topics.description: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of individual component gases: P_total = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... Each gas behaves independently, and its partial pressure is the pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume. This principle applies to ideal gas mixtures and is used in calculations involving gas col

topics.name: Electronegativity & bond polarity
topics.description: Electronegativity is an atom's ability to attract shared electrons in a chemical bond, with fluorine being the most electronegative element. When atoms with different electronegativities form covalent bonds, electrons are unequally shared, creating polar bonds with partial positive and negative charges. Large electronegativity differences (>1.7) typically result in ionic bonds, while smaller diffe

topics.name: Empirical vs molecular formula
topics.description: The empirical formula shows the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound, while the molecular formula shows the actual number of each type of atom in a molecule. The molecular formula is always a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula. For example, glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) has molecular formula revealing actual atom counts, while its empirical formula (CH₂O) shows the simplest ratio. De

topics.name: Energy of ionic bonds formation
topics.description: Ionic bond formation involves electron transfer followed by electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions, with energy changes at each step. The process includes ionization energy (removing electrons from metal, endothermic), electron affinity (adding electrons to nonmetal, usually exothermic), and lattice energy (bringing gaseous ions together to form solid, highly exothermic). The Bor

topics.name: Energy of phase changes
topics.description: Phase changes involve energy transfer without temperature change as substances convert between solid, liquid, and gas phases. Heat of fusion (ΔH_fus) is energy required for melting, while heat of vaporization (ΔH_vap) is energy for vaporization. These are endothermic processes requiring energy to overcome intermolecular forces. Reverse processes (freezing, condensation) release equivalent energy.

topics.name: Equilibrium constants
topics.description: The equilibrium constant (K) is a numerical value expressing the ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to their stoichiometric coefficients. For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD, K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. Large K (>1) indicates products favored; small K (

topics.name: Formal charges
topics.description: Formal charge is a bookkeeping method to track electron distribution in molecules, calculated as: valence electrons minus nonbonding electrons minus half the bonding electrons. Formal charges help determine the most stable Lewis structure by identifying which arrangement minimizes charge separation. The most favorable structures have formal charges closest to zero, with negative charges on more el

topics.name: Gases
topics.description: Gases are a state of matter characterized by particles widely separated, moving randomly at high speeds, with negligible attractive forces between particles. Gas particles occupy all available space in their container, have low density compared to liquids and solids, and are highly compressible. The kinetic molecular theory explains gas behavior: particle collisions with container walls create pre

topics.name: Gas laws & the ideal gas laws
topics.description: Gas laws describe mathematical relationships between pressure (P), volume (V), temperature (T), and moles (n) of gases. Boyle's Law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂) relates pressure and volume at constant temperature. Charles's Law (V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂) relates volume and temperature at constant pressure. The Ideal Gas Law (PV = nRT) combines these relationships, where R is the gas constant. These laws assume ideal behavi

topics.name: Gas pressures
topics.description: Gas pressure is the force per unit area exerted by gas particles colliding with container walls, measured in various units including atmospheres (atm), pascals (Pa), torr, and mmHg. At sea level, atmospheric pressure is approximately 1 atm = 101,325 Pa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr. Pressure depends on the number of collisions and force per collision, which increase with higher particle concentration, fas

topics.name: Heat
topics.description: Heat (q) is energy transferred between objects or systems due to temperature difference, always flowing spontaneously from higher to lower temperature. Heat capacity (C) is the energy required to raise temperature by 1°C, while specific heat (c) is heat capacity per gram. The relationship is q = mcΔT, where m is mass, c is specific heat, and ΔT is temperature change. Different substances have diff

topics.name: Hybridization
topics.description: Hybridization is the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new, equivalent hybrid orbitals oriented for optimal bonding. Common hybridizations include sp (linear), sp² (trigonal planar), sp³ (tetrahedral), sp³d (trigonal bipyramidal), and sp³d² (octahedral). Hybrid orbitals explain molecular geometries predicted by VSEPR and account for equivalent bond lengths and strengths when all bonds around an at

topics.name: Intermolecular forces
topics.description: Intermolecular forces are attractive interactions between molecules that are significantly weaker than covalent or ionic bonds. The main types include London dispersion forces (present in all molecules, arising from temporary dipoles), dipole-dipole interactions (between polar molecules), and hydrogen bonding (special strong dipole-dipole interaction involving H bonded to N, O, or F). These forces

topics.name: Intro to electromagnetic radiation
topics.description: Electromagnetic radiation is energy that travels through space as waves, characterized by wavelength and frequency. It encompasses a broad spectrum including radio waves, microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. The relationship between wavelength (λ) and frequency (ν) is inversely proportional, connected by the speed of light (c = λν). This form of energy requires

topics.name: Intro to thermochemistry
topics.description: Thermochemistry studies energy changes, particularly heat transfer, during chemical reactions and physical processes. Energy is conserved but can be transferred between system and surroundings. Exothermic processes release heat to surroundings (negative ΔH), while endothermic processes absorb heat from surroundings (positive ΔH). The First Law of Thermodynamics states that energy cannot be created

topics.name: Lewis structure
topics.description: Lewis structures are diagrams representing molecular structure by showing how valence electrons are arranged among atoms in a molecule. Atoms are represented by element symbols, covalent bonds by lines (or pairs of dots), and nonbonding electrons as dots. These structures help predict molecular geometry, polarity, and reactivity by illustrating bonding patterns and showing which atoms have lone pa

topics.name: Light & energy
topics.description: Light carries energy that is directly proportional to its frequency and inversely proportional to its wavelength, expressed by E = hν where h is Planck's constant. Different colors of visible light carry different amounts of energy, with violet light being most energetic and red least. When atoms absorb energy, electrons transition to higher energy levels; when they emit light, electrons drop to l

topics.name: Limiting reagent
topics.description: The limiting reagent is the reactant that is completely consumed first in a chemical reaction, thus determining the maximum amount of product that can form. Other reactants present in excess remain after the reaction completes. Identifying the limiting reagent requires comparing the mole ratio of reactants available to the mole ratio required by the balanced equation. The limiting reagent concept

topics.name: Limiting reagent concepts & percentage yield
topics.description: Limiting reagent concepts involve identifying which reactant determines the maximum theoretical yield of product. Percentage yield compares actual yield (obtained experimentally) to theoretical yield (calculated from stoichiometry): % yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100. Yields less than 100% result from side reactions, incomplete reactions, product loss during separation, or measurement errors. Th

topics.name: Molecular
topics.description: Molecular geometry describes the three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in a molecule, determined by the positions of atomic nuclei. While electron geometry (from VSEPR) considers all electron pairs, molecular geometry considers only the positions of atoms, ignoring lone pairs. The presence of lone pairs affects bond angles and overall shape. Common molecular geometries include linear, bent, trigo

topics.name: Moles & mass
topics.description: The mole is the SI unit for amount of substance, defined as exactly 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number). This allows conversion between atomic-scale (amu) and laboratory-scale (grams) measurements using molar mass, which equals the atomic or molecular weight in grams per mole. Mole calculations are fundamental to stoichiometry, enabling chemists to relate masses of reactants and products, c

topics.name: Movement of gases
topics.description: Gas movement encompasses diffusion (spontaneous mixing of gases due to random molecular motion) and effusion (escape of gas molecules through tiny openings). Graham's Law states that the rate of diffusion or effusion is inversely proportional to the square root of molar mass: rate₁/rate₂ = √(M₂/M₁). Lighter gases move faster than heavier gases at the same temperature. These principles explain phen

topics.name: Partial pressures
topics.description: Partial pressure is the pressure contributed by a single gas component in a mixture, calculated as if that gas alone occupied the entire volume. For an ideal gas, partial pressure equals the mole fraction of the gas times total pressure: P_i = X_i × P_total, where X_i is the mole fraction (n_i/n_total). Alternatively, using the ideal gas law: P_i = (n_i RT)/V. Partial pressures are essential for u

topics.name: Periodic trends
topics.description: Periodic trends are systematic patterns in element properties that correlate with position on the periodic table. Key trends include atomic radius (decreases left to right, increases top to bottom), ionization energy (increases left to right, decreases top to bottom), electron affinity, and electronegativity. These trends arise from variations in nuclear charge, electron shielding, and distance of

topics.name: Phase diagrams
topics.description: Phase diagrams are graphs plotting pressure versus temperature, showing which phase (solid, liquid, gas) is stable under different conditions. Lines represent phase boundaries where two phases coexist in equilibrium. The triple point is where all three phases coexist, and the critical point marks conditions beyond which liquid and gas phases become indistinguishable (supercritical fluid). The diag

topics.name: Phase transitions
topics.description: Phase transitions are changes between different states of matter: solid, liquid, and gas. Major transitions include melting/fusion (solid→liquid), freezing/solidification (liquid→solid), vaporization (liquid→gas), condensation (gas→liquid), sublimation (solid→gas), and deposition (gas→solid). Transitions involve energy changes and occur at characteristic temperatures (melting point, boiling point)

topics.name: Photoelectric effect
topics.description: The photoelectric effect describes the emission of electrons from a material's surface when light of sufficient frequency strikes it. Einstein explained this phenomenon by proposing that light behaves as discrete packets of energy called photons, each carrying energy proportional to its frequency (E = hν). A minimum threshold frequency is required to eject electrons, and increasing light intensity

topics.name: Physical properties
topics.description: Physical properties of substances include characteristics like melting point, boiling point, vapor pressure, density, solubility, and viscosity, which are determined by intermolecular forces, molecular size, and polarity. Stronger intermolecular forces result in higher melting and boiling points. The principle "like dissolves like" governs solubility, with polar substances dissolving in polar solv

topics.name: Polarity
topics.description: Molecular polarity describes the overall distribution of charge in a molecule, resulting from both bond polarities and molecular geometry. A molecule is polar if it has an asymmetric distribution of electron density, creating a net dipole moment with partial positive and negative regions. Even if a molecule contains polar bonds, it can be nonpolar if the geometry causes bond dipoles to cancel. Pol

topics.name: Precipitation & acid-base reactions
topics.description: Precipitation reactions occur when two aqueous solutions mix to form an insoluble ionic compound (precipitate) that falls out of solution. Acid-base reactions involve proton transfer, typically between an acid (H⁺ donor) and base (H⁺ acceptor), often forming water and a salt. Both reaction types can be represented by molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations. Net ionic equations show only

topics.name: Quantum mechanical model
topics.description: The quantum mechanical model describes electrons not as particles in fixed orbits, but as wave-like entities existing in three-dimensional regions called orbitals, where their exact position cannot be precisely determined. Instead of definite paths, we describe the probability of finding an electron in a particular region of space. This model uses quantum numbers to characterize orbitals and succe

topics.name: Reaction quotient q
topics.description: The reaction quotient (Q) has the same mathematical form as the equilibrium constant (K) but uses current concentrations rather than equilibrium concentrations. Comparing Q to K predicts reaction direction: if Q

topics.name: Resonance structures
topics.description: Resonance structures are multiple valid Lewis structures for a molecule where electron positions differ but atomic positions remain fixed. The actual molecule is a hybrid of these structures, with electron density distributed across multiple bonds. Resonance occurs when electrons can be arranged in different ways without changing the molecular skeleton, commonly seen in molecules with double bonds

topics.name: Solubility calculations
topics.description: Solubility calculations use Ksp values to determine the concentrations of ions in saturated solutions or predict whether precipitation will occur when solutions are mixed. Calculations involve setting up equilibrium expressions, using stoichiometry to relate ion concentrations, and solving for unknown concentrations. For precipitation predictions, calculate Q (ion product) and compare to Ksp. Thes

topics.name: Solubility product equilibrium
topics.description: The solubility product constant (Ksp) is an equilibrium constant for the dissolution of sparingly soluble ionic compounds. For a salt like AxBy dissolving as AxBy(s) ⇌ xA^n+(aq) + yB^m-(aq), Ksp = [A^n+]^x[B^m-]^y. Ksp depends only on temperature and indicates the extent of dissolution - higher Ksp means greater solubility. Comparing Q (ion product) to Ksp predicts whether precipitation occurs: Q

topics.name: Solubility rules
topics.description: Solubility rules are empirical guidelines predicting whether ionic compounds will dissolve in water. Key rules include: most nitrate, acetate, and Group 1 salts are soluble; most chlorides, bromides, and iodides are soluble except with Ag⁺, Pb²⁺, Hg₂²⁺; most sulfates are soluble except with Ba²⁺, Pb²⁺, Ca²⁺; most carbonates, phosphates, and hydroxides are insoluble except with Group 1 and NH₄⁺. Th

topics.name: Solution stoichiometry
topics.description: Solution stoichiometry extends stoichiometric principles to reactions occurring in aqueous solutions, incorporating molarity (moles of solute per liter of solution) as a key concentration unit. Calculations involve converting between volume, molarity, and moles to determine reactant requirements or product amounts. Titrations exemplify solution stoichiometry, where precise volumes of solutions wit

topics.name: Standard enthalpies
topics.description: Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound forms from its elements in their standard states at 1 atm and 25°C. By definition, ΔH°f for elements in standard states is zero. Standard enthalpies allow calculation of reaction enthalpies using: ΔH°rxn = Σ(n × ΔH°f products) - Σ(n × ΔH°f reactants). Standard conditions provide a reference point for comparing

topics.name: Strong vs weak electrolytes vs non-electrolytes
topics.description: Electrolytes are substances that produce ions when dissolved in water, enabling electrical conductivity. Strong electrolytes (strong acids, strong bases, most salts) completely dissociate into ions in solution. Weak electrolytes (weak acids, weak bases) only partially dissociate, establishing equilibrium between molecular and ionic forms. Non-electrolytes (most organic compounds, sugars) dissolve

topics.name: The hydrogen atom
topics.description: The hydrogen atom is the simplest atomic system, consisting of one proton and one electron. It serves as the fundamental model for understanding atomic structure and electron behavior. The electron occupies discrete energy levels around the nucleus, and transitions between these levels involve absorption or emission of specific wavelengths of light. The hydrogen atom's spectral lines provided cruc

topics.name: Valence bond theory
topics.description: Valence bond theory describes covalent bond formation as the overlap of atomic orbitals from adjacent atoms, with shared electrons occupying the overlapping region. The extent of orbital overlap determines bond strength - greater overlap creates stronger bonds. This theory explains bond directionality and introduces the concept of sigma (σ) bonds from end-to-end overlap and pi (π) bonds from side-

topics.name: Vapor pressure
topics.description: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by vapor in equilibrium with its liquid (or solid) phase in a closed system. It represents the tendency of molecules to escape from the liquid surface into the gas phase. Vapor pressure increases with temperature as more molecules have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome intermolecular forces. Stronger intermolecular forces result in lower vapor pressure. W

topics.name: Vsepr model
topics.description: The VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) model predicts three-dimensional molecular geometry based on the principle that electron pairs around a central atom repel each other and arrange themselves to minimize repulsion. Both bonding pairs and lone pairs are considered, with lone pairs exerting stronger repulsion. This model successfully predicts shapes like linear, trigonal planar, tetra

topics.name: Work & enthalpy
topics.description: Work in thermodynamics is energy transfer through mechanical processes, particularly pressure-volume work in gases (w = -PΔV). Enthalpy (H) is a state function representing heat content at constant pressure, defined as H = U + PV where U is internal energy. Enthalpy change (ΔH) equals heat transferred at constant pressure, making it convenient for measuring heat changes in typical laboratory condi

CHEM 103.name: General Chemistry Lab I
objectives: To provide experience to the students in the performance of certain basic skills and techniques in scientific experiments.
to understand how to perform data analysis.
to learn to make stop solutions and perform dilutions.
to use spectroscopy and Beer’s law to determine concentration.
verify a chemical composition by studying known decomposition pathways.
use the ideal gas law to determine the nature of an unknown liquid substance.
use calorimetry to determine the enthalpy of solution of known and unknown substances.
use calorimetry to determine enthalpy of fusion and vaporization.
use Hess’s law to verify enthalpy experimental data.
to understand how LeChatelier’s principle works and to apply it to chemical systems.
determine the equilibrium constant for a reaction experimentally.
to determine Gibb’s Free energy using spectroscopy.

topics.name: Beer's law
topics.description: Beer's Law (Beer-Lambert Law) states that absorbance of light by a solution is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the path length through the solution: A = εbc, where A is absorbance, ε is molar absorptivity (extinction coefficient), b is path length, and c is concentration. This fundamental relationship enables spectrophotometric determination of concentration

topics.name: Buret
topics.description: A buret is a long, graduated glass tube with a stopcock at the bottom, designed for precise dispensing of variable volumes of liquid in titrations. Burets typically measure volumes to 0.01 mL precision and are calibrated 'to deliver' (TD). Proper technique includes cleaning, conditioning with titrant, eliminating air bubbles, reading the meniscus at eye level, controlling flow rate, and recording

topics.name: Calorimetry
topics.description: Calorimetry is the experimental measurement of heat changes during physical or chemical processes using a calorimeter. In laboratory settings, simple calorimeters (like coffee cup calorimeters) measure heat transfer at constant pressure, determining enthalpy changes. The fundamental principle uses q = mcΔT, where the temperature change of water (or other solvent) reveals the heat released or absor

topics.name: Determining the molar mass of an unknown solution
topics.description: This laboratory technique determines the molar mass of a soluble unknown substance using colligative properties (such as freezing point depression or boiling point elevation) or gas laws. For colligative property methods, the change in freezing/boiling point caused by adding a known mass of solute allows calculation of molar mass using the relationship between concentration and property change. Al

topics.name: Empirical formula
topics.description: The empirical formula laboratory experiment determines the simplest whole-number ratio of elements in a compound through quantitative analysis. Students typically react a known mass of one element (often magnesium) with excess of another element (such as oxygen or chlorine), then calculate mole ratios from mass data. By converting masses to moles and finding the lowest whole-number ratio, the empi

topics.name: Enthalpies of solution
topics.description: Enthalpy of solution (ΔHsoln) is the heat change when a solute dissolves in a solvent, measured experimentally using calorimetry. When an ionic solid dissolves, the process involves breaking apart the crystal lattice (endothermic) and hydrating the ions (exothermic). The net enthalpy can be positive (endothermic dissolution) or negative (exothermic dissolution) depending on which process dominates

topics.name: Enthalpy of formation of magnesium oxide
topics.description: This laboratory experiment determines the standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f) of magnesium oxide (MgO) using Hess's Law and calorimetry. Since direct combustion of magnesium in oxygen is difficult to measure accurately, students measure enthalpies of related reactions (such as Mg reacting with HCl and MgO reacting with HCl) and use Hess's Law to calculate ΔH°f indirectly. This experiment demonst

topics.name: Food dyes
topics.description: The food dyes laboratory experiment uses spectrophotometry and Beer's Law to determine the concentration and identity of dyes in commercial food products. Students create standard curves for known dyes (such as Red 40, Yellow 5, Blue 1) by measuring absorbance at characteristic wavelengths, then analyze unknown samples by comparing their absorbance spectra. This practical application demonstrates

topics.name: Hess's law
topics.description: Hess's Law states that the total enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is independent of the pathway and depends only on initial and final states. In laboratory applications, students combine multiple measurable reactions algebraically to determine the enthalpy change of a target reaction that may be difficult or impossible to measure directly. This practical application requires balancing equat

topics.name: Laboratory equipment and the balance
topics.description: Laboratory equipment encompasses the tools and instruments used for chemical experimentation, with the analytical balance being fundamental for precise mass measurements. Balances measure mass to several decimal places (typically 0.001g or better) and require proper technique including taring, calibration, and avoiding contamination. Understanding equipment names, functions, and proper usage is es

topics.name: Laboratory orientation & safety
topics.description: Laboratory orientation and safety encompasses essential protocols, procedures, and safety measures required for working in a chemistry laboratory. This includes understanding Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS), proper use of personal protective equipment (PPE), emergency procedures, chemical handling and disposal, location of safety equipment like eyewash stations and fire extinguishers, and gener

topics.name: Lechatelier's principle
topics.description: Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium experiences a disturbance (stress), it responds by shifting to counteract that stress and re-establish equilibrium. In laboratory demonstrations, students observe how changes in concentration, temperature, or pressure affect equilibrium position. For example, adding reactants shifts equilibrium toward products, increasing temperatur

topics.name: Molecule maker lab
topics.description: The molecule maker lab involves building three-dimensional molecular models using physical model kits to visualize molecular geometry, bond angles, and spatial arrangements. Students construct molecules to understand VSEPR theory, identify electron and molecular geometries, recognize different hybridizations, and see how molecular shape affects properties. This hands-on activity reinforces abstrac

topics.name: Serial dilutions
topics.description: Serial dilution is a systematic technique for creating progressively more dilute solutions by repeatedly diluting a stock solution by a constant factor. Each dilution step uses a portion of the previous solution as the starting material for the next dilution, often following patterns like 1:10 or 1:2 ratios. This method is essential for preparing standard solutions for calibration curves, generati

topics.name: Standard curves
topics.description: A standard curve (calibration curve) is a graph plotting known concentrations of a substance against corresponding measured values (such as absorbance in spectroscopy). By measuring standards of known concentration and plotting the relationship, unknown sample concentrations can be determined by interpolation. The curve establishes a quantitative relationship between concentration and measurement,

topics.name: The mohr pipet
topics.description: The Mohr pipet (also called graduated or measuring pipet) is a glass tube with graduated markings used to accurately measure and transfer variable volumes of liquids. Unlike volumetric pipets that deliver one fixed volume, Mohr pipets allow measurement of multiple volumes with reasonable accuracy. Proper technique includes using a pipet bulb (never mouth pipetting), reading the meniscus at eye lev

topics.name: Titration
topics.description: Titration is a quantitative analytical technique where a solution of known concentration (titrant) is gradually added to a solution of unknown concentration (analyte) until the reaction reaches completion, indicated by an endpoint. The volume of titrant required to reach equivalence point allows calculation of the unknown concentration using stoichiometry. Common types include acid-base, redox, an

topics.name: Using the calorimeter to measure changes of state
topics.description: This laboratory application uses calorimetry to experimentally determine enthalpies of phase transitions (fusion, vaporization, solidification, condensation) by measuring heat transfer during state changes. When a substance undergoes a phase change at constant temperature, the heat absorbed or released can be calculated from temperature changes in surrounding water. For example, adding ice to warm

MATH 221.name: Calculus I
MATH 221.description: First course in calculus and analytic geometry for students with some calculus background; basic techniques of differentiation and integration with applications including curve sketching; antidifferentation, the Riemann integral, fundamental theorem, exponential and trigonometric functions.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus.

topics.name: Antiderivatives
topics.description: An antiderivative (indefinite integral) of f(x) is a function F(x) whose derivative is f(x): F'(x) = f(x). Antiderivatives differ by constants, so the general antiderivative is F(x) + C where C is an arbitrary constant. Finding antiderivatives reverses differentiation, requiring recognition of derivative patterns. Basic antiderivative formulas include: ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C, ∫e^x dx = e^x +

topics.name: Areas and distance
topics.description: Areas under curves and distances traveled are computed by summing infinitely many infinitesimally small rectangles or increments, motivating the definite integral. The area under f(x) from a to b is approximated using Riemann sums: Σf(x_i)Δx, which becomes exact as Δx→0, yielding ∫[a,b] f(x)dx. Similarly, distance traveled equals ∫velocity dt, and net change equals ∫(rate of change)dt. These geome

topics.name: Areas between curves
topics.description: The area between two curves f(x) and g(x) from x=a to x=b, where f(x) ≥ g(x), is computed as ∫[a,b] [f(x) - g(x)]dx. This extends single-function area calculations by subtracting the lower curve from the upper curve. For curves intersecting multiple times, split the integral at intersection points and determine which function is greater on each subinterval. When curves are better described as func

topics.name: Average value of a function
topics.description: The average value of a continuous function f(x) on interval [a,b] is f_avg = (1/(b-a))∫[a,b] f(x)dx. This extends the discrete average concept (sum divided by count) to continuous functions by integrating over the interval and dividing by interval length. The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals guarantees existence of at least one point c where f(c) equals this average value. Applications include fin

topics.name: Calculating limits using the limit laws
topics.description: Limit laws are algebraic rules for evaluating limits of combinations of functions, including sums, differences, products, quotients, and compositions. Key laws state that limits distribute over addition, subtraction, multiplication, division (when denominator limit ≠ 0), and powers. The direct substitution property allows evaluation by simply plugging in the value for continuous functions. Student

topics.name: Continuity
topics.description: A function f is continuous at x = a if: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim(x→a) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a). Intuitively, a function is continuous if its graph can be drawn without lifting the pencil. Discontinuities include removable (hole), jump (sudden change), and infinite (vertical asymptote). Continuous functions have important properties including the Intermediate Value Theorem. Stu

topics.name: Derivative as a function
topics.description: The derivative f'(x) is itself a function giving the instantaneous rate of change or slope of the tangent line at each point x in the domain. Defined as f'(x) = lim(h→0) [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h, it transforms one function into another that describes rates of change. The derivative can be interpreted geometrically (slope), physically (velocity, acceleration), or algebraically (rate of change). Students lea

topics.name: Derivatives of logarithmic functions
topics.description: Logarithmic function derivatives include: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x and d/dx[log_a x] = 1/(x ln a). For logarithms of functions, use the chain rule: d/dx[ln(g(x))] = g'(x)/g(x). Logarithmic differentiation is a technique using properties of logarithms to simplify differentiation of complicated products, quotients, or powers by taking ln of both sides, then differentiating implicitly. This method is particu

topics.name: Derivatives of polynomials and exponential functions
topics.description: Polynomial derivatives follow the power rule: d/dx[x^n] = nx^(n-1), applied term-by-term with constant multiples factored out. Exponential function derivatives have the unique property: d/dx[e^x] = e^x, and more generally d/dx[a^x] = (ln a)·a^x. The constant rule states d/dx[c] = 0. These basic rules, combined with linearity (sum rule, constant multiple rule), allow differentiation of any polynomi

topics.name: Derivatives of trigonometric functions
topics.description: Trigonometric function derivatives include: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = -sin x, d/dx[tan x] = sec²x, d/dx[cot x] = -csc²x, d/dx[sec x] = sec x tan x, and d/dx[csc x] = -csc x cot x. These derivatives require angles in radians and follow patterns worth memorizing. Students learn these formulas, understand their derivation from limits of trigonometric ratios, and combine them with previously

topics.name: Exponential growth and decay
topics.description: Exponential growth and decay models describe quantities changing at rates proportional to their current value: dy/dt = ky, with solution y(t) = y₀e^(kt). When k > 0, the model describes growth (population, compound interest); when k

topics.name: Horizonal asymptotes
topics.description: Horizontal asymptotes are horizontal lines y = L that a function approaches as x → ±∞. They represent the limit of f(x) as x approaches infinity: if lim(x→∞) f(x) = L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote. A function can have at most two horizontal asymptotes (one as x→∞, one as x→-∞), unlike vertical asymptotes of which there may be many. Horizontal asymptotes describe long-term function behavior

topics.name: How derivatives affect the shape of a graph
topics.description: Derivatives reveal graph behavior: f' > 0 indicates increasing function, f'

topics.name: Hyperbolic functions
topics.description: Hyperbolic functions are exponential combinations: sinh x = (e^x - e^(-x))/2, cosh x = (e^x + e^(-x))/2, and tanh x = sinh x/cosh x, with reciprocals csch, sech, and coth. They satisfy identities analogous to trigonometric identities (e.g., cosh²x - sinh²x = 1) and have derivatives resembling trig derivatives but with different signs. Hyperbolic functions appear in applications including hanging c

topics.name: Implicit differentiation
topics.description: Implicit differentiation finds derivatives when the relationship between variables is given implicitly (e.g., x² + y² = 25) rather than explicitly as y = f(x). The technique applies d/dx to both sides of the equation, treating y as a function of x and using the chain rule to obtain dy/dx terms. Terms with dy/dx are collected and solved for dy/dx algebraically. This method handles equations that ar

topics.name: Indefinite integrals and the net change theorem
topics.description: Indefinite integrals ∫f(x)dx represent the family of all antiderivatives F(x) + C of f(x). The Net Change Theorem states that ∫[a,b] F'(x)dx = F(b) - F(a), meaning the integral of a rate of change equals the total net change. Applications include finding displacement from velocity, total amount from rate of flow, and change in quantity from rate of change. This theorem formalizes how integration a

topics.name: Indeterminate forms of l'hospital's rule
topics.description: L'Hôpital's Rule evaluates limits of indeterminate forms 0/0 or ∞/∞ by replacing them with limits of derivatives: if lim f(x) and lim g(x) both approach 0 or both approach ∞, then lim[f(x)/g(x)] = lim[f'(x)/g'(x)] (if the latter exists). Other indeterminate forms (0·∞, ∞-∞, 0^0, 1^∞, ∞^0) can be algebraically converted to 0/0 or ∞/∞ forms. Students learn to identify indeterminate forms, apply L'Hô

topics.name: Limit of a function
topics.description: The limit of a function describes the value a function approaches as the input approaches some value, written as lim(x→a) f(x) = L. Limits may exist even when the function is undefined at the point. One-sided limits (left-hand and right-hand) must agree for a two-sided limit to exist. Limits form the foundation for continuity, derivatives, and integrals. Students learn to evaluate limits using gra

topics.name: Limits at infinity
topics.description: Limits at infinity describe function behavior as x approaches ±∞, written as lim(x→∞) f(x) or lim(x→-∞) f(x). These limits determine end behavior of functions and horizontal asymptotes. For rational functions, the limit depends on degree comparison of numerator and denominator. Students learn techniques including dividing by highest power, recognizing dominant terms, and applying rules for exponen

topics.name: Linear approximations and differentials
topics.description: Linear approximation uses the tangent line at x = a to estimate function values near a: f(x) ≈ f(a) + f'(a)(x-a). This approximation is good when |x-a| is small. Differentials provide notation for small changes: dy = f'(x)dx represents the change in the tangent line approximation when x changes by dx. Differentials estimate error propagation and approximate function values. Students learn when lin

topics.name: Maximum and minimum values
topics.description: Maximum and minimum values (extrema) are the largest and smallest values a function attains on an interval. Absolute (global) extrema are highest/lowest overall; relative (local) extrema are highest/lowest in a neighborhood. The Extreme Value Theorem guarantees continuous functions on closed intervals have absolute extrema. Critical points (where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) doesn't exist) and endpoints are

topics.name: Mean value theorem
topics.description: The Mean Value Theorem (MVT) states that if f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) where f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a). Geometrically, there's a point where the tangent line is parallel to the secant line connecting endpoints. The MVT has important consequences including proving functions with zero derivative are constant. Rolle's Theorem is a s

topics.name: Newton's method
topics.description: Newton's Method is an iterative algorithm for approximating roots of equations f(x) = 0 using the recurrence relation: x_(n+1) = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Starting from an initial guess x_0, the method generates successively better approximations by following tangent lines to x-intercepts. Newton's Method typically converges rapidly (quadratically) when near a root, but may fail if f'(x_n) ≈ 0, the in

topics.name: Optimization problems
topics.description: Optimization problems find maximum or minimum values of quantities in applied contexts, such as maximizing profit, minimizing cost, or optimizing dimensions. The method involves: identifying the quantity to optimize (objective function), expressing it in terms of one variable using constraints, finding critical points by differentiating and setting f'(x) = 0, and verifying the solution is a maximu

topics.name: Rates of change in the natural and social sciences
topics.description: Derivatives quantify rates of change across disciplines: velocity and acceleration in physics, population growth rates in biology, marginal cost and revenue in economics, and reaction rates in chemistry. The instantaneous rate of change f'(a) measures how quickly the dependent variable changes with respect to the independent variable at a specific point. Students apply derivatives to real-world pr

topics.name: Related rates
topics.description: Related rates problems involve finding how fast one quantity changes given information about how fast related quantities change. The technique uses the chain rule to differentiate equations relating multiple time-varying quantities with respect to time. Key steps include: draw and label diagrams, write equations relating variables, differentiate both sides with respect to time using implicit diffe

topics.name: Summary of curve sketching
topics.description: Curve sketching synthesizes derivative information to draw accurate function graphs. The systematic process includes: find domain, identify intercepts, test for symmetry, find asymptotes, determine intervals of increase/decrease using f', locate local extrema, determine concavity using f'', find inflection points, and sketch combining all information. This comprehensive analysis reveals function b

topics.name: Tangent and velocity problems
topics.description: Tangent and velocity problems introduce the derivative concept through geometric (slope of tangent line to a curve) and physical (instantaneous velocity of a moving object) interpretations. The tangent line touches a curve at exactly one point with the same slope as the curve at that point. Instantaneous velocity is the limit of average velocity as time interval approaches zero. Both problems requ

topics.name: The chain rule
topics.description: The chain rule differentiates composite functions: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x))·g'(x), often written as dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx). This 'outside-inside' rule first differentiates the outer function evaluated at the inner function, then multiplies by the derivative of the inner function. The chain rule is essential for differentiating complex expressions and appears throughout calculus. Students learn to

topics.name: The definite integral
topics.description: The definite integral ∫[a,b] f(x)dx represents the signed area between f(x) and the x-axis from x=a to x=b, defined as the limit of Riemann sums as partition width approaches zero. Positive areas (f(x) > 0) count positively; negative areas (f(x)

topics.name: The fundamental
topics.description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1, states that if F'(x) = f(x), then ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a). This connects differentiation and integration as inverse operations, providing a practical method for evaluating definite integrals without Riemann sums. Part 2 states that d/dx[∫[a,x] f(t)dt] = f(x), showing that differentiation undoes integration. Together, these theorems are calculus's ce

topics.name: Theorem of calculus
topics.description: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus establishes the profound connection between differentiation and integration, consisting of two parts. Part 1 provides the evaluation formula: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F is any antiderivative of f. Part 2 states d/dx[∫[a,x] f(t)dt] = f(x), showing integration followed by differentiation returns the original function. This theorem revolutionized mathemati

topics.name: The precise definition of a limit
topics.description: The epsilon-delta (ε-δ) definition rigorously formalizes limits: lim(x→a) f(x) = L means for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if 0

topics.name: The product an quotient rules
topics.description: The product rule states d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x) - the derivative of a product is NOT the product of derivatives. The quotient rule states d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [g(x)f'(x) - f(x)g'(x)]/[g(x)]^2. These rules handle derivatives of products and quotients when functions cannot be simplified first. Students learn to identify which function is 'first' and 'second' in products, and 'numerator'

topics.name: The substitution rule
topics.description: The substitution rule (u-substitution) evaluates integrals by changing variables: if u = g(x), then ∫f(g(x))g'(x)dx = ∫f(u)du. This reverses the chain rule for differentiation. The technique involves identifying an inner function u whose derivative (or multiple thereof) appears in the integrand, substituting to simplify, integrating with respect to u, then substituting back. For definite integrals

topics.name: Volumes
topics.description: Volumes of solids of revolution are computed by rotating regions about axes and integrating cross-sectional areas. The disk method uses V = ∫[a,b] π[r(x)]²dx when rotating around the x-axis, where r(x) is the radius function. The washer method handles regions between two curves: V = ∫[a,b] π[R(x)² - r(x)²]dx, where R is outer radius and r is inner radius (creating a washer-shaped cross-section). S

topics.name: Volumes by cylindrical shells
topics.description: The shell method computes volumes by integrating cylindrical shells rather than disks. When rotating about the y-axis, V = ∫[a,b] 2πx·f(x)dx, where 2πx is the shell circumference, f(x) is shell height, and dx is shell thickness. This method is advantageous when the disk/washer method requires difficult inverse functions or when rotation is about an axis parallel to the direction of integration. Th

topics.name: Work
topics.description: Work in physics is force times distance: W = Fd when force is constant. For variable force F(x), work is computed as W = ∫[a,b] F(x)dx, integrating force over distance. Applications include stretching springs (Hooke's Law: F = kx), lifting objects against gravity, pumping fluids from tanks, and pulling chains. Work done pumping fluids requires accounting for varying distances different layers must

CHEM 104.name: General Chemistry II
CHEM 104.description: Chemistry 104 is a second semester course in introductory chemistry orientated for students in the biological and engineering sciences. The course covers the following topics: electrochemistry, chemical kinetics, an introduction to organic chemistry and biochemistry, and acid-base chemistry.
objectives: understand thermodynamics (first, second, and third Laws of Thermodynamics).
be able to predict spontaneity for a chemical reaction or system.
be able to determine the amount of work a system could produce/needs.
understand how galvanic cells work.
determine the potential of a galvanic cell.
understand how electrolytic cells can be used for electroplating and electrolysis.
understand and apply Collision Theory to chemical reactions.
be able to use differential and integrated rate laws to study chemical reactions.
determine how long a reaction will take.
determine what the mechanism for a chemical reaction is.
be able to identify basic chemical functional groups.
understand 3D shapes of basic chemical functional groups.
be able to determine the absolute conformation of simple stereoisomers.
predict products for and understand the mechanisms of SN1 and SN2 reactions.
apply the lessons we’ve learned in previous chemistry classes as well as the material we will learn in this class to relevant real-world applications.

topics.name: 2nd law of thermodynamics
topics.description: The Second Law of Thermodynamics states that the entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes: ΔSuniverse = ΔSsystem + ΔSsurroundings > 0 for spontaneous changes. This law introduces entropy (S) as a measure of disorder or number of microstates available to a system. While energy is conserved (First Law), the Second Law determines directionality - processes naturally proceed toward m

topics.name: Acids & bases
topics.description: Acids and bases are fundamental chemical categories defined by proton transfer (Brønsted-Lowry) or electron pair acceptance/donation (Lewis). Brønsted-Lowry acids donate protons (H⁺) while bases accept them; Lewis acids accept electron pairs while bases donate them. Acid-base reactions involve proton transfer creating conjugate acid-base pairs. Acid and base strength depends on the position of equ

topics.name: Acid strength
topics.description: Acid strength refers to an acid's tendency to donate protons, quantified by the acid dissociation constant (Ka). Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) completely dissociate in water with very large Ka values, while weak acids (CH₃COOH, HF) only partially dissociate with small Ka values. Acid strength depends on stability of the conjugate base - more stable conjugate bases result from stronger acids. Fac

topics.name: Addition reactions
topics.description: Addition reactions involve atoms or groups adding across a multiple bond (C=C, C≡C, C=O), converting it to a single bond with increased saturation. Common types include hydrogenation (adding H₂), halogenation (adding X₂), hydrohalogenation (adding HX), and hydration (adding H₂O). Markovnikov's rule predicts regioselectivity - in HX additions to asymmetric alkenes, hydrogen adds to the carbon with

topics.name: Alkanes
topics.description: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons containing only carbon-carbon single bonds and carbon-hydrogen bonds, following the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They represent the simplest class of organic compounds and include linear (straight-chain), branched, and cyclic structures. Alkanes are relatively unreactive, undergoing primarily combustion and substitution reactions. Naming follows IUPAC rules involving

topics.name: Buffers
topics.description: Buffers are solutions that resist pH change upon addition of small amounts of acid or base, consisting of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and conjugate acid) in significant concentrations. Buffers work through Le Chatelier's principle - added acid reacts with the base component, added base reacts with the acid component, minimizing pH change. Buffer capacity depends on concentrati

topics.name: Collision theory
topics.description: Collision theory explains reaction rates by proposing that molecules must collide with sufficient energy (activation energy, Ea) and proper orientation to react. Not all collisions lead to reaction - only those meeting energy and orientation requirements are effective. Reaction rate depends on collision frequency, which increases with concentration and temperature. The Arrhenius equation relates r

topics.name: Electrolytic cells
topics.description: Electrolytic cells use electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous redox reactions (ΔG > 0), performing electrolysis. Unlike galvanic cells, electrolytic cells require external power supply. The positive terminal connects to the anode where oxidation occurs, and the negative terminal connects to the cathode where reduction occurs. Applied voltage must exceed the cell's opposing potential to force th

topics.name: Electroplating
topics.description: Electroplating is an electrolytic process that deposits a thin layer of metal onto a conducting surface using electrical current. The object to be plated serves as the cathode where metal ions in solution are reduced and deposited. A metal anode (often the plating metal) undergoes oxidation to replenish metal ions in solution. Electroplating improves corrosion resistance, appearance, and wear resi

topics.name: Entropy
topics.description: Entropy (S) is a thermodynamic property measuring the disorder, randomness, or number of microstates available to a system. Higher entropy means greater disorder. Entropy increases with temperature, volume, and number of particles. Phase transitions show large entropy changes: gases have much higher entropy than liquids, which have higher entropy than solids. Standard molar entropies (S°) are alwa

topics.name: Equilibrium
topics.description: Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state where forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. At equilibrium, ΔG = 0 and Q = K. The equilibrium constant K relates to standard free energy by ΔG° = -RT ln K. Le Chatelier's principle predicts how equilibrium shifts in response to disturbances. Equilibrium calculations involve ICE tables, e

topics.name: Functional groups
topics.description: Functional groups are specific groups of atoms within molecules that determine chemical reactivity and properties. Common functional groups include alcohols (-OH), aldehydes (-CHO), ketones (C=O), carboxylic acids (-COOH), amines (-NH₂), and others. Molecules with the same functional group undergo similar reactions regardless of the rest of their structure. Recognition of functional groups allows

topics.name: Galvanic cells
topics.description: Galvanic (voltaic) cells are electrochemical devices that spontaneously generate electrical energy from redox reactions. They consist of two half-cells with different electrodes in electrolyte solutions connected by a salt bridge. Oxidation occurs at the anode (negative terminal), releasing electrons that flow through an external circuit to the cathode (positive terminal) where reduction occurs. C

topics.name: Half-life
topics.description: Half-life (t₁/₂) is the time required for reactant concentration to decrease to half its initial value. For first-order reactions, t₁/₂ = 0.693/k is constant and independent of initial concentration, making it particularly useful for dating and pharmacokinetics. For second-order reactions, t₁/₂ = 1/(k[A]₀) depends on initial concentration. Zero-order half-life is t₁/₂ = [A]₀/(2k). Half-life provid

topics.name: Henerson-hasselbalch
topics.description: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation is a mathematical relationship for calculating pH of buffer solutions: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]), where [A⁻] is conjugate base concentration and [HA] is weak acid concentration. This equation, derived from the Ka expression, allows easy calculation of buffer pH and determination of required concentration ratios to achieve desired pH. It's most accurate when the r

topics.name: Hybridization
topics.description: Hybridization describes the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals with specific geometries optimized for bonding. Common hybridizations include sp (linear, 180°), sp² (trigonal planar, 120°), sp³ (tetrahedral, 109.5°), sp³d (trigonal bipyramidal), and sp³d² (octahedral). The type of hybridization determines molecular geometry and bond angles. Sigma bonds form from hybrid orbitals,

topics.name: Mechanisms
topics.description: A reaction mechanism is the step-by-step molecular pathway showing how reactants convert to products, consisting of elementary steps that may involve intermediates. The rate-determining step (slowest step) controls overall reaction rate. Mechanisms must be consistent with the stoichiometric equation (elementary steps sum to overall reaction) and the experimentally determined rate law. Intermediate

topics.name: Molecular orbitals
topics.description: Molecular Orbital (MO) theory describes bonding through delocalized orbitals formed by combining atomic orbitals from multiple atoms. When atomic orbitals combine, they form equal numbers of bonding (lower energy, increase electron density between nuclei) and antibonding (higher energy, decrease electron density between nuclei) molecular orbitals. Electrons fill MOs following aufbau principle, Pau

topics.name: Molecular structures
topics.description: Molecular structure encompasses the three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in molecules, including bond lengths, bond angles, and overall geometry. Structure determination combines Lewis structures, VSEPR theory, and hybridization to predict molecular shapes. Molecular structure profoundly affects physical and chemical properties including polarity, reactivity, boiling point, and biological activi

topics.name: Nernst equation
topics.description: The Nernst equation calculates the cell potential under non-standard conditions: E = E° - (RT/nF) ln Q, where E° is standard cell potential, n is moles of electrons transferred, F is Faraday's constant, and Q is reaction quotient. At 25°C, this simplifies to E = E° - (0.0592V/n) log Q. The equation shows how cell potential depends on concentrations and allows calculation of equilibrium constants f

topics.name: Non-standard free energy
topics.description: Non-standard free energy (ΔG) describes spontaneity under conditions different from standard state, calculated using ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q, where Q is the reaction quotient. This equation shows how free energy depends on actual concentrations/pressures. When Q

topics.name: Ph
topics.description: pH is a logarithmic scale measuring hydrogen ion concentration in aqueous solutions, defined as pH = -log[H⁺]. The scale typically ranges from 0 (most acidic) to 14 (most basic), with 7 being neutral at 25°C. Each pH unit represents a tenfold change in [H⁺] concentration. pH relates to pOH by pH + pOH = 14 at 25°C. Understanding pH is essential for chemistry, biology, medicine, and environmental s

topics.name: Ph of salts
topics.description: The pH of salt solutions depends on the relative strengths of the parent acid and base from which the salt derives. Salts from strong acid-strong base (like NaCl) produce neutral pH 7 solutions. Salts from weak acid-strong base (like sodium acetate) produce basic solutions as the anion acts as a weak base. Salts from strong acid-weak base (like ammonium chloride) produce acidic solutions as the ca

topics.name: Polyprotic acids
topics.description: Polyprotic acids contain multiple ionizable protons that dissociate sequentially, each with its own dissociation constant (Ka1, Ka2, Ka3...). Common examples include H₂SO₄ (diprotic), H₃PO₄ (triprotic), and H₂CO₃ (diprotic). Each successive dissociation is weaker (Ka1 > Ka2 > Ka3) because removing a proton from an increasingly negative species becomes progressively more difficult. Polyprotic acid

topics.name: Polyprotic acid titrations
topics.description: Polyprotic acid titrations involve multiple equivalence points corresponding to successive proton removals, each producing a distinct step in the titration curve. For a diprotic acid like H₂A, there are two equivalence points: first at half the total volume (H₂A → HA⁻) and second at the full volume (HA⁻ → A²⁻). Each equivalence point pH is calculated differently, and buffer regions exist around ea

topics.name: Rate law determination
topics.description: Rate law determination involves experimental methods to find reaction orders and rate constants. The method of initial rates compares how changing initial concentrations affects initial rates - if doubling [A] doubles rate, reaction is first-order in A; if rate quadruples, second-order; if unchanged, zero-order. Graphical methods plot concentration vs time data: zero-order gives linear [A] vs t, f

topics.name: Rate laws
topics.description: A rate law expresses the mathematical relationship between reaction rate and reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, where k is the rate constant, m and n are reaction orders (usually integers determined experimentally). The overall reaction order is m+n. Rate laws must be determined experimentally and cannot be predicted from balanced equations. First-order reactions have rate proportional t

topics.name: Rates
topics.description: Reaction rate measures how quickly reactant concentrations decrease or product concentrations increase over time, typically expressed as change in concentration per unit time (M/s). Rates depend on reactant concentrations, temperature, presence of catalysts, and surface area for heterogeneous reactions. Instantaneous rate at any moment differs from average rate over a time interval. Initial rate (

topics.name: Redox reactions
topics.description: Redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions involve electron transfer between species, where one species is oxidized (loses electrons) and another is reduced (gains electrons). Oxidation states track electron distribution in molecules. Oxidizing agents gain electrons and are reduced; reducing agents lose electrons and are oxidized. Redox reactions can be split into half-reactions (oxidation and reductio

topics.name: Salts
topics.description: Salts are ionic compounds formed from the reaction of acids and bases, consisting of cations (from bases) and anions (from acids). While salts are electrically neutral overall, they can affect solution pH when dissolved because their ions may undergo hydrolysis. The cation from a weak base or anion from a weak acid will react with water, affecting pH. Salts from strong acid-strong base reactions p

topics.name: Spontaneity
topics.description: Spontaneity describes whether a process occurs naturally without external intervention, determined by the sign of free energy change (ΔG). Spontaneous processes have ΔG

topics.name: Standard free energy
topics.description: Standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) is the free energy change for a process occurring under standard conditions (1 atm pressure, 1 M concentrations, 25°C). It determines spontaneity: ΔG°

topics.name: Stereochemistry
topics.description: Stereochemistry studies three-dimensional arrangement of atoms in molecules and how spatial arrangements affect properties and reactivity. Key concepts include chirality (molecules lacking mirror symmetry), enantiomers (non-superimposable mirror images), diastereomers (non-mirror image stereoisomers), and geometric isomers (cis/trans or E/Z). Chiral centers (often tetrahedral carbons with four dif

topics.name: Substitution reaction mechanisms
topics.description: Substitution reactions involve one atom or group replacing another in a molecule, with two main mechanisms: SN1 (unimolecular nucleophilic substitution) and SN2 (bimolecular nucleophilic substitution). SN1 proceeds through a carbocation intermediate in two steps with first-order kinetics and racemization at chiral centers. SN2 occurs in one concerted step with second-order kinetics and inversion o

topics.name: Titrations
topics.description: Titration is a quantitative analytical technique where a solution of known concentration (titrant) is added to a solution of unknown concentration until the reaction reaches stoichiometric completion (equivalence point). The volume of titrant required allows calculation of the unknown concentration. Strong acid-strong base titrations have equivalence points at pH 7, while weak acid-strong base tit

topics.name: Weak acids
topics.description: Weak acids are acids that only partially dissociate in aqueous solution, establishing an equilibrium between molecular and ionic forms. The equilibrium is described by the acid dissociation constant (Ka), where smaller Ka values indicate weaker acids. Weak acid calculations involve ICE tables (Initial, Change, Equilibrium) and often require the quadratic formula or simplifying assumptions when Ka

topics.name: Weak acids titrations
topics.description: Weak acid titrations involve adding strong base to a weak acid solution, producing a distinctive titration curve with four regions: initial pH (weak acid), buffer region (half-equivalence point where pH = pKa), equivalence point (pH > 7 due to conjugate base), and excess base region. The buffer region shows resistance to pH change, and the equivalence point pH is calculated using Kb of the conjuga

CS 101.name: Intro to Computing: Engrg & Sci
CS 101.description: Fundamental principles, concepts, and methods of computing, with emphasis on applications in the physical sciences and engineering. Basic problem solving and programming techniques; fundamental algorithms and data structures; use of computers in solving engineering and scientific problems. Intended for engineering and science majors.
objectives: Goal 1 Students should be able to solve problems algorithmically.

Goal 2 Students should be proficient in 'computational thinking', meaning that students should be able to conduct major- appropriate abstraction, modeling, representations of information, and algorithmic thinking related to solving engineering problems.
Goal 3 Students should be confident about using computation as a standard tool, on the same level as math and physics.
Goal 4 Students should be able to program in a high-level language (e.g., Python or MATLAB).
Goal 5 Students should access data sources, process data, and create outputs including various, plots.
Goal 6 Students should use MATLAB proficiently.

topics.name: Curve fitting & regression
