topics.description: Curve fitting determines function parameters best matching data by minimizing error between model predictions and observations. Linear regression fits lines; polynomial and nonlinear regression fit more complex functions. Least squares is common criterion. Understanding regression enables extracting relationships from data, validating theoretical models, predicting outcomes, and quantifying uncert

topics.name: Data analytics
topics.description: Data analytics extracts insights from datasets through statistical analysis, visualization, and machine learning. Workflows include data loading, cleaning, exploration, transformation, analysis, and visualization. Pandas enables data manipulation; scikit-learn provides machine learning tools. Understanding data analytics enables processing experimental results, identifying patterns, testing hypoth

topics.name: File i/o
topics.description: File input/output (I/O) reads data from and writes data to files, enabling programs to process large datasets, save results, and exchange data with other programs. Operations include opening files, reading/writing (text, CSV, binary), closing files, and error handling. Understanding file I/O enables analyzing experimental data, saving simulation results, and automating data processing pipelines es

topics.name: Functions and scripts
topics.description: Functions are reusable code blocks performing specific tasks, accepting inputs (parameters) and returning outputs. Scripts are files containing sequences of commands executed together. Functions promote modularity, reusability, and organization. Understanding function definition, calling, scope, and script structure enables writing organized, maintainable code for scientific computing. Functions a

topics.name: Libraries
topics.description: Libraries are collections of pre-written functions and tools that extend programming language capabilities without writing code from scratch. Common scientific libraries include NumPy (numerical computing), Pandas (data analysis), Matplotlib (plotting), and SciPy (scientific computing). Understanding how to import, use, and explore library functions enables leveraging existing solutions for comple

topics.name: Numerical algebra & calculus
topics.description: Numerical methods approximate mathematical operations that lack analytical solutions or are computationally expensive symbolically. Techniques include numerical integration (trapezoidal, Simpson's), differentiation (finite differences), root finding (Newton's method), and solving differential equations (Euler, Runge-Kutta). Understanding numerical methods enables solving real-world problems where

topics.name: Numerical optimization
topics.description: Numerical optimization finds parameter values minimizing or maximizing objective functions using algorithms like gradient descent, Newton's method, or genetic algorithms. Applications include parameter fitting, machine learning training, and engineering design. Constraints can limit feasible solutions. Understanding optimization enables finding best-fit parameters for models, optimizing experiment

topics.name: Plotting
topics.description: Plotting creates visual representations of data and results using libraries like Matplotlib. Plot types include line plots, scatter plots, histograms, bar charts, and 3D visualizations. Customization includes labels, legends, colors, styles, and subplots. Understanding plotting enables communicating results effectively, exploring data patterns, validating models, and creating publication-quality f

topics.name: Statements
topics.description: Statements are complete instructions telling computer to perform actions: assignment (x = 5), conditionals (if-else), loops (for, while), function calls, and input/output. Control flow statements direct program execution order. Understanding statement syntax, control structures, and program flow enables writing programs that make decisions, repeat tasks, and solve computational problems. Statement

topics.name: Symbolic algebra & calculus
topics.description: Symbolic computation manipulates mathematical expressions algebraically without numerical evaluation, using libraries like SymPy. Capabilities include equation solving, differentiation, integration, series expansion, and simplification with exact results. Unlike numerical methods giving approximate answers, symbolic methods provide exact mathematical expressions. Understanding symbolic computing e

topics.name: Variables
topics.description: Variables are named storage locations holding data values that can change during program execution. Variable types include integers, floats, strings, and booleans. Understanding variable declaration, assignment, naming conventions, and type conversions enables storing and manipulating data in programs. Variables are fundamental to all programming, allowing calculations, storing results, and repres

MATH 231.name: Calculus II
MATH 231.description: Second course in calculus and analytic geometry: techniques of integration, conic sections, polar coordinates, and infinite series.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus.

topics.name: Absolute convergence and the ratio and roots tests
topics.description: A series Σa_n converges absolutely if Σ|a_n| converges; absolute convergence implies convergence but not vice versa. The Ratio Test uses lim(n→∞) |a_(n+1)/a_n| = L: if L

topics.name: Alternating series
topics.description: Alternating series have terms that alternate in sign: Σ(-1)^n·a_n or Σ(-1)^(n+1)·a_n where a_n > 0. The Alternating Series Test (Leibniz Test) states that if a_n is decreasing and lim(n→∞) a_n = 0, the series converges. The alternating nature causes partial cancellation, often improving convergence compared to the corresponding positive series. The error in approximating the sum with n terms is bo

topics.name: Applications of taylor polynomials
topics.description: Taylor polynomials T_n(x) are partial sums of Taylor series, providing polynomial approximations to functions near a point. The nth Taylor polynomial includes terms up to (x-a)^n. Taylor's Remainder Theorem bounds the error: |R_n(x)| ≤ M·|x-a|^(n+1)/(n+1)! where M bounds the (n+1)st derivative. Applications include approximating function values, evaluating limits using series expansions, estimatin

topics.name: Applications to physics and engineering
topics.description: Calculus II techniques apply to numerous physics and engineering problems including calculating work (W = ∫F dx), fluid pressure and force on submerged surfaces, center of mass, moments of inertia, and electric/magnetic field calculations. These applications typically involve setting up integrals that model physical quantities as accumulations of infinitesimal contributions. Students learn to tran

topics.name: Arc length
topics.description: Arc length calculates the distance along a curve from one point to another using the formula L = ∫[a,b] √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx for y = f(x), or L = ∫[a,b] √([dx/dt]² + [dy/dt]²)dt for parametric curves. The formula derives from approximating the curve with many tiny straight line segments and taking the limit as segment size approaches zero. Arc length integrals often require numerical methods or compu

topics.name: Area of a surface of revolution
topics.description: Surface area of revolution calculates the area of a surface formed by rotating a curve around an axis. For rotation about the x-axis: S = ∫[a,b] 2πy√(1 + [dy/dx]²)dx, where 2πy represents the circumference at each point and √(1 + [dy/dx]²)dx is the arc length element. Similar formulas exist for rotation about the y-axis or parametric curves. This integral sums the lateral surface areas of infinite

topics.name: Areas and lengths in polar coordinates
topics.description: Area in polar coordinates for region bounded by r = f(θ) from θ = α to θ = β is A = (1/2)∫[α,β] [f(θ)]²dθ, derived by summing areas of circular sectors. Arc length of polar curve r = f(θ) is L = ∫[α,β] √[r² + (dr/dθ)²]dθ, using the parametric arc length formula with x = r·cos(θ), y = r·sin(θ). These formulas account for the polar coordinate geometry where area elements are sectors and distance ele

topics.name: Calculus with parametric curves
topics.description: Calculus with parametric curves adapts derivative and integral techniques to parametric forms. The slope is dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) where dx/dt ≠ 0, found by differentiating both parametric equations and dividing. Arc length is L = ∫√[(dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²]dt. Surface area of revolution uses similar parametric formulas. Second derivatives d²y/dx² require the chain rule applied to dy/dx. These extens

topics.name: Curves defined by parametric equations
topics.description: Parametric equations define curves using a parameter t: x = f(t), y = g(t), where t varies over an interval. Instead of expressing y directly as a function of x, both coordinates depend on the parameter, allowing representation of curves that fail the vertical line test and enabling description of motion with time as parameter. Parametric curves can loop, self-intersect, and trace paths multiple t

topics.name: Improper integrals
topics.description: Improper integrals extend definite integration to unbounded intervals or unbounded integrands. Type 1 improper integrals have infinite limits: ∫[a,∞) f(x)dx = lim(t→∞) ∫[a,t] f(x)dx. Type 2 have infinite discontinuities in the integration interval. The integral converges if the limit exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges. Comparison tests help determine convergence without explicit evaluatio

topics.name: Integration by parts
topics.description: Integration by parts is a technique for evaluating integrals of products of functions, based on the product rule for differentiation: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du. The method involves strategically choosing which part of the integrand to differentiate (u) and which to integrate (dv), often guided by the LIATE rule (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential). Sometimes multiple applica

topics.name: Integration of rational functions by partial fractions
topics.description: Partial fraction decomposition breaks rational functions (ratios of polynomials) into simpler fractions that can be integrated individually. The method applies when the numerator degree is less than denominator degree; otherwise, polynomial division is performed first. The denominator is factored into linear and irreducible quadratic factors, each contributing terms to the decomposition. Coefficie

topics.name: Polar coordinates
topics.description: Polar coordinates (r, θ) specify points using distance r from origin and angle θ from positive x-axis, related to Cartesian coordinates by x = r·cos(θ) and y = r·sin(θ). Curves in polar form r = f(θ) include circles, spirals, rose curves, and cardioids. Polar coordinates naturally describe curves with radial symmetry and rotational features. Converting between Cartesian and polar forms uses the re

topics.name: Power series
topics.description: A power series is an infinite series of the form Σc_n(x-a)^n where c_n are coefficients and a is the center. Power series behave like polynomials of infinite degree and converge for x values within the radius of convergence R, diverge outside, and require individual testing at endpoints. The interval of convergence is (a-R, a+R) plus possibly the endpoints. Power series can be differentiated and i

topics.name: Representation of functions by power series
topics.description: Functions can be represented as power series by manipulating known series (like geometric series 1/(1-x) = Σx^n for |x|

topics.name: Sequences
topics.description: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers {a_n} where n takes integer values, often defined by a formula like a_n = f(n) or recursively. Sequences can converge (approach a finite limit as n→∞) or diverge (grow without bound or oscillate). Key concepts include monotonic sequences (always increasing or decreasing), bounded sequences (constrained above and/or below), and the Monotone Convergence Theor

topics.name: Series
topics.description: A series is the sum of terms in a sequence: Σa_n = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + ..., where the infinite sum is defined as the limit of partial sums S_n = Σ(k=1 to n) a_k. A series converges if lim(n→∞) S_n exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges. Important series include geometric series (Σar^n converges if |r|

topics.name: Strategy for integration
topics.description: Integration strategy involves systematically approaching integrals by recognizing patterns and selecting appropriate techniques. Key decision points include: checking for basic forms, attempting u-substitution, considering algebraic simplification, identifying products requiring integration by parts, recognizing trigonometric forms, applying trigonometric substitution for radicals, using partial f

topics.name: Strategy for testing series
topics.description: Series testing strategy involves systematically determining convergence or divergence by selecting appropriate tests. General approach: check if lim(n→∞) a_n = 0 (if not, series diverges by divergence test); identify series type (geometric, p-series, alternating); for positive terms, try comparison, limit comparison, ratio, or root tests; use integral test if terms match a nice function; for alter

topics.name: Taylor and maclaurin series
topics.description: Taylor series represents a function as a power series centered at x = a: f(x) = Σ[f^(n)(a)/n!]·(x-a)^n, where f^(n)(a) is the nth derivative at a. Maclaurin series is the special case when a = 0. If the series converges to f(x) near a, the function is analytic at a. Important Maclaurin series include e^x = Σx^n/n!, sin(x) = Σ(-1)^n·x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!, and cos(x) = Σ(-1)^n·x^(2n)/(2n)!. Taylor series

topics.name: The comparison tests
topics.description: Comparison tests determine series convergence by comparing with series of known behavior. The Direct Comparison Test states: if 0 ≤ a_n ≤ b_n and Σb_n converges, then Σa_n converges; if 0 ≤ b_n ≤ a_n and Σb_n diverges, then Σa_n diverges. The Limit Comparison Test compares lim(n→∞) (a_n/b_n): if the limit is positive and finite, both series behave the same. These tests are particularly useful when

topics.name: The integral test and estimates of sums
topics.description: The integral test determines series convergence by comparing the series Σa_n with the integral ∫f(x)dx where f(n) = a_n and f is positive, continuous, and decreasing. If the improper integral converges, so does the series; if the integral diverges, so does the series. The remainder (difference between series sum and partial sum) can be estimated using the integral: ∫[n+1,∞) f(x)dx ≤ R_n ≤ ∫[n,∞) f

topics.name: Trigonometric integrals
topics.description: Trigonometric integrals involve products and powers of trigonometric functions like ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx. Strategies depend on the powers: if either power is odd, use substitution with the other function; if both are even, use power-reducing identities (sin²x = (1-cos2x)/2). For products like ∫sin(mx)cos(nx)dx, use product-to-sum formulas. Integrals of tan, sec, and their powers require specific te

topics.name: Trigonometric substitution
topics.description: Trigonometric substitution handles integrals containing expressions like √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²) by substituting x with trigonometric functions to eliminate radicals. For √(a²-x²), use x = a·sin(θ); for √(a²+x²), use x = a·tan(θ); for √(x²-a²), use x = a·sec(θ). The substitution converts the integral into a trigonometric integral, which is then solved and converted back to x. This techniqu

ME 170.name: Computer Aided Design
ME 170.description: Geometry and topology of engineered components: creation of engineering models and their presentation in standard 2d blueprint form and as 3D wire-frame and shaded solids; meshed topologies for engineering analysis and tool-path generation for component manufacture; ISO and ANSI standards for coordinate dimensioning and tolerancing, with modeling software for creating associative models at the component and assembly levels with automatic blueprint creation, interference checking, and linked bill of materials.
objectives: Be able to create fully defined designs of engineered components to ANSI and ISO standards.
Be able to present component design and form specifications in standard 2D blueprint form (orthographic views), 3D wireframe and shaded solid models.
Be able to create assembly models, exploded views, linked Bill of Materials (BOM), full view and sectioned orthographic assembly drawings.

Be able to develop a functional mechanical design with appreciation of limits and fits required for interchangeable assembly, functionality of fits (bearing fits, press and push fits, etc.), and economics of manufacture.
Be able to develop a product design specification for a new product concept addressing broad issues of societal impact, safety, environmental, economic and ethical considerations.
Be able to professionally present design concepts in written and oral form.

topics.name: 2d cad
topics.description: 2D CAD creates flat, two-dimensional drawings using lines, circles, arcs, and other geometric primitives with precise dimensions. This approach mimics traditional drafting but with digital precision, layer management, easy modification, and automatic dimensioning. 2D CAD is used for floor plans, circuit diagrams, schematic layouts, and creating manufacturing drawings from 3D models. Commands inclu

topics.name: 3d solids and surfaces
topics.description: 3D solid modeling creates complete volumetric representations with defined interior and exterior, enabling mass property calculations, interference detection, and unambiguous visualization. Surface modeling defines only the bounding skin using patches (NURBS, Bezier, etc.), suitable for complex curved shapes. Solid modelers use Constructive Solid Geometry (CSG) combining primitives or Boundary Rep

topics.name: 3d wireframe
topics.description: 3D wireframe modeling represents objects as collections of points, lines, and curves in three-dimensional space, showing only edges without surfaces or volumes. This approach provides spatial visualization while being computationally simple and allowing views from any angle. However, wireframe models are ambiguous (unclear which side is 'inside'), lack mass properties, and cannot show hidden line

topics.name: Adding ribs and bosses
topics.description: Ribs are thin-walled stiffening features connecting surfaces, increasing strength and rigidity while minimizing weight. Bosses are raised cylindrical features providing mounting points for fasteners or other components. Both are essential for injection molded parts where thick sections cause defects. Ribs typically run perpendicular to stress directions with thickness 50-60% of adjacent walls and

topics.name: Basic part modeling
topics.description: Basic part modeling involves creating simple individual components using fundamental CAD operations: sketching 2D profiles, adding dimensions and constraints, then using operations like extrude, revolve, or sweep to create 3D geometry. The process emphasizes design intent through parametric relationships, feature-based modeling, and proper construction sequence. Beginning with simple geometric sha

topics.name: Complex parts and surfaces
topics.description: Complex parts feature intricate geometries including curved surfaces, blended transitions, variable cross-sections, and organic shapes beyond simple prismatic forms. Creating complex surfaces requires advanced techniques: lofting between multiple profiles, sweeping along paths, boundary surfaces from edge curves, and surface editing tools. These parts challenge manufacturability, often requiring m

topics.name: Coordinate dimensioning and tolerancing principles and standards
topics.description: Coordinate dimensioning specifies locations using X, Y, Z distances from reference datums, providing absolute position information. Tolerancing defines acceptable variation from nominal dimensions, expressed as bilateral (±), unilateral (+/-0), or limit dimensions. Standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101) govern notation, symbol usage, and interpretation. Understanding statistical tolerance accumulation,

topics.name: Creating parametric designs
topics.description: Parametric design creates models controlled by parameters (dimensions, equations, relationships) that automatically update geometry when parameter values change. This approach captures design intent through dimensional constraints, geometric relationships, and mathematical formulas. Parametric models enable rapid design iteration, family of parts creation (different sizes from one model), and opti

topics.name: Curved surfaces and blends
topics.description: Curved surfaces in CAD represent complex free-form geometry using mathematical descriptions (NURBS, Bezier splines, etc.) enabling smooth, continuous shapes. Blends create smooth transitions between surfaces or edges, including fillets (constant or variable radius), chamfers (angled transitions), and complex surface blends. Blend quality depends on continuity: G0 (touching), G1 (tangent continuous

topics.name: Defining the coordinate systems
topics.description: Defining coordinate systems establishes the mathematical framework for locating points, specifying directions, and orienting parts in CAD models. The global coordinate system (World Coordinate System) provides absolute reference, while local coordinate systems (part or feature-based) simplify modeling specific geometries. Coordinate system definition includes origin location, axis orientation (fol

topics.name: Detailing and blueprint creation
topics.description: Detailing involves creating technical drawings (blueprints) from 3D CAD models, providing manufacturing-ready documentation with orthographic views, dimensions, tolerances, notes, and symbols. The process includes selecting appropriate views, adding dimensions following standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO), specifying tolerances, noting materials and finishes, and creating title blocks. Modern CAD automate

topics.name: Dimension driven 3d sketching
topics.description: Dimension-driven 3D sketching creates parametric 2D profiles that define feature geometry through dimensions and constraints rather than fixed coordinates. Sketches are under-constrained (flexible), fully-constrained (defined), or over-constrained (conflicting) based on dimension sufficiency. Dimensions can be driving (controlling geometry) or driven (measuring existing geometry). This approach en

topics.name: Equations
topics.description: Equations in CAD define mathematical relationships between parameters, creating intelligent geometry that maintains design intent during modifications. Equations use variables, arithmetic operators, and functions (sin, cos, if-then, etc.) to express dependencies like 'hole_spacing = part_length / 4' or 'thickness = max(2mm, load/1000)'. This enables complex parametric behavior: adapting geometry t

topics.name: Extruding
topics.description: Extruding creates 3D geometry by projecting a 2D sketch profile perpendicular to the sketch plane for a specified distance or to a termination surface. The operation can add material (boss), remove material (cut), or create thin-walled features. Extrusion options include blind (fixed depth), through all, up to surface, and symmetric. This is the most common CAD operation, creating prismatic featur

topics.name: Feature selection
topics.description: Feature selection involves choosing appropriate modeling operations (extrude, revolve, sweep, loft, holes, fillets, chamfers, etc.) to create desired geometry efficiently while capturing design intent. Good feature selection considers manufacturability, model flexibility, computational efficiency, and ease of modification. Features should represent meaningful design elements (a bolt hole, not just

topics.name: Forms and tables
topics.description: Forms and tables organize parametric data, enabling efficient configuration of part families and design variants. Design tables use spreadsheet-like interfaces where rows represent configurations and columns represent parameters, allowing rapid generation of multiple part sizes from one parametric model. Forms provide user-friendly interfaces for entering design parameters, often with validation,

topics.name: Geometric dimensioning and tolerancing
topics.description: Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing (GD&T) uses symbols and standards (ASME Y14.5, ISO 1101) to specify geometric characteristics and tolerances beyond simple dimensional limits. GD&T controls form (straightness, flatness), orientation (parallelism, perpendicularity), location (position, concentricity), profile, and runout. Tolerances are specified in feature control frames relative to datum re

topics.name: Hidden lines
topics.description: Hidden line removal displays 3D objects with obscured edges shown as dashed or removed entirely, clarifying which surfaces are visible from the current viewpoint. This technique eliminates ambiguity inherent in wireframe views by indicating depth relationships. Hidden line views are essential for technical drawings, allowing readers to understand 3D form from 2D projections. The algorithm determin

topics.name: Introduction to cad
topics.description: Computer-Aided Design (CAD) uses software to create, modify, analyze, and optimize designs digitally, replacing traditional drafting with precise digital models. CAD systems enable designers to visualize concepts in 2D or 3D, perform simulations, generate manufacturing instructions, and collaborate efficiently. The technology revolutionized engineering by improving accuracy, enabling rapid iterati

topics.name: Line and text forms
topics.description: Line and text forms in technical drawings follow standards for clarity and universal interpretation. Line types include visible edges (continuous thick), hidden edges (dashed), centerlines (chain thin), dimension lines (continuous thin), and extension lines. Text formatting covers dimensioning numbers, notes, specifications, and labels using standard fonts, sizes, and placement conventions. Proper

topics.name: Orthographic projections
topics.description: Orthographic projection creates 2D views by projecting 3D objects onto planes perpendicular to viewing directions, maintaining true dimensions without perspective distortion. Standard views include front, top, right side, following either first-angle (European) or third-angle (American) projection conventions. Unlike perspective views, parallel lines remain parallel and dimensions are measurable,

topics.name: Parent/child relationships
topics.description: Parent/child relationships in parametric CAD describe feature dependencies where child features reference parent geometry, dimensions, or parameters. For example, a hole (child) depends on the face (parent) it's placed on. Changes to parents propagate to children, enabling design exploration but also causing failures if dependencies break. Understanding these relationships is crucial for creating

topics.name: Perspective view
topics.description: Perspective view projects 3D models with size decreasing with distance from viewpoint, mimicking human vision and photography. Unlike orthographic projection maintaining constant scale, perspective creates converging parallel lines (like railroad tracks appearing to meet at horizon). Perspective views provide intuitive depth perception, useful for design review and presentations but inappropriate

topics.name: Protrusions
topics.description: Protrusions are features that add material to a part, creating raised sections extending from base surfaces. Common protrusion types include extruded bosses (raised cylindrical sections for mounting), ribs (thin walls for reinforcement), and custom shaped projections. Creating protrusions involves sketching the profile on a reference plane, specifying depth or termination condition, and defining d

topics.name: Revolving
topics.description: Revolving (revolution) creates 3D geometry by rotating a 2D profile about an axis, generating rotationally symmetric parts. The operation requires a sketch profile and axis of revolution, with options for full 360° rotation or partial angles. Revolution efficiently creates cylindrical parts, spheres, cones, bottles, and any axisymmetric geometry. This operation is fundamental for parts like shafts

topics.name: Section and part-section views
topics.description: Section views cut through objects along a defined plane, removing material on one side to reveal interior features otherwise hidden in standard orthographic views. Full sections cut completely through, half sections cut halfway (useful for symmetric parts), and partial sections remove only necessary portions. Hatching (cross-sectional lines) indicates cut surfaces, following material-specific patt

topics.name: Setting up datum planes
topics.description: Datum planes are reference planes establishing coordinate systems for part modeling, serving as construction surfaces for sketches and features. Setting up datums involves defining three mutually perpendicular planes (typically Front, Top, Right) analogous to XY, YZ, and XZ planes. Additional datum planes can be created offset from, angled to, or through specific geometry. Proper datum selection i

topics.name: Shaded view
topics.description: Shaded view renders 3D models with surfaces colored/shaded based on lighting, material properties, and viewing angle, providing realistic appearance that clarifies form and depth. Simple shading uses flat or smooth surface coloring, while advanced rendering includes reflections, shadows, and textures. Shaded views enable intuitive understanding of complex geometry, support design review without ma

topics.name: Shelled/molded parts
topics.description: Shelled parts are created by hollowing solid models, leaving thin walls of specified thickness - essential for injection molded plastics, castings, and lightweight structures. The shell operation removes one or more faces and offsets remaining faces inward/outward by the wall thickness. Molded parts require additional considerations: draft angles (taper for mold release), uniform wall thickness (a

topics.name: Variables
topics.description: Variables in parametric CAD are named parameters storing values (dimensions, counts, options) that can be referenced throughout a model, enabling centralized control of design parameters. Variables can be dimensions (length = 50mm), mathematical expressions (width = 2*length), or conditional (if height > 100, use_reinforcement = yes). Using variables rather than hard-coded numbers makes design int

topics.name: Visualization
topics.description: Visualization in CAD encompasses techniques for viewing and representing 3D models to communicate design intent, including wireframe, hidden line, shaded, rendered, and sectioned views. Effective visualization enables design evaluation, error detection, and communication with stakeholders. Techniques include view orientation (isometric, orthographic), display modes (solid, transparent, x-ray), lig

PHYS 211.name: University Physics, Mechanics
PHYS 211.description: Newton's Laws, work and energy, static properties and dynamics, fluids, oscillations, transverse waves, systems of particles, and rotations. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of mechanics.
apply mechanics concepts to solve a wide array of relevant problems.
interactive lectures to present, discuss, and demonstrate the principles.
laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles.
online homework to engage students in problem solving.
interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem-solving skills.

topics.name: 1d and 2d kinematics
topics.description: Kinematics describes motion without considering the forces causing it, focusing on position, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time. In one dimension, motion occurs along a line with scalar quantities. Two-dimensional kinematics extends this to planar motion using vector components, commonly applied to projectile motion. Understanding displacement, average and instantaneous velocities, an

topics.name: Angular momentum
topics.description: Angular momentum is rotational momentum, defined as the cross product of position vector and linear momentum (L = r × p) for a point mass, or as moment of inertia times angular velocity (L = Iω) for rigid bodies. When net external torque is zero, angular momentum is conserved. This conservation law is fundamental to understanding planetary orbits, figure skating spins, gyroscopes, and quantum mech

topics.name: Angular momentum vector
topics.description: Angular momentum as a vector quantity has both magnitude and direction, with direction determined by the right-hand rule perpendicular to the plane of rotation. Vector analysis of angular momentum enables understanding of gyroscopic effects, precession, and three-dimensional rotation. The vector nature explains why spinning objects resist changes in orientation and is crucial for analyzing tops, g

topics.name: Center of mass
topics.description: The center of mass is the average position of mass in a system, the point where the system's total mass can be considered concentrated for translational motion analysis. For a system of particles, it's the mass-weighted average of positions. The center of mass moves as if all external forces act on the total mass located there, simplifying analysis of complex systems like collisions, explosions, a

topics.name: Conservation of momentum
topics.description: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity (p = mv), representing an object's quantity of motion. The law of conservation of momentum states that total momentum of an isolated system remains constant when no external forces act. This principle is fundamental to analyzing collisions, explosions, and interactions between objects. Unlike energy, momentum is always conserved in isolated systems rega

topics.name: Conservative forces
topics.description: Conservative forces are forces where the work done depends only on initial and final positions, not the path taken. Examples include gravity and spring forces. For conservative forces, work done around any closed path is zero, and a potential energy function can be defined. The mechanical energy (kinetic plus potential) remains constant in systems with only conservative forces, enabling powerful e

topics.name: Elastic collisions
topics.description: Elastic collisions are interactions where both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. Objects bounce apart with no energy lost to heat, sound, or deformation. These ideal collisions occur approximately in atomic-scale interactions and bouncing balls with minimal energy loss. Analyzing elastic collisions requires simultaneous application of momentum and energy conservation, yielding unique solu

topics.name: Fluid dynamics
topics.description: Fluid dynamics studies fluids in motion, using conservation laws for mass, momentum, and energy. The continuity equation expresses mass conservation, relating fluid speed to cross-sectional area. Bernoulli's equation relates pressure, velocity, and height along streamlines for ideal fluids. Understanding laminar versus turbulent flow, viscosity effects, and applications like airplane lift and bloo

topics.name: Fluid statics
topics.description: Fluid statics studies fluids at rest, focusing on pressure variation with depth, buoyancy, and forces on submerged surfaces. Pascal's principle states pressure applied to a confined fluid transmits equally throughout. Archimedes' principle states buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid. Understanding hydrostatic pressure (P = ρgh), pressure measurement, and floating/sinking conditions i

topics.name: Forces & fbd
topics.description: Forces are pushes or pulls that cause acceleration, measured in Newtons. Free body diagrams (FBD) are visual tools isolating an object and showing all forces acting on it as vectors. Common forces include gravity (weight), normal force, tension, friction, and applied forces. Drawing accurate FBDs is essential for applying Newton's second law to determine net force and resulting acceleration in mec

topics.name: Friction
topics.description: Friction is a contact force opposing relative motion or attempted motion between surfaces. Static friction prevents motion up to a maximum value proportional to the normal force, while kinetic friction opposes sliding with magnitude also proportional to normal force but typically smaller. Friction coefficients (μ_s and μ_k) characterize surface interactions. Understanding friction is crucial for a

topics.name: Harmonic waves
topics.description: Harmonic waves are sinusoidal disturbances propagating through a medium, characterized by wavelength, frequency, amplitude, and wave speed. They satisfy the wave equation and transport energy without net material transport. Understanding wave parameters, the relationship v = fλ, and mathematical representation using sine or cosine functions is fundamental to acoustics, optics, and electromagnetic

topics.name: Impulse & reference frames
topics.description: Impulse is the change in momentum, equal to the integral of force over time. It provides an alternative to Newton's second law useful when forces vary with time or act over short intervals. Reference frames are coordinate systems from which motion is observed; inertial frames move at constant velocity while non-inertial frames accelerate. Understanding how physical laws appear in different frames

topics.name: Newton's laws
topics.description: Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics. The first law (inertia) states objects maintain constant velocity unless acted upon by net force. The second law quantifies force as mass times acceleration (F=ma), relating force to motion. The third law states that forces occur in equal and opposite action-reaction pairs. These laws enable prediction of motion from forces and are fu

topics.name: Parallel axis theorem & torque
topics.description: The parallel axis theorem relates a body's moment of inertia about any axis to its moment of inertia about a parallel axis through the center of mass. Torque is rotational force, the cross product of position vector and force, measuring the tendency to cause rotation. Torque's magnitude depends on force magnitude, distance from rotation axis, and angle between them. These concepts are fundamental

topics.name: Relative & circular motion
topics.description: Relative motion examines how velocity and position depend on the observer's reference frame, showing that motion is observer-dependent. Circular motion involves objects moving along circular paths, requiring centripetal acceleration directed toward the center even at constant speed. Understanding relative velocity addition, reference frames, angular velocity, period, frequency, and centripetal acc

topics.name: Rotational dynamics
topics.description: Rotational dynamics applies Newton's second law to rotation: net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration (τ = Iα). This relates how applied torques cause angular acceleration, analogous to how forces cause linear acceleration. Moment of inertia (I) is rotational mass, depending on mass distribution relative to rotation axis. Understanding rotational dynamics enables analysis of

topics.name: Rotational kinematics
topics.description: Rotational kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position, angular velocity, and angular acceleration, analogous to linear kinematics. Angular displacement (θ), angular velocity (ω), and angular acceleration (α) relate through calculus just as their linear counterparts. Constant angular acceleration equations parallel linear kinematic equations. Understanding the connection between

topics.name: Simple harmonic motion
topics.description: Simple harmonic motion (SHM) is oscillatory motion where restoring force is proportional to displacement from equilibrium (F = -kx), resulting in sinusoidal motion. Examples include mass-spring systems and small-angle pendulums. SHM is characterized by period, frequency, amplitude, and phase, with energy continuously converting between kinetic and potential. Understanding SHM is fundamental to wav

topics.name: Simple & physical pendula
topics.description: A simple pendulum consists of a point mass on a massless string, exhibiting simple harmonic motion for small angles with period depending on length and gravity. A physical pendulum is any extended rigid body pivoting about a point, with period depending on moment of inertia and distance from pivot to center of mass. Both demonstrate oscillatory motion and provide practical examples of rotational d

topics.name: Statics 1 & statics 2
topics.description: Statics analyzes systems in mechanical equilibrium where net force and net torque are both zero, resulting in no translational or rotational acceleration. Problems involve determining forces, tensions, and reaction forces in structures like bridges, ladders, and cranes. Success requires drawing complete free body diagrams, choosing convenient rotation axes for torque calculations, and systematical

topics.name: Superposition
topics.description: The superposition principle states that when multiple waves overlap, the resulting displacement is the algebraic sum of individual wave displacements. This linear principle enables understanding of interference (constructive and destructive), standing waves, and beats. Superposition is fundamental to wave physics, explaining phenomena from noise-canceling headphones to quantum mechanics, and enabl

topics.name: Vectors
topics.description: Vectors are mathematical objects with both magnitude and direction, essential for representing physical quantities like displacement, velocity, force, and acceleration. Vector operations include addition, subtraction, scalar multiplication, dot product, and cross product. Understanding component decomposition, unit vectors, and vector algebra is crucial for analyzing motion and forces in two and t

topics.name: Work & kinetic energy
topics.description: Work is energy transferred by a force acting over a distance, calculated as the dot product of force and displacement vectors. Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, equal to (1/2)mv². The work-energy theorem states that net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy, providing a powerful alternative to Newton's laws for analyzing motion. This connects force, displacement, and spe

topics.name: Work & potential energy
topics.description: Potential energy is stored energy due to an object's position in a conservative force field, such as gravitational or elastic potential energy. Work done by conservative forces equals the negative change in potential energy. The total mechanical energy (kinetic plus potential) is conserved when only conservative forces act. This principle simplifies many mechanics problems by avoiding detailed for

MATH 241.name: Calculus III
MATH 241.description: Third course in calculus and analytic geometry including vector analysis: Euclidean space, partial differentiation, multiple integrals, line integrals and surface integrals, the integral theorems of vector calculus.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of calculus to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using calculus.

topics.name: 2d divergence theorem
topics.description: The 2D divergence theorem (also called Green's theorem in flux form) relates the flux of a vector field across a closed curve to the double integral of the field's divergence over the enclosed region. It states that the total outward flow through a boundary equals the sum of all sources and sinks within. This provides a powerful tool for converting boundary integrals to area integrals in planar re

topics.name: Application of double integral
topics.description: Double integral applications include: computing areas (∫∫_R 1 dA), volumes between surfaces (∫∫_R [f(x,y) - g(x,y)] dA), masses of laminas (∫∫_R δ(x,y) dA with density δ), centers of mass (using moments M_x = ∫∫ y δ dA), moments of inertia, surface areas (with additional ds factor), probabilities (joint probability density functions), and average values of functions over regions. These application

topics.name: Arclength
topics.description: Arc length of a space curve r(t) from t = a to t = b is L = ∫[a,b] ||r'(t)|| dt = ∫[a,b] √[(x'(t))² + (y'(t))² + (z'(t))²] dt. This generalizes 2D arc length to three dimensions, measuring distance traveled along the curve. Arc length parameterization uses s = ∫[a,t] ||r'(u)|| du to reparameterize by distance traveled, giving unit-speed curves where ||r'(s)|| = 1. Arc length is fundamental for com

topics.name: Chanin rule
topics.description: The chain rule (likely 'Chain rule') for multivariable functions handles composition of functions. If z = f(x,y) where x = g(t) and y = h(t), then dz/dt = (∂f/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂f/∂y)(dy/dt). For z = f(x,y) with x = g(s,t) and y = h(s,t), we get ∂z/∂s = (∂f/∂x)(∂x/∂s) + (∂f/∂y)(∂y/∂s), and similarly for ∂z/∂t. The chain rule uses tree diagrams showing dependency paths, multiplying partial derivatives

topics.name: Computing integrals over rectangles
topics.description: Double integrals over rectangular regions R = [a,b] × [c,d] are computed using iterated integrals: ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA = ∫[a,b] ∫[c,d] f(x,y) dy dx or equivalently with reversed order. Fubini's Theorem guarantees that for continuous functions, the order of integration doesn't matter. The double integral represents volume under the surface z = f(x,y) above rectangle R when f ≥ 0, or signed volume when f

topics.name: Computing work in a vector field
topics.description: Work done by force field F moving an object along curve C from point A to B is W = ∫_C F·dr = ∫[a,b] F(r(t))·r'(t) dt, where r(t) parameterizes C. This measures energy transferred by the force along the path. When F points with motion direction (positive dot product), work is positive; opposite direction gives negative work; perpendicular force does no work. For conservative fields, work depends o

topics.name: Conservative vector field in 3-space topology primer
topics.description: This topic examines conservative vector fields in three-dimensional spaces with non-trivial topology. While conservative fields have zero curl everywhere, in multiply-connected spaces (with holes), a field can have zero curl yet not be conservative. Understanding the topological requirements for conservative fields—such as simply-connected domains—is essential for properly applying potential theor

topics.name: Conservative vector fields
topics.description: A vector field F is conservative if F = ∇f for some scalar potential function f. Conservative fields have several equivalent properties: line integrals are path-independent, ∫_C F·dr = 0 for closed curves, and curl F = 0 (in simply connected domains). Finding potential function: integrate P = ∂f/∂x to get f (up to function of y,z), then use Q = ∂f/∂y and R = ∂f/∂z to determine f completely. Conser

topics.name: Continuity
topics.description: A function f(x,y) is continuous at (a,b) if: (1) f(a,b) is defined, (2) lim((x,y)→(a,b)) f(x,y) exists, and (3) the limit equals f(a,b). Continuous functions can be evaluated by direct substitution. Sums, products, quotients (with non-zero denominator), and compositions of continuous functions are continuous. Most elementary functions (polynomials, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic) are cont

topics.name: Coordinate systems
topics.description: Coordinate systems provide frameworks for specifying positions in space using ordered tuples of numbers. In Calculus III, key systems include Cartesian (x, y, z), cylindrical (r, θ, z), and spherical (ρ, θ, φ) coordinates. Each system has advantages for different geometries: Cartesian for rectangular structures, cylindrical for objects with circular symmetry about an axis, and spherical for radial

topics.name: Cross product
topics.description: The cross product u × v of vectors u and v produces a vector perpendicular to both, with magnitude ||u × v|| = ||u|| ||v|| sin(θ) where θ is the angle between them. Direction follows the right-hand rule. Computed as u × v =

topics.name: Curl and divergence
topics.description: Curl and divergence are two fundamental differential operators applied to vector fields. Divergence measures the rate at which 'stuff' expands or contracts from a point, producing a scalar field. Curl measures the rotation or circulation of a field around a point, producing a vector field. Together, these operators characterize the local behavior of vector fields and are central to physics applica

topics.name: Curves in space
topics.description: Curves in 3D space are defined by vector-valued functions r(t) =

topics.name: Cylindrical and spherical coordinates
topics.description: Cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z) extend polar coordinates to 3D: x = r cos(θ), y = r sin(θ), z = z, useful for regions with circular symmetry about the z-axis. Spherical coordinates (ρ, θ, φ) use distance from origin ρ, azimuthal angle θ, and polar angle φ: x = ρ sin(φ) cos(θ), y = ρ sin(φ) sin(θ), z = ρ cos(φ), ideal for spherical regions. Both systems simplify integrals over certain geometries,

topics.name: Derivatives of vector functions
topics.description: The derivative of vector function r(t) =

topics.name: Determinant
topics.description: The determinant of a 3×3 matrix is a scalar computed using a specific formula involving products and differences of matrix entries. For matrix with rows [a₁,a₂,a₃], [b₁,b₂,b₃], [c₁,c₂,c₃], det = a₁(b₂c₃-b₃c₂) - a₂(b₁c₃-b₃c₁) + a₃(b₁c₂-b₂c₁). Geometrically, the determinant gives the signed volume of the parallelepiped formed by the three row vectors. Determinants appear in computing cross products,

topics.name: Differentiability
topics.description: A function f(x,y) is differentiable at (a,b) if it can be well-approximated by its tangent plane near that point: f(x,y) ≈ f(a,b) + fₓ(a,b)(x-a) + f_y(a,b)(y-b) with error approaching zero faster than distance from (a,b). Differentiability implies continuity and existence of all partial derivatives, but the converse isn't always true. Sufficient condition: if partial derivatives exist and are cont

topics.name: Directional derivative and gradient
topics.description: The directional derivative D_u f(x,y) measures the rate of change of f in the direction of unit vector u =

topics.name: Divergence theorem in 3d
topics.description: The divergence theorem in three dimensions relates the flux of a vector field through a closed surface to the triple integral of the field's divergence over the enclosed volume. It states that the total outward flow through a boundary surface equals the sum of all sources and sinks within the volume. This powerful theorem converts surface integrals into volume integrals and is fundamental to physi

topics.name: Dot product
topics.description: The dot product (scalar product) of vectors u =

topics.name: Electrostatics and gauss's law
topics.description: Gauss's law applies the divergence theorem to electrostatics, stating that the electric flux through a closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by the permittivity constant. This fundamental law relates the electric field's behavior at boundaries to charge distributions in the interior. It provides an elegant method for calculating electric fields with high symmetry and demonstrates a dee

topics.name: Extreme value theorem and absolute maximum/minimum
topics.description: The Extreme Value Theorem for multivariable functions states that a continuous function on a closed, bounded region D attains absolute maximum and minimum values. These extrema occur either at critical points in the interior of D or on the boundary of D. Finding absolute extrema requires: (1) finding all critical points inside D, (2) finding extreme values on the boundary (often using parameteriza

topics.name: Fundamental theorem of line integrals
topics.description: The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals states that if F = ∇f (F is conservative with potential function f), then ∫_C F·dr = f(B) - f(A) where A and B are the endpoints of curve C. This means work in a conservative field depends only on endpoints, not the path taken. The theorem generalizes the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to line integrals, providing powerful simplification for conservative

topics.name: Gradient and tangent planes
topics.description: The gradient ∇f =

topics.name: Green's theorem
topics.description: Green's theorem relates a line integral around a simple closed curve in the plane to a double integral over the region it encloses. It states that the circulation of a vector field around a closed curve equals the double integral of the curl (or a related expression) over the enclosed region. This fundamental theorem connects boundary integrals to interior properties and is essential for vector ca

topics.name: Green's theorem and conservative vector fields
topics.description: This topic explores the connection between Green's theorem and conservative vector fields, which have zero circulation around closed curves. A vector field is conservative if its line integral around any closed path is zero, which by Green's theorem means the field has zero curl. Conservative fields can be expressed as gradients of scalar potential functions, making path integrals path-independent

topics.name: Integral theorems overview
topics.description: This topic provides a comprehensive view of the major integral theorems in vector calculus: Green's theorem, Stokes's theorem, and the divergence theorem. These theorems form a unified framework connecting line integrals, surface integrals, and volume integrals through boundary relationships. They represent the multivariable generalizations of the fundamental theorem of calculus and are cornerston

topics.name: Integrating along a curve
topics.description: Line integrals compute accumulated quantities along curves. The scalar line integral ∫_C f ds integrates scalar function f along curve C with respect to arc length, measuring total of f distributed along the curve. For vector field F, the line integral ∫_C F·dr measures work done by F moving along C, computed as ∫[a,b] F(r(t))·r'(t) dt. Applications include computing mass of curved wires, work aga

topics.name: Integrating in cylindrical and spherical coordinates
topics.description: Integration in cylindrical coordinates: ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV = ∫∫∫ f(r cos θ, r sin θ, z) r dr dθ dz with limits describing E in cylindrical coordinates. In spherical: ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV = ∫∫∫ f(ρ sin φ cos θ, ρ sin φ sin θ, ρ cos φ) ρ² sin φ dρ dφ dθ. The Jacobian factors (r or ρ² sin φ) are crucial. These coordinate systems dramatically simplify integrals over cylinders, cones, spheres, and other sy

topics.name: Integrating over complicated regions
topics.description: Integrating over non-rectangular regions involves determining appropriate limits of integration that describe the region's boundaries. For Type I regions (vertically simple), y ranges between functions of x: ∫[a,b] ∫[g₁(x), g₂(x)] f(x,y) dy dx. For Type II regions (horizontally simple), x ranges between functions of y. Some regions require splitting into multiple subregions or switching integratio

topics.name: Integrating over triangles
topics.description: Integrating over triangular regions involves setting up double integrals with limits describing the triangle. For triangle with vertices, express one variable's limits as constants (outer integral) and the other variable's limits as linear functions of the outer variable (inner integral). Alternatively, parameterize the triangle using barycentric coordinates. This extends rectangular integration r

topics.name: Integrating vector fields
topics.description: Integrating vector fields encompasses line integrals ∫_C F·dr along curves and surface integrals ∫∫_S F·dS over surfaces. Line integrals measure circulation or work along paths. Surface integrals measure flux through surfaces - how much field 'flows through' the surface. For F =

topics.name: Interpreting divergence and 2d curl
topics.description: This topic focuses on understanding the physical and geometric meaning of divergence and curl in two dimensions. Divergence at a point indicates the net outward flux per unit area from that point, representing sources or sinks. In 2D, curl is a scalar measuring counterclockwise rotation intensity. These interpretations help visualize and understand vector field behavior in applications like fluid

topics.name: Intro to optimization
topics.description: Optimization in multivariable calculus finds maximum or minimum values of functions f(x,y) or f(x,y,z). Critical points occur where ∇f = 0 (all partial derivatives equal zero) or where partial derivatives don't exist. These points are candidates for local extrema or saddle points. The process involves finding critical points algebraically, then classifying them using the second derivative test. Ap

topics.name: Investigating limits
topics.description: Investigating limits in multiple variables involves techniques to determine if lim((x,y)→(a,b)) f(x,y) exists and find its value if it does. Methods include: testing along various paths (lines y=mx, parabolas, circles) to find contradictions; using polar coordinates centered at (a,b); applying the squeeze theorem; using limit laws for continuous functions; and employing inequalities to bound expre

topics.name: Jacobian of multivariable functions
topics.description: The Jacobian is a matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function. It represents the best linear approximation of a multivariable function near a given point and describes how the function transforms infinitesimal volumes in space. The determinant of the Jacobian matrix indicates how areas or volumes scale under the transformation and is crucial for changing variables in

topics.name: Level sets in 3d
topics.description: Level sets (level surfaces) of a function f(x,y,z) are surfaces where the function takes a constant value: f(x,y,z) = k for various constants k. These surfaces show how the function value changes through space - moving between level surfaces means function value changes. Level sets provide 3D visualization of four-dimensional graphs (can't directly plot w = f(x,y,z) in 3D). Examples include equipo

topics.name: Limits in several variables
topics.description: Limits in several variables extend single-variable limits to functions f(x,y) or f(x,y,z): lim((x,y)→(a,b)) f(x,y) = L means f(x,y) approaches L as (x,y) approaches (a,b) from any direction. The key challenge is that points can approach (a,b) along infinitely many paths. If different paths give different limit values, the limit doesn't exist. If approaching along all paths yields the same value an

topics.name: Linear change of coordinates
topics.description: Linear change of coordinates uses linear transformation to convert integration from (x,y) to (u,v): x = au + bv, y = cu + dv. The change of variables formula ∫∫_R f(x,y) dx dy = ∫∫_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J| du dv uses the Jacobian J = ∂(x,y)/∂(u,v) = |det([∂x/∂u, ∂x/∂v; ∂y/∂u, ∂y/∂v])|. This generalizes polar coordinates and simplifies integrals when the transformed region S has simpler boundaries t

topics.name: Linear transformations
topics.description: Linear transformations T: R^n → R^m are functions that preserve vector addition and scalar multiplication: T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cu) = cT(u). Represented by matrices, T(x) = Ax maps vector x to Ax. Key properties include transforming lines to lines (or points), parallels to parallels, and zero to zero. Understanding includes finding transformation matrices, computing images of vectors and r

topics.name: Lines from gradient
topics.description: Normal lines to surfaces are lines perpendicular to the surface at a point, with direction given by the gradient vector at that point. For surface f(x,y,z) = k at point (x₀,y₀,z₀), the normal line is r(t) =

topics.name: More examples of double integrals
topics.description: Additional double integral examples reinforce techniques including: choosing appropriate integration order, handling regions requiring splitting, applying symmetry to simplify calculations, computing volumes between surfaces, finding areas using ∫∫ 1 dA, calculating averages f_avg = (1/A) ∫∫ f dA, and modeling physical quantities like mass (density integrated), center of mass, and probability (joi

topics.name: Nonlinear change of coordinates
topics.description: Nonlinear coordinate transformations use general functions x = x(u,v), y = y(u,v) to change integration variables, with Jacobian J = |∂(x,y)/∂(u,v)| accounting for area distortion that now varies with position. Examples include polar coordinates (nonlinear due to trigonometric functions) and other curvilinear systems. The formula ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA = ∫∫_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J(u,v)| du dv generalizes t

topics.name: Optimization examples
topics.description: Optimization examples apply theoretical methods to practical problems: maximizing volumes or areas given constraints, minimizing material costs in manufacturing, finding shortest distances to curves or surfaces, optimizing resource allocation, and determining best-fit parameters in models. These problems involve translating word problems into mathematical functions, finding critical points, applyi

topics.name: Optimization subject to a constraint
topics.description: Constrained optimization finds extrema of f(x,y,z) subject to constraint g(x,y,z) = k. This restricts search to points on a surface or curve rather than the entire domain. Methods include: (1) substitution - solve constraint for one variable and substitute into f, reducing to unconstrained problem; (2) parameterization - parameterize the constraint surface and optimize over parameters; (3) Lagrang

topics.name: Partial derivatives
topics.description: Partial derivatives measure how a multivariable function changes with respect to one variable while holding others constant. For f(x,y), the partial derivative ∂f/∂x = lim(h→0) [f(x+h,y) - f(x,y)]/h measures the rate of change in the x-direction. Geometrically, ∂f/∂x gives the slope of the curve formed by intersecting the surface z = f(x,y) with a plane of constant y. Partial derivatives are compu

topics.name: Path independence of line integrals
topics.description: A line integral ∫_C F·dr is path-independent if its value depends only on the endpoints of C, not the specific path connecting them. Path independence occurs if and only if F is conservative (F = ∇f for some potential function f). Equivalently, ∫_C F·dr = 0 for every closed curve C. Testing for path independence includes checking if curl F = 0 (in simply connected domains). Path-independent integr

topics.name: Planes in 3-space
topics.description: A plane in 3D space is a flat two-dimensional surface extending infinitely, uniquely determined by a point and a normal vector (perpendicular to the plane). The plane equation is a(x-x₀) + b(y-y₀) + c(z-z₀) = 0 or ax + by + cz = d, where

topics.name: Polar changes of coordinates
topics.description: Polar coordinate substitution in double integrals uses x = r cos(θ), y = r sin(θ), transforming ∫∫_R f(x,y) dA to ∫∫ f(r cos θ, r sin θ) r dr dθ. The extra factor r is the Jacobian accounting for area distortion. Polar coordinates simplify integrals over circular regions, sectors, and when integrand involves x² + y². Limits: r typically ranges from 0 or an inner radius to outer radius, θ ranges ov

topics.name: Quadric surfaces
topics.description: Quadric surfaces are 3D surfaces defined by second-degree equations in x, y, and z, generalizing conic sections to three dimensions. Types include ellipsoids (egg-shaped), hyperboloids (saddle-like, one or two sheets), paraboloids (bowl-shaped, elliptic or hyperbolic), cones, and cylinders. Standard forms like x²/a² + y²/b² + z²/c² = 1 (ellipsoid) help identify and graph these surfaces. Understand

topics.name: Review of limits
topics.description: Limits review reinforces single-variable limit concepts: lim(x→a) f(x) = L means f(x) approaches L as x approaches a. Key ideas include one-sided limits, limits at infinity, limit laws (sum, product, quotient rules), continuous functions, and techniques like factoring, rationalization, and L'Hôpital's rule. This foundation is essential because multivariable limits build on these concepts but invol

topics.name: Second derivative test
topics.description: The second derivative test classifies critical points of f(x,y) using second partial derivatives. The discriminant D = fₓₓf_yy - (fₓ_y)² is evaluated at critical point (a,b) where ∇f(a,b) = 0. If D > 0 and fₓₓ > 0: local minimum; if D > 0 and fₓₓ

topics.name: Stokes's theorem
topics.description: Stokes's theorem is a fundamental result relating the surface integral of the curl of a vector field over a surface to the line integral of the field around the surface's boundary curve. It generalizes Green's theorem to three dimensions and states that circulation around a boundary equals the total curl through the enclosed surface. This theorem is essential in physics for understanding electroma

topics.name: Surface area
topics.description: Surface area in multivariable calculus measures the total area of a curved surface in three-dimensional space. For parametric surfaces, it is computed using a double integral involving the magnitude of the cross product of partial derivative vectors. For surfaces given as z=f(x,y), the formula incorporates the partial derivatives to account for how the surface stretches and tilts relative to the x

topics.name: Surface integrals
topics.description: Surface integrals extend the concept of integration to functions defined over curved surfaces in three-dimensional space. They compute the accumulated value of a scalar field over a surface, accounting for the surface's geometry. Surface integrals are used to calculate quantities like mass, charge distribution, or flux, and require integrating the product of a function with the surface area elemen

topics.name: Surface integrals of vector fields
topics.description: Surface integrals of vector fields, also called flux integrals, measure the flow of a vector field through a surface in three-dimensional space. The integral computes the dot product of the vector field with the surface's normal vector over the entire surface. This quantifies how much of the field passes through the surface and is crucial for applications in fluid dynamics, electromagnetism, and h

topics.name: Surfaces in 3-space
topics.description: Surfaces in three-dimensional space are two-dimensional objects embedded in 3D, defined by equations relating x, y, and z coordinates, or parametrically using two parameters. They can be represented as level sets of functions (implicit form), explicit functions z=f(x,y), or parametric equations. Understanding surfaces is fundamental to visualizing and analyzing three-dimensional geometric objects

topics.name: Tangent planes
topics.description: The tangent plane to a surface z = f(x,y) at point (x₀, y₀, f(x₀,y₀)) is the best linear approximation to the surface near that point. Its equation is z - z₀ = fₓ(x₀,y₀)(x - x₀) + f_y(x₀,y₀)(y - y₀), where fₓ and f_y are partial derivatives at (x₀,y₀). Geometrically, the tangent plane contains all tangent lines to curves on the surface through that point. The tangent plane is to surfaces what the

topics.name: Tangent planes to parametric surfaces
topics.description: A tangent plane to a parametric surface at a point is the flat plane that best approximates the surface near that point. It is constructed using two tangent vectors obtained by taking partial derivatives of the parametric equations with respect to each parameter. The normal vector to this plane is found via the cross product of these tangent vectors, providing crucial information for surface orien

topics.name: Tests for conservativity
topics.description: To test if vector field F =

topics.name: Triple integrals
topics.description: Triple integrals ∫∫∫_E f(x,y,z) dV integrate over three-dimensional regions E, computed as iterated integrals in six possible orders (choosing order strategically based on region boundaries). When f = 1, the integral gives volume of E; when f = δ(x,y,z) is density, it gives mass. Applications include finding volumes, masses, centers of mass, moments of inertia in 3D solids, and computing average v

topics.name: Understanding curl
topics.description: A deeper exploration of curl focusing on its physical interpretation as measuring local rotation intensity and axis in a vector field. Curl at a point indicates how much and around which axis the field tends to rotate. Zero curl indicates irrotational flow (conservative field), while non-zero curl indicates circulation. This understanding is crucial for applications in fluid mechanics, electromagn

topics.name: Using lagrange multipliers
topics.description: Lagrange multipliers solve constrained optimization: optimize f(x,y,z) subject to g(x,y,z) = k by introducing multiplier λ and solving the system ∇f = λ∇g along with the constraint equation. This gives equations fₓ = λgₓ, f_y = λg_y, f_z = λg_z, plus g = k. Solutions yield critical points; comparing function values at these points (and considering boundary if applicable) finds extrema. The method

topics.name: Vector addition and scalar multiplication
topics.description: Vector addition combines vectors component-wise:

topics.name: Vector fields
topics.description: A vector field assigns a vector to each point in space: F(x,y,z) =

topics.name: Vectors
topics.description: Vectors are mathematical objects with both magnitude and direction, represented as ordered tuples or arrows in space. In 3D, vectors are written as

topics.name: Visualizing functions of several variables
topics.description: Functions of several variables map multiple inputs to one output: f(x,y) or f(x,y,z). Visualizing f(x,y) uses 3D surfaces (graphs z = f(x,y)) or level curves (contour maps showing f(x,y) = k for various constants k). For f(x,y,z), level surfaces show f(x,y,z) = k. Visualization techniques include 3D plots, contour plots, cross-sections, and interactive tools. Understanding multivariable function b

MATH 415.name: Applied Linear Algebra
MATH 415.description: Introductory course emphasizing techniques of linear algebra with applications to engineering; topics include matrix operations, determinants, linear equations, vector spaces, linear transformations, eigenvalues, and eigenvectors, inner products and norms, orthogonality, equilibrium, and linear dynamical systems.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of linear algebra to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using linear algebra.

ME 270.name: Design for Manufacturability
ME 270.description: Introduction to DFM methodologies and tools; material selection (new and traditional materials); designing for primary manufacturing processes (cutting fundamentals, casting, forming, and shaping); designing with plastics (snap-fits, integral hinges, etc.); design for assembly (DFA); geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (GD&T).
objectives: Ability to evaluate trade-offs between alternative design and process selection decisions with regard to manufacturability.
Broad working knowledge of manufacturing processes and technologies.

Ability to work as a team on a design project and consider in detail a broad spectrum of issues including manufacturability, cost (local and global), environmental, safety, ethical, political, and cultural context.
Ability to implement DFM methodologies and understanding of fundamental issues in CAD/ CAM, metrology, and manufacturing automation.
Ability to quantitatively estimate forces, powers, pressures, and rates in metal forming and material removal processes, and choose appropriate operating parameters.
Ability to design and conduct laboratory experiments with manufacturing processes and metrology equipment.
Familiarity with CAD/CAM and process simulation software as a tool for design and process planning tasks.
Broad knowledge of manufacturing processes and technologies that are currently in the developmental stage.

topics.name: Cnc machining
topics.description: Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machining uses computer programs to control machine tool motion, enabling precise, repeatable, and complex part manufacturing. CNC systems include mills, lathes, routers, and multi-axis machines. Programming can be manual (G-code) or CAM-generated from CAD models. CNC advantages include high precision, repeatability, lights-out operation, and handling complex geome

topics.name: Concept selection
topics.description: Concept selection systematically evaluates and chooses among alternative design concepts using structured methods like Pugh matrices, weighted decision matrices, or analytical hierarchy process (AHP). The process defines evaluation criteria (performance, cost, manufacturability, risk, etc.), weights their importance, scores alternatives, and calculates overall ratings. Effective concept selection

topics.name: Design for assembly (dfa)
topics.description: Design for Assembly (DFA) minimizes assembly time and cost through systematic design simplification: reducing part count, eliminating fasteners, ensuring easy part handling and insertion, mistake-proofing (poka-yoke), and designing for automated assembly. DFA methodologies provide quantitative metrics for comparing designs based on assembly difficulty, using databases of handling and insertion tim

topics.name: Design for economic manufacture (material removal, casting, forming, & shaping)
topics.description: Design for economic manufacture optimizes designs for specific manufacturing processes to minimize cost while meeting requirements. For material removal (machining), this means minimizing cuts, using standard tools, avoiding difficult geometries, and considering setup time. For casting, it involves uniform wall thickness, generous fillets, draft angles, and parting line placement. For forming (sta

topics.name: Designing with plastics (snap-fits, integral hinges, etc.)
topics.description: Designing with plastics exploits unique material properties: flexibility for snap-fits, chemical bonding for welding, flow characteristics for complex shapes, and moldability for integral features. Snap-fits use material elasticity for tool-free assembly. Integral hinges use thin sections in polypropylene creating durable flexure points. Living hinges, texture, and overmolding enable designs impos

topics.name: Design of experiments
topics.description: Design of Experiments (DOE) is a statistical methodology for systematically varying process or design parameters to understand their effects on outcomes, optimize performance, and quantify relationships. DOE techniques include factorial designs (testing all combinations), fractional factorial (testing subset), response surface methodology (optimizing), and Taguchi methods (robust design). DOE enab

topics.name: Dfm overview/strategy
topics.description: Design for Manufacturability (DFM) is a systematic approach integrating manufacturing considerations early in design, reducing production costs, improving quality, and shortening time-to-market. DFM strategies include simplifying designs, reducing part counts, using standard components, selecting appropriate materials and processes, designing for existing capabilities, and considering assembly. Th

topics.name: Geometric dimensioning and tolerancing (gd&t)
topics.description: In manufacturability context, GD&T enables functional specifications maximizing manufacturing tolerance while ensuring assembly and performance requirements. Understanding GD&T allows designers to specify what matters functionally while allowing manufacturing flexibility in non-critical aspects. Proper datum selection aligns with manufacturing and inspection fixtures. Using position tolerances wit

topics.name: Injection molding
topics.description: Injection molding manufactures plastic parts by injecting molten polymer into a mold cavity under high pressure, then cooling and ejecting. The process enables complex geometries, high production rates, excellent repeatability, and minimal secondary operations. Design considerations include wall thickness uniformity (preventing sink marks), draft angles (enabling ejection), parting line location,

topics.name: Inspection/metrology (cmm/gd&t)
topics.description: Inspection and metrology verify manufactured parts meet design specifications using measurement instruments and techniques. Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMMs) are programmable devices with precision probes measuring 3D coordinates of part features, comparing actual dimensions to CAD models. CMMs excel at verifying GD&T specifications (position, perpendicularity, flatness, etc.) providing objecti

topics.name: Introduction to machine tools (in machine shop)
topics.description: Machine tools remove material to create precise shapes through operations like turning (lathe), milling (end mill/face mill), drilling, grinding, and boring. Understanding machine tool capabilities, limitations, and economics informs design decisions. Lathes create cylindrical parts through rotation. Mills create features on flat or contoured surfaces. Each machine has accuracy limits, size constr

topics.name: Product design specification
topics.description: Product Design Specification (PDS) is a comprehensive document defining all requirements a product must satisfy, including functional performance, regulatory compliance, manufacturing constraints, cost targets, reliability, aesthetics, environmental conditions, and market positioning. The PDS guides design decisions, provides measurable success criteria, and serves as a contract between stakeholde

topics.name: Quality function deployment (qfd)
topics.description: Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured method for translating customer requirements into engineering specifications and manufacturing parameters. The process uses matrices (House of Quality) correlating 'what' customers want with 'how' engineering will deliver it, prioritizing features based on importance and competitive benchmarking. QFD cascades customer voice through multiple stages:

topics.name: Rapid prototyping
topics.description: Rapid prototyping (additive manufacturing/3D printing) builds parts layer-by-layer from CAD models without tooling, enabling fast iteration, complex geometries, and customization. Technologies include FDM (fused deposition modeling), SLA (stereolithography), SLS (selective laser sintering), and metal printing. Applications range from concept models to functional prototypes to end-use parts. Design

topics.name: Sand casting
