topics.description: Sand casting creates metal parts by pouring molten metal into sand molds formed around patterns. The process accommodates large parts, complex shapes, and various alloys at relatively low tooling cost. Design considerations include shrinkage allowance, draft angles, uniform wall thickness, generous fillets, parting line placement, coring for internal cavities, and avoiding sharp corners causing st

topics.name: Selection of materials (life-cycle economics)
topics.description: Material selection considering life-cycle economics evaluates total cost of ownership, not just material purchase price. Analysis includes manufacturing costs (machinability, yield, scrap), operating costs (weight affecting fuel, energy efficiency), maintenance (corrosion resistance, wear), and end-of-life (recyclability, disposal). Methods like Ashby charts plot material properties enabling syste

topics.name: Water jet cutting
topics.description: Water jet cutting uses high-pressure water (often with abrasive) to cut materials through erosion rather than melting or burning. The process cuts virtually any material (metals, composites, glass, stone, plastics) without heat-affected zones, mechanical stresses, or hazardous gases. Advantages include no thermal distortion, cutting thick sections, tight nesting minimizing scrap, and safe operatio

PHYS 212.name: University Physics: Elec & Mag
PHYS 212.description: Coulomb's Law, electric fields, Gauss' Law, electric potential, capacitance, circuits, magnetic forces and fields, Ampere's law, induction, electromagnetic waves, polarization, and geometrical optics. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: learn the basic concepts of electricity and magnetism.
students view prelectures and answer checkpoint questions that are used to prepare lectures.
interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles.
laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles.
online homework to engage students in problem solving.
interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills.

topics.name: Ac circuits
topics.description: AC (alternating current) circuits use sinusoidally varying voltages and currents, requiring analysis with phasors, complex impedance, and RMS values. Resistors, capacitors, and inductors respond differently to AC: resistors dissipate power, capacitors block DC but pass AC (capacitive reactance), and inductors pass DC but oppose AC (inductive reactance). Understanding AC is essential for power dist

topics.name: Ac power & resonant circuits
topics.description: AC power differs from DC power because voltage and current may be out of phase, with real power depending on the power factor (cos φ). Resonance occurs in RLC circuits when inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, minimizing impedance and maximizing current at resonant frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC). Understanding power factor, resonance, and frequency response is crucial for power transmission, radio

topics.name: Ampere's law
topics.description: Ampère's law states that the line integral of magnetic field around a closed path equals the current passing through the enclosed area (times μ₀). For symmetric current distributions (infinite wires, solenoids, toroids), Ampère's law provides an elegant method for calculating magnetic fields, analogous to how Gauss's law simplifies electric field calculations. This law, part of Maxwell's equations

topics.name: Biot-savart law
topics.description: The Biot-Savart law calculates the magnetic field produced by a current element, stating that field contribution is proportional to current, element length, and inversely proportional to distance squared, with direction given by the right-hand rule. Integrating over current distributions (wires, loops, solenoids) yields total field. This law is fundamental to understanding how currents create magn

topics.name: Capacitance
topics.description: Capacitance measures a conductor's ability to store electric charge at a given potential, defined as C = Q/V. Capacitance depends on geometry and materials, not on charge or voltage. Capacitors consist of two conductors separated by an insulator (dielectric), storing energy in the electric field between plates. Understanding capacitance is essential for electronics, filtering, energy storage, and

topics.name: Capacitors
topics.description: Capacitors are devices that store electrical energy in electric fields, consisting of conductors separated by insulators. Parallel plate capacitors have uniform fields, with capacitance depending on area, separation, and dielectric material. Capacitors in series and parallel combine differently than resistors. Capacitors store energy (U = ½CV²), block DC current while passing AC, and are fundament

topics.name: Coulomb's law
topics.description: Coulomb's law describes the electrostatic force between two point charges, stating force magnitude is proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them. Force direction is along the line connecting charges—repulsive for like charges, attractive for opposite charges. This fundamental law, analogous to Newton's gravitational law, underlies all e

topics.name: Electric fields
topics.description: An electric field is a vector field representing the force per unit charge at each point in space, created by source charges. It enables analyzing electrical forces without explicitly considering test charges. Field lines visualize field direction (tangent to line) and strength (density of lines). Understanding field calculations from point charges, continuous distributions, and using superpositio

topics.name: Electric flux
topics.description: Electric flux quantifies the amount of electric field penetrating through a surface, calculated as the surface integral of the electric field's normal component. It represents the 'flow' of field lines through an area, though nothing physically flows. Flux is positive when field lines emerge from a surface, negative when entering. Understanding flux, including its relationship to charge and surfac

topics.name: Electric potential
topics.description: Electric potential (voltage) is the electric potential energy per unit charge at a point, representing the work needed to bring a unit positive charge from infinity to that point. Potential is a scalar field, simpler than the vector electric field, with electric field pointing in the direction of maximum potential decrease (E = -∇V). Potential differences drive current flow and determine energy ch

topics.name: Electromagnetic waves
topics.description: Electromagnetic waves are coupled oscillations of electric and magnetic fields propagating through space at light speed (c = 3×10⁸ m/s), requiring no medium. E and B fields are perpendicular to each other and to propagation direction, with magnitudes related by E = cB. The electromagnetic spectrum ranges from radio waves to gamma rays, all traveling at c in vacuum. Understanding EM waves is fundam

topics.name: Faraday's law
topics.description: Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction states that changing magnetic flux through a loop induces an EMF proportional to the rate of flux change (ε = -dΦ_B/dt). The negative sign (Lenz's law) indicates induced current opposes the flux change. This fundamental law explains generators, transformers, inductors, and electromagnetic wave production. Faraday's law connects changing magnetic fields to

topics.name: Forces and magnetic dipoles
topics.description: Magnetic dipoles are objects with north and south magnetic poles, like bar magnets or current loops, characterized by magnetic dipole moment. They experience torque in magnetic fields tending to align the dipole with the field, and forces in non-uniform fields pulling toward stronger field regions. Understanding dipole behavior explains compass operation, magnetic materials, atomic magnetism, and

topics.name: Gauss's law
topics.description: Gauss's law states that the net electric flux through any closed surface equals the enclosed charge divided by the permittivity constant (ε₀). This fundamental law, one of Maxwell's equations, relates electric field behavior at boundaries to interior charge distributions. For highly symmetric charge distributions (spherical, cylindrical, planar), Gauss's law provides an elegant method for calculat

topics.name: Kirchoff's laws
topics.description: Kirchhoff's laws are fundamental rules for circuit analysis. The junction rule (current law) states that total current entering a junction equals total current leaving, reflecting charge conservation. The loop rule (voltage law) states that the sum of potential differences around any closed loop is zero, reflecting energy conservation. These laws enable systematic analysis of complex circuits with

topics.name: Lenses
topics.description: Lenses are transparent curved optical elements that refract light to converge or diverge rays, forming images. Converging lenses focus parallel rays to a focal point; diverging lenses spread rays apart. The thin lens equation (1/f = 1/do + 1/di) relates object distance, image distance, and focal length. Magnification, real versus virtual images, and lens combinations are crucial for cameras, eyegl

topics.name: Magnetic force
topics.description: Magnetic force acts on moving charges in magnetic fields, perpendicular to both velocity and field direction, given by F = qv × B. This force does no work since it's perpendicular to velocity, causing charges to move in circular or helical paths. Magnetic force on current-carrying wires depends on current, length, and field strength. Understanding the right-hand rule and motion in magnetic fields

topics.name: Maxwell's displacement current and electromagnetic waves
topics.description: Maxwell introduced displacement current to complete Ampère's law, recognizing that changing electric fields (even in vacuum) create magnetic fields just as currents do. This modification predicts electromagnetic waves: self-propagating oscillations of coupled electric and magnetic fields traveling at light speed. Maxwell's equations unified electricity, magnetism, and optics, predicting radio wave

topics.name: Mirrors
topics.description: Mirrors reflect light to form images through specular reflection. Plane mirrors create virtual, upright, same-size images. Curved mirrors (concave and convex) focus or diverge light with focal length f = R/2 (R is radius of curvature). The mirror equation (1/f = 1/do + 1/di) determines image location and properties. Understanding mirrors is essential for telescopes, solar concentrators, vehicle mi

topics.name: Motional emf
topics.description: Motional EMF is the voltage generated when a conductor moves through a magnetic field, with charges inside experiencing magnetic force causing charge separation and potential difference. The EMF depends on conductor velocity, field strength, and length (ε = Bℓv). This phenomenon, explained by the Lorentz force on moving charges, is fundamental to electric generators, where mechanical energy conver

topics.name: Oscillations: lc circuits
topics.description: LC circuits contain inductors and capacitors with no resistance, exhibiting electrical oscillations where energy continuously exchanges between electric field (capacitor) and magnetic field (inductor). The oscillation frequency ω = 1/√(LC) depends only on circuit components. LC circuits demonstrate electrical resonance, analogous to mechanical oscillators, and are fundamental to radio tuning, sign

topics.name: Polarization
topics.description: Polarization refers to the orientation of electromagnetic wave oscillations, particularly the electric field vector direction. Light can be linearly polarized (oscillating in one plane), circularly polarized (rotating), or unpolarized (random orientations). Polarization occurs through reflection, scattering, or passing through polarizing filters. Understanding polarization is essential for LCD dis

topics.name: Rc circuits
topics.description: RC circuits contain resistors and capacitors, exhibiting time-dependent behavior as capacitors charge or discharge through resistors. The time constant τ = RC characterizes how quickly the circuit responds, with voltage and current decaying or growing exponentially. RC circuits act as filters, timing devices, and smoothing circuits. Understanding exponential charging/discharging, time constants, a

topics.name: Reflection and refraction
topics.description: Reflection occurs when light bounces off surfaces, obeying the law that incident and reflected angles are equal. Refraction is light bending when entering different media due to wave speed changes, governed by Snell's law (n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂). The refractive index measures light speed reduction in materials. Understanding these phenomena explains mirrors, lenses, rainbows, mirages, fiber optics, an

topics.name: Self-inductance rl circuits
topics.description: Self-inductance (L) measures a coil's opposition to current changes, with changing current inducing an EMF opposing that change (ε = -L dI/dt). RL circuits containing resistors and inductors exhibit time-dependent current growth or decay with time constant τ = L/R. Inductors store energy in magnetic fields (U = ½LI²) and oppose sudden current changes, acting as electrical inertia. They're essentia

topics.name: Simple circuits
topics.description: Simple circuits consist of power sources (batteries), resistors, and connecting wires arranged in series or parallel configurations. Current flows from high to low potential, with resistance limiting flow rate. Ohm's law (V = IR) relates voltage, current, and resistance. Understanding series circuits (same current everywhere) versus parallel circuits (same voltage across branches) and calculating

TAM 210.name: Introduction to Statics
TAM 210.description: Forces, moments, and couples; resultants of force systems; equilibrium analysis and free-body diagrams; analysis of forces acting on members of trusses, frames, etc.; shear-force and bending-moment distributions; Coulomb friction; centroids and center of mass; applications of statics in design.
objectives: Be able to calculate resultants of discrete and distributed force systems.
Be able to draw correct free body diagrams.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems for particles, systems of particles, rigid bodies, frames, and machines.
Be able to analyze trusses by the methods of joints and of sections.
Be able to calculate internal forces and moments in frame and machine members.
Be able to draw shear-force and bending-moment diagrams.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving Coulomb friction.
Be able to calculate centroids, mass centers, and centers of gravity of general bodies.

topics.name: Bending-moment diagrams
topics.description: Bending moment M(x) is internal moment about beam's cross-section at position x, causing beam to curve. Sign convention: positive moment causes compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Relationship: dM/dx = V(x), connecting moment and shear. M maximum where dM/dx = V = 0. Moment diagrams plot M(x) vs. x. Distributed load creates parabolic moment curves. Maximum moment location critical for

topics.name: Centroids and center of mass
topics.description: Centroid is geometric center of area, volume, or line; center of mass is mass-weighted average position. For homogeneous body, centroid coincides with center of mass (center of gravity). Coordinates: x̄ = ∫x dA / ∫dA (and similarly ȳ, z̄). For composite shapes: x̄ = ΣxᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ (weighted average of parts). Center of gravity is where weight W = mg effectively acts. Centroids used for locating resul

topics.name: Coulomb friction
topics.description: Coulomb (dry) friction is resistance force between contacting surfaces, opposing relative motion or impending motion. Static friction fs ≤ μsN prevents motion until threshold; kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes sliding motion, where μs (static) and μk (kinetic) are friction coefficients, N is normal force. Static friction adjusts up to maximum: fs,max = μsN. Friction force direction opposes motion

topics.name: Couples
topics.description: A couple consists of two parallel forces of equal magnitude but opposite direction, separated by distance d. Couple produces pure rotation without translation. Couple moment **M** = **r** × **F** has magnitude M = Fd, where d is perpendicular distance between forces. Unique property: couple's moment is same about any point (independent of moment center). Couples cannot be balanced by single force,

topics.name: Distributed forces
topics.description: Distributed forces spread continuously over area or volume, rather than acting at single point. Examples: pressure, weight of beam, fluid forces, wind loading. Represented by intensity w(x) (force per unit length/area). Total force: F = ∫w(x)dx. Location of resultant: x̄ = ∫xw(x)dx / ∫w(x)dx (centroid of loading diagram). Common distributions: uniform (constant w), linear, parabolic. Replace distr

topics.name: Equilibrium
topics.description: Static equilibrium occurs when object remains at rest: no translation (ΣF = 0) and no rotation (ΣM = 0). Six scalar equations in 3D: three for force components, three for moment components. In 2D: three equations (ΣFₓ=0, ΣFᵧ=0, ΣM=0). Equilibrium requires balancing all forces and moments. Used to determine unknown forces, reactions, and internal forces in structures. Two-force members have forces

topics.name: Equilibrium analysis
topics.description: Equilibrium analysis applies ΣF = 0 and ΣM = 0 to determine unknown forces and reactions in static systems. Process: (1) draw free-body diagram showing all forces, (2) choose coordinate system, (3) write equilibrium equations, (4) solve simultaneously for unknowns. Systematic approach handles multiple connected bodies. Statically determinate systems: number of unknowns equals number of equilibrium

topics.name: Equipollent systems
topics.description: Two force systems are equipollent if they have the same resultant force and same resultant moment about any point. Equipollent systems produce identical external effects but may differ internally. Test: ΣF₁ = ΣF₂ and ΣM₁ = ΣM₂ (about same point). Useful for simplifying complex force distributions to equivalent simpler systems. Example: distributed load replaced by single resultant force. Equipolle

topics.name: Forces
topics.description: Forces are vector quantities representing push or pull interactions, measured in Newtons (N) or pounds (lb). Characterized by magnitude, direction, and point of application (line of action). Types: contact forces (normal, friction, tension) and body forces (gravity, magnetic). Newton's first law (statics): object in equilibrium has ΣF = 0 (zero net force). Forces can be resolved into components, c

topics.name: Free-body diagrams
topics.description: Free-body diagram (FBD) is a sketch isolating a body from surroundings, showing all external forces and moments acting on it. Essential for equilibrium analysis. Includes: applied forces, reaction forces (supports), weight, dimensions. Constraints removed and replaced with reaction forces. Types of supports: pin (two reactions), roller (one reaction perpendicular), fixed (three reactions: two forc

topics.name: Methods of joint and sections
topics.description: Two methods analyze truss internal forces. Method of Joints: isolate each joint as FBD, apply equilibrium ΣFₓ=0, ΣFᵧ=0. Start at joint with ≤2 unknowns. Proceeds joint-by-joint. Method of Sections: cut through members of interest, isolate portion as FBD, apply all three equilibrium equations. Efficient for finding specific member forces without solving entire truss. Both yield internal member forc

topics.name: Moments
topics.description: Moment (torque) is the rotational effect of a force about a point or axis, calculated as **M** = **r** × **F**, where **r** is position vector from pivot to force application point. Magnitude: M = rF sin θ = Fd, where d is perpendicular distance (moment arm). Units: N·m or lb·ft. Moments cause rotation; positive/negative indicates direction (right-hand rule). Varignon's theorem: moment of resultan

topics.name: Multi-force members
topics.description: Multi-force members have forces acting at three or more points or in non-collinear directions, unlike two-force members (forces only at ends along axis). Experience bending and shear in addition to axial forces. Examples: beams, frames, machine parts. Analysis requires considering forces and moments at multiple points. Internal forces vary along member length. Free-body diagrams show distributed l

topics.name: Resultants
topics.description: The resultant is the single force and/or couple that replaces a system of forces, producing the same external effect. Force resultant **R** = ΣF (vector sum of all forces). Moment resultant **M**ᴼ = Σ(**rᵢ** × **Fᵢ**) about point O. General system reduces to force-couple system at chosen point, or single resultant force if moment about one point is zero (concurrent or parallel forces). Finding res

topics.name: Review of vector algebra
topics.description: Vector algebra reviews mathematical operations for vectors in 2D and 3D. Vectors have magnitude and direction, represented as bold or with arrows: **F** or F⃗. Operations include: addition (parallelogram/triangle rule), subtraction, scalar multiplication, dot product (**a**·**b** = |**a**||**b**|cos θ, yields scalar), cross product (**a**×**b** = |**a**||**b**|sin θ **n̂**, yields vector perpendic

topics.name: Shear-force
topics.description: Shear force V(x) is internal force perpendicular to beam axis at position x, representing transverse loading effect. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation of left section. V varies along beam length according to applied loads. Relationship: dV/dx = -w(x), where w is distributed load intensity. Shear maximum often at supports or concentrated load points. Shear diagrams plot V(x)

topics.name: Statics and structural design
topics.description: Statics principles applied to structural design ensure buildings, bridges, and machines safely support loads. Process: (1) identify loads (dead, live, wind, seismic), (2) structural analysis (forces, moments, reactions), (3) member sizing based on stress limits, (4) safety factors for uncertainties. Considerations: material properties (strength, stiffness), failure modes (yielding, buckling, fatig

topics.name: Trusses
topics.description: Trusses are structures composed of slender members (bars) connected at joints (pins), forming triangular units. Assumptions: members are two-force members (axial forces only), loads applied only at joints, joints are frictionless pins. Members in tension (pulling) or compression (pushing). Trusses efficiently carry loads through internal axial forces. Common types: Pratt, Howe, Warren. Analysis de

TAM 211.name: Statics
TAM 211.description: Forces, moments, and couples; resultants of force systems; equilibrium analysis and free-body diagrams; analysis of forces acting on members of trusses, frames, etc.; shear-force and bending-moment distributions; Coulomb friction; centroids, center of mass, moment of inertia, polar moment of inertia, and product of inertia; virtual work; hydrostatic pressure; applications of statics in design.
objectives: Be able to calculate resultants of discrete and distributed force systems.
Be able to draw correct free body diagrams.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems for particles, systems of particles, rigid bodies, frames, and machines.
Be able to analyze trusses by the methods of joints and of sections.
Be able to calculate internal forces and moments in frame and machine members.
Be able to draw shear-force and bending-moment diagrams.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving Coulomb friction.
Be able to calculate centroids, mass centers, and centers of gravity of general bodies.
Be able to calculate moments and products of inertia of plane regions.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems in terms of the principle of virtual work.
Be able to set up and solve equilibrium problems involving hydrostatic pressure.

topics.name: Bending-moment diagrams
topics.description: Bending moment M(x) is the internal moment about beam's neutral axis at any cross-section, causing beam curvature. Positive moment creates compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Calculated by summing moments on either side of section. Key relationship: dM/dx = V(x) connects moment slope to shear. Maximum moment occurs where shear equals zero. Moment diagrams plot M(x) along beam length,

topics.name: Centroids and center of mass
topics.description: Centroid is the geometric center of an area, volume, or line; center of mass is the mass-weighted average position where total weight effectively acts. For homogeneous bodies, centroid and center of mass coincide. Calculated by weighted averaging: x̄ = ∫x dA / ∫dA for areas, or x̄ = ΣxᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ for composite shapes. Centroids determine resultant location for distributed loads, stability analysis,

topics.name: Coulomb friction
topics.description: Coulomb (dry) friction is the tangential resistance force between contacting surfaces opposing motion or impending motion. Static friction fs adjusts from zero up to maximum fs,max = μsN to prevent motion, where μs is static friction coefficient and N is normal force. Once motion occurs, kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes sliding (typically μk

topics.name: Couples
topics.description: A couple is a special force system consisting of two parallel forces equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and separated by a perpendicular distance. Couples produce pure rotational effect without resultant force. The couple moment M = F×d is independent of the moment center - a unique property making couples free vectors. Couples cannot be balanced by single forces, only by equal and opposit

topics.name: Distributed forces
topics.description: Distributed forces are loads spread continuously over a line, area, or volume rather than concentrated at points. Common examples include weight of beams, hydrostatic pressure, wind loading, and soil pressure. Characterized by intensity w(x) (force per unit length/area/volume). Total force magnitude found by integration: FR = ∫w dA. Resultant location (centroid of load distribution) determined by:

topics.name: Equilibrium
topics.description: Equilibrium is the state where a rigid body remains at rest (static equilibrium) or moves with constant velocity (dynamic equilibrium). Static equilibrium requires two conditions: force equilibrium (ΣF = 0, preventing translation) and moment equilibrium (ΣM = 0, preventing rotation). In 3D, this yields six scalar equations; in 2D, three equations. Equilibrium principles enable determination of unk

topics.name: Equilibrium analysis
topics.description: Equilibrium analysis is the systematic process of determining unknown forces and reactions in static systems by applying equilibrium equations. The method involves: (1) isolating the body and drawing a complete free-body diagram, (2) selecting appropriate coordinate system, (3) writing equilibrium equations (ΣF=0, ΣM=0), and (4) solving for unknowns. For 2D problems: three independent equations; f

topics.name: Equipollent systems
topics.description: Equipollent force systems are different force arrangements that produce identical external effects on a rigid body. Two systems are equipollent if they have equal resultant forces (ΣF₁ = ΣF₂) and equal resultant moments about any point (ΣM₁ = ΣM₂). While producing the same overall effect, equipollent systems may have different internal stress distributions. This concept allows replacement of compl

topics.name: Forces
topics.description: Forces are fundamental vector quantities in statics representing interactions between bodies. Measured in Newtons (SI) or pounds (Imperial), forces are characterized by magnitude, direction, line of action, and point of application. Types include contact forces (normal, friction, tension, compression) and field forces (gravitational, electromagnetic). In statics, forces on bodies in equilibrium su

topics.name: Free-body diagrams
topics.description: A free-body diagram (FBD) is a schematic representation showing an isolated body with all external forces and moments acting on it. The FBD is created by: (1) selecting the body to analyze, (2) isolating it from all supports and connections, (3) replacing constraints with reaction forces/moments, (4) showing all applied forces including weight, and (5) indicating relevant dimensions and angles. Su

topics.name: Hydrostatic pressure
topics.description: Hydrostatic pressure is force per unit area exerted by fluid at rest, increasing linearly with depth: p = ρgh, where ρ is fluid density, g is gravity, h is depth. Pressure acts perpendicular to surfaces (normal direction), with magnitude depending only on depth, not surface orientation. Total force on submerged surface: F = ∫p dA, acting at center of pressure (below centroid for vertical surfaces)

topics.name: Method of composite parts
topics.description: Method of composite parts determines properties (centroid, area, volume, moment of inertia) of complex shapes by subdividing into simple standard shapes with known properties. Total property equals sum of individual contributions: x̄total = Σx̄ᵢAᵢ / ΣAᵢ for centroids. Negative areas handle cutouts or holes by subtracting their contributions. Standard shapes (rectangles, triangles, circles, semicir

topics.name: Methods of joint and sections
topics.description: Methods of joints and sections are systematic techniques for analyzing truss internal forces. Method of Joints isolates each joint as a free-body diagram with equilibrium equations ΣFₓ=0 and ΣFᵧ=0, solving for member forces sequentially starting where two or fewer unknowns exist. Method of Sections cuts through members of interest, isolating a portion and applying all three equilibrium equations (

topics.name: Moments
topics.description: Moments quantify the rotational effect of forces about points or axes. The moment vector M = r × F represents the tendency of force F to rotate about a point, where r is the position vector from the moment center to the force application point. Moment magnitude M = Fd, where d is the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from the axis to the force line of action. The right-hand rule determines momen

topics.name: Moments of inertia
topics.description: Moment of inertia (second moment of area) I measures how area is distributed relative to an axis, quantifying resistance to bending. For area A about x-axis: Iₓ = ∫y² dA; about y-axis: Iᵧ = ∫x² dA. Units: length⁴ (mm⁴, in⁴). Unlike first moment (linear), second moment gives greater weight to area farther from axis (y² term). Critical for beam bending stress (σ = My/I) and deflection calculations.

topics.name: Multi-force members
topics.description: Multi-force members experience forces at three or more points or directions, resulting in combined axial force, shear force, and bending moment. Unlike two-force members (axial loads only), multi-force members are common in frames, machines, and beams where loads apply at multiple locations. Internal forces vary along member length, requiring consideration of shear and bending in addition to axial

topics.name: Parallel-axis theorem
topics.description: Parallel-axis theorem relates moment of inertia about any axis to moment about parallel centroidal axis: I = Īc + Ad², where Īc is moment about centroidal axis, A is area, d is distance between axes. Always increases I when moving away from centroid - minimum I occurs at centroidal axis. Also applies to polar moments: J = J̄c + Ad². Essential for composite shape analysis and determining I about no

topics.name: Polar moment of inertia
topics.description: Polar moment of inertia J measures area distribution relative to a point (typically centroid), quantifying torsional rigidity. Defined as J = ∫r² dA where r is distance from point to area element. For circular sections, J = Iₓ + Iᵧ (perpendicular axis theorem). Used in torsion analysis: shear stress τ = Tρ/J and angle of twist φ = TL/GJ, where T is torque, G is shear modulus. Circular shafts are e

topics.name: Practical examples
topics.description: Practical examples apply statics principles to real-world engineering problems including structures (bridges, buildings, cranes), machines (levers, pulleys, gears), and everyday objects. These applications demonstrate how theoretical concepts of forces, moments, and equilibrium solve actual design challenges. Examples range from simple scenarios (ladder against wall, hanging sign) to complex syste

topics.name: Product of inertia
topics.description: Product of inertia Iₓᵧ = ∫xy dA measures asymmetry of area distribution relative to xy axes. Unlike moments of inertia (always positive), product of inertia can be positive, negative, or zero. Zero when either axis is symmetry axis. Used in determining principal axes (axes where Iₓᵧ = 0) and analyzing unsymmetric bending. Transformation equations relate Iₓᵧ in different coordinate systems. For sym

topics.name: Radius of gyration
topics.description: Radius of gyration k is the distance from axis where area could be concentrated as a thin line to produce same moment of inertia: I = Ak². Defined as k = √(I/A). Provides intuitive measure of area distribution - larger k means area more spread from axis. For structural members, k relates to slenderness ratio L/k used in buckling analysis. For circular shaft, k = d/4 for bending; k = d/2√2 for tors

topics.name: Resultants
topics.description: A resultant is the single force, or force-couple combination, that replaces a system of forces while producing the same external effect. The force resultant R = ΣF is the vector sum of all forces in the system. The moment resultant about point O is MO = Σ(ri × Fi). For general force systems, reduction to a point yields a force and couple. For special cases (concurrent, parallel, or coplanar forces

topics.name: Review of vector algebra
topics.description: Vector algebra forms the mathematical foundation for statics analysis. Vectors represent physical quantities with both magnitude and direction, such as forces, displacements, and velocities. Essential operations include vector addition (tip-to-tail or parallelogram method), scalar multiplication, dot product (a·b = |a||b|cosθ for projection and work), and cross product (a×b perpendicular to both,

topics.name: Shear-force
topics.description: Shear force V(x) is the internal force perpendicular to a beam's longitudinal axis at any cross-section, representing resistance to transverse loading. Calculated by summing vertical forces on either side of section. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation of element. Shear varies along beam according to applied loads, with relationship dV/dx = -w(x) where w is distributed load i

topics.name: Statics and structural design
topics.description: Statics principles form the foundation for structural design, ensuring buildings, bridges, and machines safely support applied loads. Design process integrates load determination (dead, live, environmental), structural analysis (forces, moments, reactions), stress analysis, and member sizing based on strength requirements with safety factors. Considerations include material properties, failure mod

topics.name: Trusses
topics.description: Trusses are structural frameworks composed of straight members connected at joints, arranged in triangular patterns to efficiently resist loads through axial forces only. Members experience pure tension or compression, with no bending if loads apply only at joints and connections are idealized as frictionless pins. Common truss types include Pratt, Howe, Warren, and K-trusses, each optimized for d

topics.name: Virtual work
topics.description: Virtual work principle states that for system in equilibrium, total virtual work done by all forces through compatible virtual displacements is zero: δW = ΣFᵢ·δrᵢ = 0. Virtual displacement is imaginary, infinitesimal displacement consistent with constraints. Method determines equilibrium configuration or unknown forces without considering internal forces or reactions at ideal constraints. Particul

ECE 205.name: Electrical and Electronic Ckts
ECE 205.description: ECE 205 is an introductory course on circuit analysis and electronics for non-majors in engineering. The course includes bi-weekly electronics lab experiments designed to provide students with hands-on experience. Basic principles of circuit analysis and DC circuits; time-domain analysis of 1st and 2nd order linear circuits; complex numbers, phasors, AC steady-state analysis; frequency response; op-amp, diode, and BJT circuits; logic gates and digital logic circuits. Credit is not given to Computer or Electrical Engineering majors.
objectives: ECE 205 is an introductory course on circuit analysis and electronics for non-majors in engineering.

The goals are to impart the fundamental principles of electric circuits and electronic circuits that constitute the foundation for preparing a non-major to take follow-on courses involving electric and electronic circuits.

topics.name: Ac steady-state analysis and ac steady-state power
topics.description: AC steady-state analysis uses phasors and impedances to find circuit response to sinusoidal sources after transients decay. All voltages and currents are sinusoidal at source frequency. AC power has three forms: real power P = Vrms Irms cos(θ) (average, does work, watts), reactive power Q = Vrms Irms sin(θ) (stored/returned, VARs), and apparent power S = Vrms Irms (total, VA). Power factor cos(θ)

topics.name: Binary logic and logic gates
topics.description: Binary logic uses two states: 0 (low voltage, false) and 1 (high voltage, true). Logic gates implement Boolean operations: NOT (inverter), AND (output 1 only if all inputs 1), OR (output 1 if any input 1), NAND (inverted AND), NOR (inverted OR), XOR (output 1 if inputs differ). Gates combine to create complex digital systems. Truth tables define gate behavior for all input combinations. Logic fami

topics.name: Charge
topics.description: Electric charge (Q) is a fundamental property of matter measured in coulombs (C), existing in discrete units of elementary charge e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. Charges come in two types: positive (protons) and negative (electrons). Like charges repel, opposite charges attract via Coulomb's law. Charge is conserved - total charge in isolated system remains constant. Current is the flow rate of charge: I = d

topics.name: Circuit elements
topics.description: Circuit elements are idealized components with specific voltage-current relationships. Resistors (V = IR) dissipate energy as heat. Capacitors (Q = CV, I = C dV/dt) store energy in electric fields. Inductors (V = L dI/dt) store energy in magnetic fields. Voltage sources maintain constant voltage; current sources maintain constant current. Elements connect at nodes through conductors. Passive eleme

topics.name: Current
topics.description: Electric current (I) is the rate of charge flow through a conductor: I = dQ/dt, measured in amperes (A), where 1 A = 1 C/s. Conventional current direction is positive charge flow (opposite to electron flow). Current can be direct (DC - constant direction) or alternating (AC - periodically reversing). In conductors, current results from drift velocity of charge carriers. Current density J = I/A des

topics.name: Current source comparator
topics.description: A comparator is an op-amp circuit (typically without feedback) that compares two voltages and outputs high or low based on which is larger. Output switches between saturation levels (typically ±Vsat or rail voltages). When V+ > V-, output is high; when V+

topics.name: Equivalence theorems
topics.description: Equivalence theorems replace complex circuit portions with simpler equivalents. Thévenin's theorem: any linear two-terminal network can be replaced by voltage source Vth in series with resistance Rth. Norton's theorem: equivalent current source IN in parallel with RN. These are dual: Vth = IN·RN, Rth = RN. Source transformation converts between voltage and current source models. Maximum power tran

topics.name: Frequency response
topics.description: Frequency response describes how a circuit's output amplitude and phase vary with input signal frequency. Characterized by transfer function H(ω) = Vout/Vin as a function of frequency ω. Magnitude |H(ω)| shows gain at each frequency; phase ∠H(ω) shows time delay. Bode plots (log-log magnitude, semi-log phase) visualize frequency response. Key features include cutoff frequencies, passband, stopband

topics.name: Introduction to bjts
topics.description: Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs) are three-terminal semiconductor devices (emitter, base, collector) that amplify current or act as switches. Two types: NPN (common) and PNP. Small base current IB controls large collector current IC: IC = βIB, where β (gain) typically 50-300. BJT operates in three regions: cutoff (off, no IC), active (amplifier, linear IC-VBE relation), and saturation (on, IC l

topics.name: Kirchhoff's current and voltage laws
topics.description: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): algebraic sum of currents entering any node equals zero (ΣI = 0), reflecting charge conservation. Current in equals current out. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop equals zero (ΣV = 0), reflecting energy conservation. Voltage rises equal voltage drops. These laws, combined with element equations, enable systematic circuit

topics.name: Logic gates using bjts
topics.description: BJTs implement logic gates by operating in cutoff (logic 0) and saturation (logic 1) regions. For NPN BJT: high base voltage drives saturation (output low if common emitter), low base keeps cutoff (output high). RTL (Resistor-Transistor Logic) uses resistors for pull-up/down. DTL (Diode-Transistor Logic) adds diodes for logic functions. TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) is standard BJT logic famil

topics.name: Mesh-current methods
topics.description: The mesh-current method (loop analysis) solves circuits by assigning currents to meshes (fundamental loops) and writing KVL equations. Each mesh current flows around its loop; actual branch currents are algebraic sums of mesh currents. For each mesh, write KVL: sum of voltage drops equals sum of voltage rises. Express voltages using Ohm's law: V = I_mesh R. Produces M equations for M meshes. This

topics.name: Node-voltage
topics.description: The node-voltage method (nodal analysis) solves circuits by writing KCL equations at nodes, expressing currents in terms of node voltages. Choose a reference node (ground, 0V), then unknown node voltages become variables. For each non-reference node, write KCL: sum of currents leaving equals zero. Use Ohm's law to express currents as (Vi - Vj)/R. Produces N-1 equations for N nodes. This systematic

topics.name: Ohm's law
topics.description: Ohm's law states that voltage across a resistor is proportional to current through it: V = IR, where R is resistance in ohms (Ω). This linear relationship holds for ohmic materials at constant temperature. Resistance depends on material (resistivity ρ), length (l), and cross-sectional area (A): R = ρl/A. Conductance G = 1/R is the inverse, measured in siemens (S). Ohm's law is fundamental for circ

topics.name: Op-amp- integrator
topics.description: An op-amp integrator performs mathematical integration of input voltage: Vout = -(1/RC)∫Vin dt. Circuit uses capacitor in feedback path of inverting configuration. Output voltage accumulates (integrates) input over time. At DC (ω = 0), gain is infinite (open loop); at high frequencies, gain decreases as 1/ω. Integrators are used in analog computers, waveform generation (converting square waves to

topics.name: Op-amp - inverting and non-inverting active filter
topics.description: Active filters use operational amplifiers (op-amps) with resistors and capacitors to create filters with gain and sharper roll-offs than passive filters. Inverting configuration: input to inverting terminal, gain = -Zf/Zi. Non-inverting configuration: input to non-inverting terminal, gain = 1 + Zf/Zi. By making impedances frequency-dependent (using capacitors), active filters achieve low-pass, hig

topics.name: Passive filters
topics.description: Passive filters use only resistors, capacitors, and inductors (no active components) to selectively pass or block frequency ranges. Types include low-pass (passes low frequencies), high-pass (passes high frequencies), band-pass (passes middle range), and band-stop (blocks middle range). First-order RC/RL filters have -20 dB/decade roll-off; higher orders provide sharper transitions. Cutoff frequen

topics.name: P-n junction diodes
topics.description: A p-n junction diode is a semiconductor device formed by joining p-type (hole-rich) and n-type (electron-rich) materials. It conducts current easily in forward bias (p-side positive) but blocks in reverse bias (p-side negative). Current-voltage relationship follows Shockley equation: I = Is(e^(V/nVT) - 1), where VT ≈ 26 mV at room temperature. Threshold voltage (≈0.7V for silicon) must be exceeded

topics.name: Power
topics.description: Electric power (P) is the rate of energy transfer or work done: P = dW/dt = VI, measured in watts (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. For resistive elements, P = I²R = V²/R (Joule heating). Power can be delivered (sources like batteries) or dissipated (loads like resistors). Conservation of energy requires total power delivered equals total power dissipated. In AC circuits, distinguish between real power (ave

topics.name: Rc and rl circuits
topics.description: RC circuits contain resistors and capacitors; RL circuits contain resistors and inductors. First-order circuits (one energy storage element) have exponential responses with time constant τ. For RC: τ = RC; capacitor voltage: v(t) = Vf + (Vi - Vf)e^(-t/τ). For RL: τ = L/R; inductor current: i(t) = If + (Ii - If)e^(-t/τ). Time constant represents time to reach 63% of final value. Applications includ

topics.name: Response to sinusoidal inputs
topics.description: Steady-state response to sinusoidal input v(t) = Vm cos(ωt) is sinusoidal at same frequency but different amplitude and phase: vout(t) = Vo cos(ωt + φ). Phasors represent sinusoids as complex numbers: V = Vm∠0°, enabling algebraic instead of differential equation analysis. Impedances generalize resistance: ZR = R, ZL = jωL, ZC = 1/(jωC). All circuit analysis techniques (Ohm's law, KVL, KCL, nodal,

topics.name: Rlc circuits
topics.description: RLC circuits contain resistors, inductors, and capacitors - second-order systems described by second-order differential equations. Series RLC: V = RI + L(dI/dt) + Q/C. Natural frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC), damping ratio ζ = R/(2)√(C/L). Response types: overdamped (ζ > 1, exponential decay), critically damped (ζ = 1, fastest non-oscillatory), underdamped (ζ

topics.name: Sinusoidal excitation and phasors
topics.description: Phasor representation transforms sinusoidal time-domain signals v(t) = Vm cos(ωt + φ) to complex frequency-domain: V = Vm∠φ = Vm e^(jφ). Euler's formula: e^(jωt) = cos(ωt) + j sin(ωt) enables this transformation. Phasors convert differential equations to algebraic equations using complex impedances. Time derivatives become jω multiplication: d/dt → jω. Phasor diagrams visualize amplitude and phase

topics.name: Step response
topics.description: Step response is circuit behavior when input suddenly changes (typically from 0 to constant value - step function). Represents switching events like closing switch or abrupt voltage change. For first-order circuits, response is exponential approach to new steady state: v(t) = Vfinal(1 - e^(-t/τ)) for initially relaxed RC. Rise time, settling time, and time constant characterize response speed. Ste

topics.name: Superposition
topics.description: The superposition theorem states that in linear circuits with multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) equals the sum of responses from each source acting alone with others deactivated (voltage sources shorted, current sources opened). This powerful technique simplifies analysis of complex circuits by decomposing into simpler single-source problems. Only applies to linear el

topics.name: Time domain analysis
topics.description: Time-domain analysis examines circuit behavior as functions of time t. Involves solving differential equations arising from capacitor (I = C dV/dt) and inductor (V = L dI/dt) relations. Response components: natural response (transient, source-free, decays), forced response (steady-state due to sources), and complete response (sum). Initial conditions from energy storage determine natural response

topics.name: Voltage
topics.description: Voltage (V) or electric potential difference is the energy per unit charge required to move charge between two points: V = W/Q, measured in volts (V), where 1 V = 1 J/C. Voltage represents electrical 'pressure' driving current flow. It's the work done by electric field per charge moved. Voltage is measured between two points (potential difference), with higher potential being positive terminal. Vo

topics.name: Voltage and current divisions
topics.description: Voltage divider: for series resistors, voltage divides proportionally: Vₓ = (Rₓ/(R₁+R₂+...))Vtotal. Higher resistance gets more voltage. Current divider: for parallel resistors, current divides inversely proportional to resistance: Iₓ = (Gtotal/Gₓ)Itotal, where G = 1/R. Lower resistance gets more current. These rules provide shortcuts for calculating voltages and currents without full circuit anal

MATH 285.name: Intro to Differential Equations
MATH 285.description: Techniques and applications of ordinary differential equations, including Fourier series and boundary value problems, linear systems of differential equations, and an introduction to partial differential equations. Covers all the topics of MATH 285 plus linear systems. Intended for engineering majors and other who require a working knowledge of differential equations.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of differential equations to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using differential equations.

topics.name: Annihilator method
topics.description: The annihilator method finds particular solutions by applying a differential operator that 'annihilates' (maps to zero) the forcing function, converting the inhomogeneous equation into a higher-order homogeneous equation. Solving this larger homogeneous equation and extracting the particular solution from terms not in the complementary solution provides the answer. This method is more systematic t

topics.name: Boundary value problems
topics.description: Boundary value problems specify conditions at multiple points (typically endpoints) of the domain rather than at a single initial point. Unlike initial value problems where time evolution proceeds forward, boundary value problems constrain solutions at boundaries, requiring different solution approaches. These problems arise in steady-state phenomena, structural analysis, and quantum mechanics whe

topics.name: Constant coefficient linear equations
topics.description: Constant coefficient linear differential equations have constant coefficients multiplying each derivative term, making them particularly tractable. The characteristic equation method converts the differential equation into an algebraic equation whose roots determine the solution structure. Solutions involve exponentials, sines, and cosines depending on whether roots are real, repeated, or complex.

topics.name: Constant coefficient linear systems
topics.description: Constant coefficient linear systems are systems of differential equations where the coefficient matrix has constant entries. Solutions are found using eigenvalue-eigenvector analysis: eigenvalues determine exponential growth rates while eigenvectors determine solution directions. The general solution combines these exponential modes. For complex or repeated eigenvalues, solutions involve complex e

topics.name: Damped vibrations
topics.description: Damped vibrations incorporate energy dissipation into oscillatory systems through a damping term proportional to velocity. Depending on damping strength, systems exhibit underdamped (oscillatory decay), critically damped (fastest return without oscillation), or overdamped (slow non-oscillatory return) behavior. These equations model real physical systems like shock absorbers, RLC circuits, and str

topics.name: Eigenvalue problems
topics.description: Eigenvalue problems in differential equations seek functions satisfying a differential operator equation where the function equals a scalar multiple (eigenvalue) of itself, subject to boundary conditions. These problems determine special values (eigenvalues) for which non-trivial solutions (eigenfunctions) exist. Eigenvalue problems are fundamental to vibration analysis, quantum mechanics, and par

topics.name: Eigenvalues
topics.description: Eigenvalues are special scalars associated with a matrix that indicate the factor by which eigenvectors are scaled during matrix transformation. Finding eigenvalues involves solving the characteristic equation derived from det(A - λI) = 0. In differential equations, eigenvalues determine solution behavior in systems of equations: real negative eigenvalues indicate stability, positive indicate inst

topics.name: Electric circuits
topics.description: Electric circuit analysis using differential equations models the behavior of RLC circuits containing resistors, inductors, and capacitors. Kirchhoff's laws lead to differential equations where current and charge are the dependent variables. The mathematical structure parallels mechanical vibration problems, with inductance analogous to mass, resistance to damping, and capacitance to spring compli

topics.name: Even and odd extensions
topics.description: Even and odd extensions artificially extend a function defined on a half-interval to the full interval by creating either even (symmetric) or odd (antisymmetric) versions. This technique allows use of Fourier cosine series (even extension) or sine series (odd extension) to solve problems on half-intervals. The choice of extension determines which type of Fourier series applies and must be consiste

topics.name: Exact equations
topics.description: An exact differential equation is one where the left side is the exact differential of some function, meaning it comes from taking the total derivative of a potential function. If an equation is exact, the solution can be found by integrating to recover the potential function and setting it equal to a constant. Testing for exactness involves checking if mixed partial derivatives are equal, reflect

topics.name: Existence and uniqueness
topics.description: Existence and uniqueness theorems provide conditions guaranteeing that a differential equation with an initial condition has a solution (existence) and that this solution is unique (uniqueness). Typically, if the function and its partial derivatives are continuous in a region containing the initial condition, then a unique solution exists locally. These theorems are fundamental to ensuring differe

topics.name: Fourier cosine series
topics.description: Fourier cosine series represent even functions on an interval using an infinite sum of cosine functions with integer multiples of a fundamental frequency, plus a constant term. Coefficients are determined by projecting the function onto cosine basis functions. This representation is essential for solving partial differential equations with symmetric boundary conditions, such as heat flow in insula

topics.name: Fourier series
topics.description: Fourier series represent periodic functions as infinite sums of sines and cosines with frequencies that are integer multiples of a fundamental frequency. This powerful decomposition allows any reasonable periodic function to be expressed in terms of simple harmonic components. Fourier series are foundational in solving partial differential equations, signal processing, and analyzing any phenomenon

topics.name: Fourier sine series
topics.description: Fourier sine series represent odd functions on an interval using an infinite sum of sine functions with integer multiples of a fundamental frequency. Each coefficient is computed by projecting the function onto the corresponding sine basis function. This representation is crucial for solving partial differential equations with specific boundary conditions, particularly when the function value is z

topics.name: Heat equation
topics.description: The heat equation is a partial differential equation describing how temperature distributes and evolves over time in a medium due to thermal conduction. It is parabolic, showing that heat diffuses smoothly from hot to cold regions. Solutions typically use separation of variables combined with Fourier series, yielding insights into transient heat flow and steady-state temperature distributions in r

topics.name: Higher order linear equations
topics.description: Higher-order linear differential equations involve derivatives of order two or higher, with the unknown function and its derivatives appearing linearly. These equations model phenomena like mechanical vibrations, electrical circuits, and wave propagation. The general solution combines the complementary (homogeneous) solution with a particular solution. Understanding their structure, including line

topics.name: Inhomogeneous equations
topics.description: Inhomogeneous (or non-homogeneous) differential equations include a forcing term that is not identically zero, representing external inputs or sources in the system. The general solution is the sum of the complementary solution (solving the associated homogeneous equation) and any particular solution to the inhomogeneous equation. This structure reflects the physical principle that the total respo

topics.name: Inhomogeneous heat equation
topics.description: The inhomogeneous heat equation includes a source term representing internal heat generation or external heat input, not just conduction from boundaries. This makes the equation more realistic for situations with heaters, chemical reactions, or radiation absorption. Solutions combine the homogeneous heat equation solution with a particular solution accounting for the source term, often using eigen

topics.name: Integrating factors
topics.description: An integrating factor is a function that, when multiplied by a non-exact first-order linear differential equation, transforms it into an exact equation that can be integrated directly. The method systematically finds this factor, typically an exponential function, making the equation solvable through straightforward integration. This powerful technique extends the range of solvable first-order equ

topics.name: Interactive graph: laplace equation
topics.description: The Laplace equation is a partial differential equation describing steady-state phenomena where there are no sources or sinks, such as electrostatic potential or steady-state heat distribution. Interactive visualizations show how solutions satisfy the equation with specified boundary values, demonstrating the maximum principle and harmonic functions. Students can modify boundary conditions to see

topics.name: Interactive graph: standing waves
topics.description: Standing waves are wave patterns that appear stationary, formed by the superposition of two waves traveling in opposite directions with the same frequency and amplitude. In interactive visualizations, students can manipulate parameters like frequency, boundary conditions, and initial conditions to observe how nodes (points of zero amplitude) and antinodes (points of maximum amplitude) form. This t

topics.name: Interactive graph: traveling wave
topics.description: Traveling waves are wave patterns that propagate through space carrying energy from one location to another at constant velocity. Interactive visualizations allow students to adjust parameters like wave speed, amplitude, frequency, and initial disturbances to see how waves move, reflect at boundaries, and interact. This helps understand the d'Alembert solution to the wave equation and the relation

topics.name: Laplace transform
topics.description: The Laplace transform converts differential equations in the time domain into algebraic equations in the frequency domain, making them easier to solve. After solving algebraically, the inverse Laplace transform returns the solution to the time domain. This method excels at handling initial value problems, discontinuous forcing functions, and systems with multiple interconnected equations. It's fun

topics.name: Matrices and determinants
topics.description: Matrices and determinants are fundamental algebraic structures for solving systems of differential equations. Matrices represent linear transformations and systems of equations compactly, while determinants provide information about matrix invertibility, solution existence, and geometric scaling. Understanding matrix operations, properties, and determinant calculation is essential for analyzing sy

topics.name: Matrix exponential
topics.description: The matrix exponential extends the exponential function to matrices, defined through its power series or through eigenvalue decomposition. For a system of linear differential equations dx/dt = Ax, the solution is x(t) = exp(At)x(0), making the matrix exponential fundamental to solving constant coefficient systems. Computing matrix exponentials requires techniques involving eigenvalues, eigenvector

topics.name: Numerical methods
topics.description: Numerical methods for differential equations provide approximate solutions when analytical methods fail or are impractical. Techniques like Euler's method, Runge-Kutta methods, and multistep methods generate discrete approximations by stepping through the domain using the differential equation to predict function values. These methods are essential in practice since most real-world differential eq

topics.name: Putzer's algorithm
topics.description: Putzer's algorithm provides a systematic computational method for calculating matrix exponentials without requiring diagonalization. The algorithm works for all matrices, including defective ones with incomplete eigenvector sets. It constructs the matrix exponential using the characteristic polynomial and a sequence of matrices built iteratively. This makes it particularly valuable when standard e

topics.name: Row operation
topics.description: Row operations are elementary manipulations of matrix rows used to solve systems of linear equations through Gaussian elimination and to compute determinants, inverses, and reduced forms. The three types—row swapping, row multiplication, and row addition—transform matrices into simpler forms while preserving solution sets. These operations are fundamental computational tools for analyzing systems

topics.name: Separable equations
topics.description: Separable differential equations are first-order equations that can be rewritten so all terms involving the dependent variable are on one side and all terms involving the independent variable are on the other side. The solution is found by integrating both sides separately. This is one of the simplest and most fundamental techniques for solving differential equations analytically and applies to ma

topics.name: Slope fields
topics.description: Slope fields (or direction fields) are visual tools for understanding first-order differential equations without solving them explicitly. At each point in the plane, a small line segment is drawn with slope equal to the differential equation's value at that point. These segments show the direction solution curves must follow, allowing qualitative analysis of solution behavior, stability, and long-

topics.name: System of ordinary differential equations
topics.description: Systems of ordinary differential equations involve multiple interrelated differential equations with multiple dependent variables that evolve simultaneously. These systems model coupled processes where variables influence each other, such as predator-prey populations, chemical reactions, or interconnected mechanical systems. Solution methods include matrix techniques, eigenvalue analysis, and phas

topics.name: The wave equation
topics.description: The wave equation is a hyperbolic partial differential equation describing oscillatory phenomena that propagate through space at finite speed. It models vibrating strings, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, and seismic waves. Solutions represent traveling or standing waves and typically involve superposition of sinusoidal modes. The wave equation exhibits characteristic properties like reflection

topics.name: Undamped vibrations
topics.description: Undamped vibrations model oscillatory systems with no energy dissipation, described by second-order differential equations with constant coefficients and no damping term. Solutions are purely sinusoidal, representing perpetual oscillation at the system's natural frequency. Understanding undamped vibrations provides the foundation for analyzing real systems with damping and forced oscillations, suc

topics.name: Undetermined coefficients
topics.description: The method of undetermined coefficients finds particular solutions to inhomogeneous linear differential equations with constant coefficients when the forcing function has a special form (polynomials, exponentials, sines, cosines). The method guesses a solution form with unknown coefficients, substitutes into the equation, and solves for these coefficients. This technique is efficient and practical

topics.name: Variation of parameters
topics.description: Variation of parameters is a general method for finding particular solutions to inhomogeneous linear differential equations that works even when the forcing function doesn't have a special form. Instead of assuming the parameters in the complementary solution are constants, this method treats them as unknown functions and derives equations to determine them. While computationally intensive, it is

topics.name: Variation of vector parameters
topics.description: Variation of vector parameters extends the variation of parameters method to systems of differential equations. Instead of varying scalar parameters, this method treats the coefficient vector in the complementary solution as an unknown vector function. By determining this vector through a system derived from substitution, particular solutions to inhomogeneous systems can be found systematically, e

ME 200.name: Thermodynamics
ME 200.description: Introduction to classical thermodynamics through the second law; system and control volume analyses of thermodynamic processes; irreversibility and availability; relations for ideal gas mixtures.
ME 200.instructors: Tang, K, He, J, Bradshaw, C
objectives: An ability to determine properties and thermodynamic states.
An ability to analyze mass movements and energy flow/conversions.
An ability to understand thermodynamic performance limits of cycles and devices.
An ability to determine and understand sources of irreversibility, and methods for reducing irreversibilities.
An ability to apply thermodynamic analysis tools to understanding and evaluating emerging power cycles and devices against their traditional counterparts.

topics.name: Availability
topics.description: Availability (also called exergy or available energy) represents the maximum useful work obtainable from a system as it comes to equilibrium with a reference environment through reversible processes. Unlike energy, which is conserved, availability can be destroyed through irreversibilities. Availability combines first and second law considerations, accounting for both energy quantity and quality.

topics.name: Component efficiency
topics.description: Component efficiency measures how well individual devices (turbines, compressors, pumps, nozzles) perform compared to ideal, reversible operation. Isentropic efficiency compares actual work or enthalpy change to the isentropic (constant entropy) ideal case. For turbines and nozzles, efficiency is actual output divided by ideal output; for compressors and pumps, it's ideal input divided by actual i

topics.name: Compressibility charts
topics.description: Compressibility charts (or generalized compressibility charts) are graphical tools that show the compressibility factor (Z = PV/nRT) as a function of reduced pressure and reduced temperature for real gases. These charts allow engineers to account for deviations from ideal gas behavior without using complex equations of state. By using the principle of corresponding states, a single chart can be ap

topics.name: Cycle efficiency
topics.description: Cycle efficiency quantifies the performance of thermodynamic cycles (power or refrigeration) by comparing desired output to required input. For power cycles, thermal efficiency is net work output divided by heat input. For refrigeration and heat pump cycles, the coefficient of performance (COP) is cooling or heating effect divided by work input. Carnot efficiency represents the theoretical maximum

topics.name: Definitions
topics.description: Fundamental terminology and concepts forming the foundation of thermodynamics including system (region of interest separated from surroundings by boundary), state (condition described by properties), process (path of state changes), property (macroscopic characteristic like temperature, pressure, volume), extensive properties (proportional to mass), intensive properties (independent of mass), equi

topics.name: Entropy
topics.description: Entropy is a thermodynamic property that quantifies the degree of disorder or randomness in a system, and serves as a measure of energy unavailability for doing work. In classical thermodynamics, entropy changes are calculated from heat transfer divided by absolute temperature for reversible processes. Entropy always increases in isolated systems undergoing irreversible processes (second law), mak

topics.name: Equations of state
topics.description: Equations of state are mathematical relationships that describe the interdependence of thermodynamic properties such as pressure, temperature, volume, and internal energy for a substance. In thermodynamics courses, students learn various equations including the ideal gas law (PV=nRT), van der Waals equation, and other real gas models. These equations serve as the foundation for analyzing thermodyn

topics.name: First law of thermodynamics for a control mass (closed system)
topics.description: The first law of thermodynamics for a closed system (fixed mass) is a statement of energy conservation: the change in total energy of the system equals the net energy transfer as heat and work. Mathematically expressed as ΔE = Q - W, where E is total energy (internal, kinetic, potential), Q is heat transferred to the system, and W is work done by the system. This fundamental principle allows engin

topics.name: First law of thermodynamics for a control volume (open system)
topics.description: The first law for a control volume (open system) extends energy conservation to systems where mass flows across boundaries, such as turbines, pumps, heat exchangers, and nozzles. It accounts for energy transfer via heat and work, plus energy carried by mass flow (including enthalpy, kinetic energy, and potential energy). The steady-flow energy equation, a common form, balances energy rates for sys

topics.name: Heat transfer
topics.description: Heat transfer in thermodynamics refers to energy transfer driven by temperature differences between a system and its surroundings. Unlike work, heat is also a path function and is defined only during a process, not as a property of a state. Heat flows naturally from higher to lower temperature regions and can occur through conduction, convection, or radiation. In thermodynamic analysis, heat trans

topics.name: Irreversibility
topics.description: Irreversibility quantifies the destruction of available work potential (exergy) due to real process imperfections such as friction, unrestrained expansion, mixing, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, and electrical resistance. Mathematically, irreversibility equals the difference between reversible and actual work, or equivalently, the ambient temperature multiplied by total entro

topics.name: Power and refrigeration cycles
topics.description: Power cycles (Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel) convert thermal energy from fuel combustion or heat sources into mechanical work through cyclic processes. Refrigeration cycles (vapor-compression, absorption) transfer heat from low to high temperature regions using work input, enabling cooling and air conditioning. Both involve working fluids undergoing phase changes or temperature/pressure variation

topics.name: Properties
topics.description: Measurable characteristics describing thermodynamic state including primitive properties (pressure, temperature, volume, mass, composition) and derived properties (density, specific volume, internal energy, enthalpy, entropy). Properties are either intensive (temperature, pressure, independent of system size) or extensive (volume, energy, proportional to mass). State postulate requires minimum num

topics.name: Properties and thermodynamics of ideal gas mixtures
topics.description: Ideal gas mixtures extend single-component ideal gas behavior to multiple non-reacting gases occupying the same volume. Key concepts include Dalton's law (total pressure equals sum of partial pressures), Amagat's law (total volume equals sum of partial volumes), and mixture properties determined by mass or mole fractions. Each component behaves independently as an ideal gas at the mixture temperat

topics.name: Reversible work
topics.description: Reversible work represents the maximum useful work obtainable from a system undergoing a process between two states, or the minimum work required to drive a process. It assumes the process occurs infinitely slowly through equilibrium states with no dissipative effects like friction or unrestrained expansion. Reversible work serves as the ideal benchmark for evaluating real process performance. The

topics.name: Second law of thermodynamics
topics.description: The second law of thermodynamics establishes the direction of natural processes and defines the concept of entropy. It can be stated in multiple equivalent forms: entropy of an isolated system increases in any spontaneous process; heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot; no heat engine can have 100% efficiency; and it is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces n

topics.name: State postulate
topics.description: The state postulate is a fundamental principle in thermodynamics that establishes how many independent intensive properties are needed to completely define the thermodynamic state of a simple compressible system. It states that for a pure substance in equilibrium, specifying two independent intensive properties (such as temperature and pressure) is sufficient to determine all other thermodynamic p

topics.name: Steady state and unsteady analysis
topics.description: Steady-state analysis assumes that all properties at any point within a control volume remain constant with time, though properties may vary from point to point. Mass, energy, and other quantities entering equal those leaving. Unsteady (or transient) analysis applies when properties change with time, such as during startup, shutdown, or charging processes. Engineers must choose the appropriate ana

topics.name: Work
topics.description: In thermodynamics, work is energy transfer that occurs when a system exerts a force through a distance on its surroundings, or vice versa. Work is a path function, meaning its value depends on the process taken between initial and final states. Common forms include boundary work (P-dV work), shaft work, electrical work, and flow work. Understanding work is essential for analyzing engines, compress

PHYS 213.name: Univ Physics: Thermal Physics
PHYS 213.description: First and second laws of thermodynamics including kinetic theory of gases, heat capacity, heat engines, introduction to entropy and statistical mechanics, and introduction to application of free energy and Boltzmann factor. A calculus-based approach for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics and chemistry
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of thermal physics and apply them to solve a wide array of relevant problems.

To achieve these goals several reinforcing activities are used: (1) interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles; (2) laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles; (3) online homework to engage students in problem solving; (4) interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills.

topics.name: Application to semiconductors
topics.description: In semiconductors, the Boltzmann distribution governs electron excitation from the valence band to conduction band across the band gap Eg. Carrier concentration depends exponentially on temperature: n ∝ e^(-Eg/2kT). At room temperature, only a small fraction of electrons are thermally excited, giving intrinsic semiconductors their properties. Doping introduces impurity levels that modify carrier c

topics.name: Boltzmann examples
topics.description: Applications of Boltzmann statistics include: (1) Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution describing gas molecule speeds, (2) barometric formula for atmospheric pressure P(h) ∝ e^(-mgh/kT), (3) population of atomic energy levels explaining spectral line intensities, (4) Arrhenius equation for reaction rates k ∝ e^(-Ea/kT), and (5) thermal excitation across energy gaps in semiconductors. These exam

topics.name: Chemical potential and phases
topics.description: Chemical potential (μ) is the change in free energy per particle added: μ = (∂G/∂N). It represents the 'drive' for particles to move between phases or regions. At equilibrium between phases (solid-liquid-gas), chemical potentials must be equal: μₛₒₗᵢ𝒹 = μₗᵢᵩᵤᵢ𝒹 = μ_gas. Differences in chemical potential drive phase transitions, diffusion, and chemical reactions. Temperature and pressure changes af

topics.name: Entropy
topics.description: Entropy (S) is a measure of a system's disorder or the number of microscopic states consistent with its macroscopic properties. The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy of an isolated system never decreases. For reversible processes, dS = dQ/T. Entropy quantifies energy dispersal and determines process spontaneity - systems naturally evolve toward higher entropy. In statistical mechani

topics.name: Gibbs free energy: equilibrium in contact with a bath
topics.description: Gibbs free energy G = H - TS (where H is enthalpy) determines spontaneity for processes at constant temperature and pressure. A system minimizes G at equilibrium when in contact with a thermal bath (reservoir). Processes with ΔG
