topics.name: Heat capacity
topics.description: Heat capacity (C) is the amount of heat required to raise a system's temperature by one degree: C = Q/ΔT. Specific heat capacity (c) is per unit mass: c = C/m. The heat capacity depends on the process: Cₚ (constant pressure) differs from Cᵥ (constant volume) because expansion work is involved. For ideal gases, Cₚ - Cᵥ = nR. Heat capacity reflects how much energy a substance can store and varies wi

topics.name: Internal energy
topics.description: Internal energy (U) is the total microscopic energy of a system, including kinetic energy of molecular motion and potential energy from intermolecular forces. It is a state function depending only on the system's current state, not how it arrived there. The first law of thermodynamics relates internal energy change to heat and work: ΔU = Q - W. For ideal gases, internal energy depends only on temp

topics.name: Kinetic theory of the ideal gas
topics.description: Kinetic theory explains macroscopic gas properties through microscopic molecular motion. Assumptions include: molecules are point particles in random motion, collisions are perfectly elastic, and intermolecular forces are negligible except during collisions. The theory derives the ideal gas law PV = NkT from mechanics and relates pressure to molecular collisions: P = (1/3)nmv̄². Average kinetic en

topics.name: Microscopic probabilities: boltzmann
topics.description: The Boltzmann distribution describes probability that a system in thermal equilibrium occupies a state with energy E: P(E) ∝ e^(-E/kT), where k is Boltzmann's constant. This exponential decay means higher energy states are exponentially less likely. The distribution connects microscopic quantum states to macroscopic thermodynamic properties. The partition function Z = Σe^(-Eᵢ/kT) normalizes probab

topics.name: Phase boundaries
topics.description: Phase boundaries are curves on phase diagrams separating regions of different phase stability. Along a boundary, two phases coexist in equilibrium with equal chemical potentials and temperatures. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation describes boundary slopes: dP/dT = ΔS/ΔV = L/(TΔV), where L is latent heat. Crossing a boundary requires adding/removing latent heat. Boundary slopes differ: solid-liquid i

topics.name: Phase diagrams
topics.description: Phase diagrams plot regions of thermodynamic stability for different phases (solid, liquid, gas) as functions of temperature and pressure. Boundary lines represent coexistence conditions where two phases are in equilibrium. The triple point is where all three phases coexist. The critical point marks the end of the liquid-gas boundary, beyond which the distinction disappears. Phase diagrams summari

topics.name: Quasistatic processes
topics.description: A quasistatic process proceeds infinitely slowly, allowing the system to remain in thermal and mechanical equilibrium throughout. Each intermediate state is an equilibrium state, making the process reversible in principle. Quasistatic processes can be represented as smooth curves on thermodynamic diagrams (P-V, T-S). Examples include slow compression with movable piston or gradual heating with inf

topics.name: Temperature
topics.description: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a system and determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow (from hot to cold). It is measured in Kelvin (absolute scale), Celsius, or Fahrenheit. Temperature defines thermal equilibrium - two systems at the same temperature exchange no net heat (zeroth law of thermodynamics). At the microscopic level, temperature relates to

topics.name: Thermodynamic cycles
topics.description: A thermodynamic cycle is a sequence of processes that returns a system to its initial state, with no net change in state functions (ΔU = 0, ΔS = 0 for reversible cycles). Heat engines (Carnot, Otto, Diesel) convert heat to work through cycles, with efficiency η = W/Qₕ limited by the Carnot efficiency ηc = 1 - Tc/Tₕ. Refrigerators and heat pumps run cycles in reverse. Cycles are represented on P-V

PHYS 214.name: Univ Physics: Quantum Physics
PHYS 214.description: Interference and diffraction, photons and matter waves, the Bohr atom, uncertainty principle, and wave mechanics. A calculus-based course for majors in engineering, mathematics, physics, and chemistry.
objectives: To provide an instructional environment in which students can learn the basic concepts of waves and quantum physics and apply them to solve a wide array of relevant problems.

To achieve these goals several reinforcing activities are used: (1) interactive lectures to present, discuss and demonstrate the principles; (2) laboratory experiments to actively explore these principles; (3) online homework to engage students in problem solving; (4) interaction with instructors and students in discussion sections to improve conceptual understanding and to develop problem solving skills.

topics.name: Band structure
topics.description: In crystalline solids, periodic atomic potential creates allowed energy bands separated by forbidden gaps. Valence band (filled) and conduction band (empty/partially filled) determine electrical properties. Metals have overlapping bands, insulators have large gaps (Eg > 3 eV), semiconductors have moderate gaps (~1 eV). Band structure arises from constructive/destructive interference of electron wa

topics.name: Complex numbers
topics.description: Complex numbers z = a + bi (where i² = -1) are essential for quantum mechanics because wave functions are complex-valued. Key operations: addition, multiplication, complex conjugate z* = a - bi, and magnitude |z| = √(a² + b²). Euler's formula e^(iθ) = cos θ + i sin θ connects exponentials to oscillations. In quantum mechanics, complex phases encode interference, and physical observables come from

topics.name: Diffraction
topics.description: Diffraction is wave bending around obstacles and spreading through apertures, most pronounced when obstacle/aperture size is comparable to wavelength. Single-slit diffraction produces a central bright fringe with diminishing side fringes, with minima at a sin θ = nλ. Diffraction gratings with many slits produce sharp interference maxima used in spectroscopy. Diffraction limits optical resolution t

topics.name: Energy eigenstates
topics.description: Energy eigenstates (stationary states) are solutions to the time-independent Schrödinger equation Ĥψₙ = Eₙψₙ, where Ĥ is the Hamiltonian operator. These states have definite energy Eₙ and time evolution ψₙ(t) = ψₙ(0)e^(-iEₙt/ℏ). Probability density |ψₙ|² is time-independent (hence 'stationary'). General states are superpositions of energy eigenstates. Energy quantization emerges from boundary cond

topics.name: Harmonic oscillator
topics.description: The quantum harmonic oscillator models a particle in parabolic potential V(x) = ½kx² with equally-spaced energy levels Eₙ = ℏω(n + ½), where ω = √(k/m) and n = 0, 1, 2, ... The ground state has non-zero energy E₀ = ½ℏω (zero-point energy). Eigenstates are described by Hermite polynomials. The harmonic oscillator is exactly solvable and models molecular vibrations, phonons in solids, and quantum fi

topics.name: Interference
topics.description: Interference occurs when two or more waves overlap, combining according to superposition principle. Constructive interference (waves in phase) produces larger amplitude; destructive interference (waves out of phase) produces cancellation. For two coherent sources with path difference Δr, constructive interference occurs when Δr = nλ, destructive when Δr = (n+1/2)λ. Young's double-slit experiment d

topics.name: Molecules
topics.description: Molecules are bound systems of multiple atoms held together by quantum mechanical electron sharing (covalent bonding) or charge transfer (ionic bonding). Born-Oppenheimer approximation separates nuclear and electronic motion due to mass difference. Molecular orbitals form from atomic orbital combinations (LCAO - linear combination of atomic orbitals). Bonding orbitals (lower energy) and antibondin

topics.name: Momentum
topics.description: In quantum mechanics, momentum is represented by the operator p̂ = -iℏ(∂/∂x), where ℏ = h/2π. Momentum eigenfunctions are plane waves e^(ikx) with eigenvalues p = ℏk (de Broglie relation). Expectation value ⟨p⟩ = ∫ψ*(-iℏ∂ψ/∂x)dx gives average momentum. Heisenberg uncertainty principle Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2 states position and momentum cannot both be precisely determined. Momentum operator generates spatial

topics.name: Multiple electrons
topics.description: Systems with multiple electrons require considering indistinguishability, Pauli exclusion principle (no two electrons in identical quantum states), and antisymmetric wave functions. Total wave function must change sign under electron exchange: ψ(r₁,r₂) = -ψ(r₂,r₁). Electron spin (intrinsic angular momentum with s = ½) means each orbital holds two electrons (spin up/down). Electron-electron repulsi

topics.name: Photons
topics.description: Photons are discrete packets (quanta) of electromagnetic radiation with energy E = hf and momentum p = h/λ, where h is Planck's constant. Einstein's photoelectric effect explanation required photon concept - light comes in particles, not just waves. Photons are massless, travel at speed c, and exhibit both particle and wave properties (wave-particle duality). Photon absorption and emission by atom

topics.name: Polarization and spin
topics.description: Polarization describes orientation of electromagnetic wave oscillations (light). Linear, circular, and elliptical polarizations exist. Quantum mechanically, photons have spin ±1 (helicity), corresponding to right/left circular polarization. Electrons have intrinsic spin s = ½ with two states (spin up/down), measurable along any axis. Spin is purely quantum - no classical analog. Stern-Gerlach expe

topics.name: Position
topics.description: In quantum mechanics, position is represented by the operator x̂ that multiplies the wave function by x. Position eigenfunctions are delta functions δ(x - x₀) representing particles precisely at x₀. Position expectation value ⟨x⟩ = ∫x|ψ(x)|²dx gives average measured position. Position representation uses wave functions ψ(x); momentum representation uses Fourier transforms ψ̃(p). The position-momen

topics.name: Probability
topics.description: In quantum mechanics, probability plays a fundamental role - measurements yield probabilistic outcomes rather than deterministic predictions. The wave function ψ(x,t) contains all information about a system, with |ψ(x,t)|² giving probability density of finding a particle at position x. Total probability integrates to unity (normalization). Unlike classical probability from ignorance, quantum proba

topics.name: Superconductors
topics.description: Superconductors exhibit zero electrical resistance and perfect diamagnetism (Meissner effect - expelling magnetic fields) below critical temperature Tc. Cooper pairs - bound electron pairs mediated by lattice vibrations (phonons) - form a macroscopic quantum state described by a single wave function. BCS theory explains conventional superconductivity. Type I superconductors completely expel fields

topics.name: The wave function
topics.description: The wave function ψ(x,t) is a complex-valued function containing complete information about a quantum system's state. Born's interpretation: |ψ(x,t)|² is the probability density of measuring position x at time t. Wave functions must be normalized (∫|ψ|²dx = 1), single-valued, and continuous. The Schrödinger equation governs wave function evolution. Measuring observables 'collapses' the wave functi

topics.name: Wave equations
topics.description: The wave equation ∂²ψ/∂t² = v²∂²ψ/∂x² describes wave propagation, relating temporal evolution to spatial curvature. Solutions include traveling waves ψ(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt) and standing waves. The dispersion relation ω = vk connects frequency to wavelength. In quantum mechanics, the Schrödinger equation is the wave equation for matter waves, with complex wave functions. Understanding classical wa

topics.name: Waves
topics.description: Waves are disturbances that propagate through space and time, transferring energy without net transport of matter. Characterized by wavelength λ (spatial period), frequency f (temporal oscillation rate), amplitude A (maximum displacement), and speed v = fλ. Waves can be transverse (displacement perpendicular to propagation) or longitudinal (displacement parallel). Wave phenomena include superposit

TAM 212.name: Introductory Dynamics
TAM 212.description: Kinematics and dynamics of the three-dimensional motion of particles; kinematics and dynamics of the plane motion of rigid bodies; methods of work energy and impulse momentum; moving reference frames.
objectives: Be able to draw free-body diagrams, especially for moving bodies.
Be able to calculate centers of mass and moments and products of inertia of solid bodies.
Be able to determine analytically the (Cartesian coordinate) trajectory of particles and mass centers subjected to specified time-, velocity-, and position-dependent forces.
Be able to calculate the vector velocity and acceleration of a particle whose motion is described in cylindrical, polar, Cartesian, or path variables.
Be able to set up and solve for the forces acting on an accelerating particle or rigid body.
Be able to identify and use the principles of work-energy and energy conservation to calculate the motion of particles and rigid bodies.
Be able to identify and use the principles of (angular) impulse-momentum and (angular) momentum conservation to calculate the motion of particles and rigid bodies.
Be able to analyze collisions, and determine subsequent motion, using principles of momentum and angular momentum conservation.
Be able to apply the relationships between the velocities and accelerations of points of a rigid body in plane motion.
Be able to apply the relations between the velocities and accelerations of points that may be moving relative to a rigid body in plane motion.

topics.name: Dynamics of 2d motion of rigid bodies
topics.description: Rigid body dynamics in 2D applies Newton's second law for translation (ΣF = macm) and rotation (ΣMG = IGα), where ΣF is net force, macm is center of mass acceleration, ΣMG is net moment about center of mass, IG is mass moment of inertia, α is angular acceleration. These equations are independent and solved simultaneously. Mass moment of inertia I = ∫r²dm depends on mass distribution. Common proble

topics.name: Dynamics of particles
topics.description: Particle dynamics relates forces to motion through Newton's second law: ΣF = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, a is acceleration. Analysis determines motion from known forces (kinetics) or forces from known motion (inverse dynamics). Free-body diagrams identify all forces; kinematic equations relate motion quantities. Common forces include gravity, friction, normal forces, tension, and applied

topics.name: Impulse-momentum
topics.description: Impulse-momentum principle relates force impulse to momentum change. Linear impulse: ∫F dt = Δ(mv) states that impulse equals momentum change. Angular impulse: ∫M dt = Δ(Iω) for rotation. Particularly useful for impact problems, collisions, and impulsive forces. Momentum conservation: for isolated system with no external impulse, momentum is conserved. Coefficient of restitution e relates relative

topics.name: Kinematics of 2d motion of rigid bodies
topics.description: Rigid body kinematics in 2D describes translation and rotation without deformation. General plane motion combines translation of reference point (often center of mass) and rotation about axis perpendicular to plane. Position of any point P: rₚ = rₒ + rₚ/ₒ. Velocity: vₚ = vₒ + ω × rₚ/ₒ, where ω is angular velocity. Acceleration: aₚ = aₒ + α × rₚ/ₒ - ω²rₚ/ₒ, including tangential (α × r) and centripe

topics.name: Kinematics of a particle
topics.description: Kinematics describes particle motion geometry without considering forces causing motion. Position vector r(t) locates particle; velocity v = dr/dt is rate of position change; acceleration a = dv/dt = d²r/dt² is rate of velocity change. Coordinate systems include Cartesian (x,y,z), normal-tangential (along path), and polar/cylindrical. Equations relate position, velocity, acceleration, and time. Fo

topics.name: Kinematics of systems of particles
topics.description: Systems of particles involve multiple interacting particles, requiring description of each particle's motion plus system center of mass. Center of mass position: rcm = Σmᵢrᵢ / Σmᵢ moves as if all mass concentrated there with resultant external force applied. Relative motion between particles described by relative position, velocity, and acceleration vectors. Applications include multi-body systems

topics.name: Methods of work-energy
topics.description: Work-energy methods relate work done by forces to kinetic energy change: W₁₋₂ = ΔT, where W₁₋₂ = ∫F·dr is work, T = ½mv² (particle) or T = ½mv²cm + ½Iω² (rigid body) is kinetic energy. For conservative forces, mechanical energy conserves: T₁ + V₁ = T₂ + V₂, where V is potential energy. Work-energy avoids calculating acceleration and is efficient for problems involving velocities at different posit

topics.name: Moving reference frames
topics.description: Moving reference frames analyze motion relative to accelerating or rotating coordinate systems. Absolute acceleration: aₐbs = arel + 2ω × vrel + ω × (ω × r) + a₀, where arel is relative acceleration, 2ω × vrel is Coriolis acceleration, ω × (ω × r) is centripetal acceleration, a₀ is frame acceleration. These apparent accelerations arise from non-inertial frames. Applications include Earth-surface p

topics.name: Newton's laws
topics.description: Newton's three laws form the foundation of classical mechanics. First law (inertia): object remains at rest or constant velocity unless acted upon by net force. Second law: ΣF = ma relates net force to acceleration. Third law: for every action force, there exists equal and opposite reaction force. These laws apply to particles and centers of mass of systems. First law defines inertial reference fr

TAM 251.name: Introductory Solid Mechanics
TAM 251.description: on deformable bodies, and design principles based on mechanics of solids: normal stresses, shear stresses, and deformations produced by tensile, compressive, torsional, and bending loading of members; beam deflections; elastic energy and impact; multi-dimensional stress states; and buckling of columns.
objectives: Be able to calculate normal stresses in straight bars subjected to combinations of bending and either tension or compression.
Be able to apply principles of failure analysis and factors of safety based on stress.
Be able to calculate shear stresses in transversely loaded beams and in torsionally loaded members of circular cross section.
Be able to calculate maximum principal stresses and principal stress orientations in combined loading cases of generalized plane stress.
Be able to calculate deflections of elastic beams subjected to simple types of transverse loading.
Be able to determine the buckling loads of slender columns with simple types of end condition.

topics.name: Basic concepts of stress and strain
topics.description: Stress is internal force intensity (force per unit area) within deformed body, measured in Pa or psi. Normal stress σ acts perpendicular to surface; shear stress τ acts parallel. Strain is deformation intensity (change in length per unit length or angular distortion), dimensionless. Normal strain ε = ΔL/L; shear strain γ is angular change. Stress and strain are tensors with multiple components. Th

topics.name: Beam deflections
topics.description: Beam deflection is lateral displacement from undeformed position under load. Governed by differential equation: EI d²y/dx² = M(x), relating curvature to bending moment. Solutions provide deflection y(x) and slope θ = dy/dx along beam. Boundary conditions (support constraints) determine integration constants. Methods include direct integration, superposition, moment-area, and energy methods. Deflec

topics.name: Bending moments
topics.description: Bending moment M(x) represents internal moment about beam's neutral axis at any cross-section, causing bending curvature. Calculated from equilibrium: M = sum of moments about section from forces on one side. Sign convention: positive moment causes compression on top, tension on bottom (sagging). Relationships: dM/dx = V(x) and d²M/dx² = -w(x). Moment diagrams visualize M(x) distribution. Maximum

topics.name: Buckling of columns
topics.description: Buckling is sudden lateral deflection instability of slender columns under axial compression when critical load Pcr is reached. Unlike material failure (yielding/fracture), buckling is geometric instability where straight configuration becomes unstable. Critical load depends on stiffness EI, length L, and end conditions: Pcr = π²EI/(KL)² where K is effective length factor (K=1 for pinned-pinned, K

topics.name: Built-up beams
topics.description: Built-up beams consist of multiple components fastened together (bolts, welds, adhesives) to create composite section. Examples include wooden beams with multiple planks, steel I-beams with welded plates, reinforced concrete. Analysis requires checking component stresses and connection capacity. Shear flow q = VQ/I determines fastener force per length. Fastener spacing s ≤ Fallow/q ensures adequat

topics.name: Design based on deflections
topics.description: Deflection-based design limits beam deformation to acceptable levels for serviceability. Requirements: δmax ≤ δallow, commonly L/360 for floors, L/240 for roofs, where L is span. Calculate maximum deflection using appropriate method (integration, superposition, formulas). Required moment of inertia: I ≥ (load factor × Lⁿ)/(E × δallow), where n depends on loading type. Often governs design for long

topics.name: Design based on yield strength, ultimate strength
topics.description: Design based on strength ensures stresses remain below material limits with safety factors. Yield strength σy is stress where permanent deformation begins; ultimate strength σu is maximum stress before failure. Allowable stress: σallow = σy/SF (yield) or σu/SF (ultimate), where SF is safety factor (typically 1.5-3). Required area: A ≥ P/σallow. Design considers worst-case loading, material variabi

topics.name: Design of beams for structural applications
topics.description: Beam design ensures adequate strength and stiffness for applied loads. Requirements: σmax ≤ σallow (bending stress), τmax ≤ τallow (shear stress), δmax ≤ δallow (deflection limit), often L/360 for buildings. Select section providing required section modulus S ≥ Mmax/σallow and moment of inertia I ≥ (load function)/δallow. Consider economy (minimum weight/cost), availability (standard sections), co

topics.name: Design of columns
topics.description: Column design accounts for buckling and material failure. Process: (1) determine loads and effective length KL considering end conditions, (2) calculate slenderness ratio λ = KL/r, (3) use appropriate formula: Euler (long columns), empirical formulas like AISC equations (intermediate), yielding (short columns). Design equations often incorporate safety factors and account for imperfections. Select

topics.name: Design of shafts for power transmission
topics.description: Power transmission shafts design involves sizing for torque, stress, twist angle, and critical speed. Power-torque-speed relationship: P = Tω = 2πnT, where P is power (W), T is torque (N·m), ω is angular velocity (rad/s), n is rotational speed (rev/s). Design requirements: τmax ≤ τallow (strength) and φ ≤ φallow (stiffness). Diameter from strength: d ≥ ∛(16T/πτallow) for solid shaft. Critical spee

topics.name: Design problems for combined states of stress
topics.description: Combined loading creates multiaxial stress states requiring sophisticated analysis. Examples: shaft under bending and torsion, pressure vessel with internal pressure and axial load. Design process: (1) determine stress components from each loading, (2) combine using superposition, (3) find principal stresses and maximum shear, (4) apply failure criterion (yield or fracture), (5) calculate safety f

topics.name: Differential equations
topics.description: Differential equation method for beam deflection starts with EI d⁴y/dx⁴ = w(x), where w is distributed load, or EI d²y/dx² = M(x). Successive integration yields slope and deflection functions with integration constants determined by boundary conditions. For simple cases with constant EI, direct integration is efficient. Method provides exact solutions for deflection, slope, moment, and shear throu

topics.name: Direct integration
topics.description: Direct integration determines beam deflection by integrating load, shear, moment, slope, and deflection relationships sequentially. Starting from w(x): integrate to get V = ∫w dx, then M = ∫V dx, then EIθ = ∫M dx, finally EIy = ∫θ dx. Each integration introduces constant determined by boundary or continuity conditions. Method systematically builds from loading to deflection through all intermediat

topics.name: Double integration
topics.description: Double integration method solves EI d²y/dx² = M(x) by integrating twice to obtain slope θ = dy/dx and deflection y(x). First integration: EI dy/dx = ∫M dx + C₁. Second integration: EI y = ∫(∫M dx) dx + C₁x + C₂. Constants C₁ and C₂ determined from boundary conditions (deflection and slope at supports). Method is straightforward for beams with simple moment functions. Each load region may require s

topics.name: Euler theory
topics.description: Euler's theory derives critical buckling load for ideal elastic columns. Assumes: perfectly straight column, axial load through centroid, elastic material, small deflections. Differential equation: EI d²y/dx² + Py = 0. Solution gives sine wave deflection with critical load Pcr = π²EI/L² for pinned-pinned column. Critical stress: σcr = π²E/(L/r)², where r = √(I/A) is radius of gyration. Theory vali

topics.name: Geometry of deformation
topics.description: Geometry of deformation describes how beam cross-sections deform under bending. Plane sections remain plane (Bernoulli-Euler assumption): cross-sections perpendicular to neutral axis before bending remain plane after, but rotate. Curvature κ = 1/ρ relates to bending strain: ε = -y/ρ, where ρ is radius of curvature, y is distance from neutral axis. Combining with Hooke's law (σ = Eε) and stress res

topics.name: Method of superposition
topics.description: Superposition method determines beam deflection by combining solutions for simpler load cases. Total deflection equals sum of individual deflections: ytotal = y₁ + y₂ + y₃ + ... Uses tabulated solutions for standard cases (concentrated loads, distributed loads, moments on various beam configurations). Applicable only for linear elastic behavior where deflections are small. Efficient for complex lo

topics.name: Mohr's circle representations
topics.description: Mohr's circle is a graphical method for visualizing stress transformation in two dimensions. Circle is plotted with normal stress σ as horizontal axis and shear stress τ as vertical axis. Each point on circle represents stress state on a particular plane orientation. Center at (σavg, 0) with radius R = √[(σx-σy)²/4 + τxy²]. Principal stresses are circle's rightmost and leftmost points (maximum and

topics.name: Multi-axial stress and strain states
topics.description: Multi-axial stress states involve stress components in multiple directions: normal stresses (σx, σy, σz) and shear stresses (τxy, τyz, τzx). Stress is a tensor with nine components (six independent by symmetry). Principal stresses are normal stresses on planes with zero shear; found from eigenvalues of stress tensor. Maximum shear stress: τmax = (σ₁-σ₃)/2. Mohr's circle graphically represents 2D s

topics.name: Principal stresses and strains
topics.description: Principal stresses are maximum and minimum normal stresses occurring on planes where shear stress equals zero. For 2D: σ₁,₂ = (σx+σy)/2 ± √[(σx-σy)²/4 + τxy²]. Principal planes are mutually perpendicular. Maximum shear stress: τmax = (σ₁-σ₂)/2 occurs on plane at 45° to principal planes. Similarly, principal strains are maximum and minimum normal strains. Principal stress/strain directions define n

topics.name: Shear forces
topics.description: Shear force V(x) in solid mechanics context represents internal force perpendicular to beam axis resisting transverse loads. Calculated from equilibrium: V = sum of transverse forces on one side of section. Relationships: dV/dx = -w(x) and dM/dx = V, linking load, shear, and moment. Sign convention: positive shear causes clockwise rotation on left face. Shear diagrams visualize V(x) distribution.

topics.name: Slopes
topics.description: Slope θ = dy/dx is the angular rotation of beam's tangent line at any point along its length. Related to curvature by EI d²y/dx² = M, so EI dθ/dx = M, meaning slope change depends on bending moment. Boundary conditions include: fixed support (θ = 0), pin support (moment = 0 but θ ≠ 0), free end (moment = 0). Continuity conditions at internal points require slope continuous unless hinge present. Sl

topics.name: States of plane stress and plane strain
topics.description: Plane stress assumes stress components perpendicular to plane are negligible: σz = τxz = τyz = 0. Common in thin plates and shells where thickness dimension is small. Plane strain assumes strain perpendicular to plane is negligible: εz = γxz = γyz = 0, typical in long structures where length >> cross-section (dams, tunnels). Both simplify 3D problems to 2D analysis. Plane stress allows σx, σy, τxy

topics.name: Statically determinate and indeterminate problems
topics.description: Statically determinate systems have reactions and internal forces solvable using equilibrium equations alone. Number of unknowns equals number of independent equilibrium equations. Statically indeterminate (redundant) systems have more unknowns than equilibrium equations, requiring compatibility conditions (deformation constraints) and material properties for solution. Degree of indeterminacy: num

topics.name: Statically determinate and indeterminate systems
topics.description: In torsion, statically determinate shafts have torque distribution determinable from equilibrium (ΣT = 0) alone. Single-segment shafts or those with one fixed end are typically determinate. Indeterminate torsion problems have redundant constraints, requiring compatibility equations: sum of twist angles in closed loop equals zero. Combined with torque-twist relationship φ = TL/GJ, these enable solu

topics.name: Stress distribution
topics.description: Stress distribution describes how stress varies throughout a member. In bending: normal stress varies linearly with depth (σ = -My/I), maximum at extreme fibers. In torsion: shear stress varies linearly with radius (τ = Tρ/J for circular sections), maximum at outer surface. For combined loading, superposition applies (if material remains elastic). Stress concentrations occur at geometric discontin

topics.name: Stresses due to bending
topics.description: Bending creates normal stresses varying linearly across beam depth: σ = -My/I, where M is bending moment, y is distance from neutral axis, I is second moment of area. Maximum stress at extreme fibers: σmax = Mc/I = M/S, where c is distance to extreme fiber, S = I/c is section modulus. Tension on one side, compression on other; zero at neutral axis (centroid for symmetric sections). Compressive str

topics.name: Symmetric elastic beams
topics.description: Symmetric elastic beams have at least one axis of symmetry through centroid, simplifying analysis. Common sections include rectangular, circular, I-beams, channels. For symmetric sections, neutral axis coincides with centroidal axis, principal axes align with symmetry axes, and product of inertia Ixy = 0. Bending about symmetry axis produces pure bending without twisting. Elastic implies linear st

topics.name: Torsion of circular shafts and thin-walled sections
topics.description: Torsion involves twisting of members under applied torque. For circular shafts, shear stress varies linearly from zero at center to maximum at surface: τmax = Tρ/J, where T is torque, ρ is radius, J is polar moment of inertia. Angle of twist: φ = TL/GJ, where G is shear modulus, L is length. For solid circular shaft: J = πd⁴/32; hollow: J = π(do⁴-di⁴)/32. Thin-walled tubes: τ = T/(2At), where A is

topics.name: Transformation of stress and strain
topics.description: Transformation of stress and strain involves determining stress and strain components in different coordinate systems. Stress and strain are tensors that transform according to specific rules when rotating axes. Transformation equations for 2D stress: σx' = (σx+σy)/2 + (σx-σy)/2·cos(2θ) + τxy·sin(2θ). Similar equations exist for strain. Understanding transformations enables finding stresses on any

topics.name: Transverse shear
topics.description: Transverse shear stress in beams results from shear force, varying across section depth (unlike bending stress). For rectangular beam: τ = VQ/Ib, where V is shear force, Q = ∫y dA is first moment of area above point, I is moment of inertia, b is width. Maximum at neutral axis: τmax = 3V/2A for rectangle. Zero at extreme fibers. Shear flow q = VQ/I represents shear force per unit length along beam.

topics.name: Two-dimensional elastic stress-strain relations
topics.description: For plane stress: εx = (σx - νσy)/E, εy = (σy - νσx)/E, γxy = τxy/G where E is Young's modulus, ν is Poisson's ratio, G = E/2(1+ν) is shear modulus. For plane strain: similar but with modified elastic constants E' = E/(1-ν²), ν' = ν/(1-ν). These constitutive relations connect stress to strain for isotropic linear elastic materials. Inverse relations give stress from strain. Essential for solving e

topics.name: Uniaxial loading and deformation
topics.description: Uniaxial loading involves force along single axis, creating uniform normal stress σ = P/A and strain ε = δ/L in simple members. For elastic materials, Hooke's law: σ = Eε, where E is Young's modulus (material stiffness). Poisson's ratio ν describes lateral contraction: εlateral = -νεaxial. Elongation δ = PL/AE for constant cross-section. Stress-strain curves characterize material behavior: elastic

topics.name: Yield criteria
topics.description: Yield criteria predict when material begins plastic deformation under multiaxial stress. Maximum shear stress theory (Tresca): yielding occurs when τmax = (σ₁-σ₃)/2 = σy/2. Von Mises theory: yielding when √[(σ₁-σ₂)² + (σ₂-σ₃)² + (σ₃-σ₁)²]/√2 = σy, equivalent to distortion energy reaching critical value. Von Mises generally more accurate for ductile metals; Tresca is conservative. For 2D: (σx-σy)²

ECE 206.name: Electrical and Electronics Lab
ECE 206.description: Laboratory experiments in digital logic and controllers; transistor amplifier and switching circuits; DC motor control and voltage regulators; sensors and motion control with feedback; wireless communication.
objectives: ECE206 is the lab course which builds on ECE 205 concepts; ECE 205 is an introductory course in circuit analysis for non-majors in engineering.
The goals are to supplement the material of ECE 205 and provide a hands-on experience in assembling and testing electric and electronic circuits.

topics.name: Arduino (or similar microcontroller platform) introduction and programming
topics.description: Arduino is an open-source microcontroller platform for embedded systems programming and physical computing. Features digital I/O pins, analog inputs, PWM outputs, and serial communication. Programming in C/C++-based Arduino language using IDE. Basic structure: setup() (initialization) and loop() (continuous execution). Learn to read sensors, control actuators, implement timing, and communicate wit

topics.name: Bjt review and logic gates
topics.description: Laboratory exploration of BJT characteristics and logic gate implementation. Measure BJT parameters: IC-VBE curves, β determination, and switching times. Build and test logic gates using discrete BJTs and resistors. Observe voltage levels for logic 0 and 1, verify truth tables, and measure propagation delays. Compare theoretical predictions with measurements. Use oscilloscope to observe switching

topics.name: H-bridge control of dc motor (closed loop)
topics.description: Closed-loop control measures actual motor speed (using encoder or tachometer) and adjusts PWM duty cycle to match desired setpoint. Feedback loop compares reference speed to measured speed; error drives controller (typically PID: Proportional-Integral-Derivative). Controller output adjusts H-bridge PWM to minimize error. Advantages: maintains consistent speed despite load changes, compensates for

topics.name: H-bridge control of dc motor (open loop)
topics.description: Open-loop motor control uses H-bridge without feedback - command input directly determines PWM duty cycle and direction, but system doesn't measure actual motor response. Microcontroller sends PWM signals to H-bridge switches to set desired speed (duty cycle) and direction (which transistor pair active). Advantages: simple implementation, fast response. Disadvantages: no compensation for load chan

topics.name: H-bridge design and construction
topics.description: An H-bridge is a circuit using four switches (typically MOSFETs or BJTs) arranged in H-shape to control DC motor direction and speed. Four switches labeled: Q1, Q2 (high side), Q3, Q4 (low side). Forward: Q1, Q4 on → current flows left-to-right through motor. Reverse: Q2, Q3 on → current flows right-to-left. Brake: Q3, Q4 on (or Q1, Q2) → motor terminals shorted. Combining with PWM enables speed c

topics.name: Mosfet based logic and switching
topics.description: CMOS (Complementary MOS) logic uses NMOS and PMOS transistors to create power-efficient gates. NMOS pulls output low (strong 0), PMOS pulls output high (strong 1). CMOS inverter: NMOS and PMOS in series; input high → NMOS on, PMOS off → output low. NAND, NOR, and complex gates follow similar complementary design. Key advantages: near-zero static power (one transistor always off), full voltage swin

topics.name: Mosfet device operation and characteristics
topics.description: MOSFETs (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors) are voltage-controlled switches/amplifiers with three terminals: gate, drain, source. N-channel (NMOS) conducts when VGS > VTH; P-channel (PMOS) conducts when VGS

topics.name: Pwm operation
topics.description: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) encodes analog information in digital signal by varying duty cycle (ratio of on-time to period). PWM signal has fixed frequency but adjustable pulse width. Duty cycle D = Ton/T, where 0 ≤ D ≤ 1. Average voltage Vavg = D × Vsupply. Applications include motor speed control, LED brightness, DC-DC conversion, and audio synthesis. Low-pass filtering recovers analog signal.

ME 310.name: Fundamentals of Fluid Dynamics
ME 310.description: Introduction to fluid mechanics with coverage of theory and applications of incompressible viscous and inviscid flows, and compressible high-speed flows.
objectives: Be able to analyze hydrostatic pressures, forces, and moments.
Be able to apply control volume and/or differential analyses to incompressible viscous and inviscid flow, and high-speed compressible flow.
Have a working knowledge of fluid flow devices.
Be able to design, conduct, analyze, and present the results from fluid flow experiments.

topics.name: Angular momentum
topics.description: The angular momentum equation for a control volume relates torques to rotational motion of fluid: the sum of torques equals the rate of angular momentum change plus net angular momentum flux. Written as Στ = ∂/∂t(∫r×ρVdV) + ∫r×ρV(V⋅n)dA, where r is position vector from the axis of rotation. This principle is fundamental for analyzing rotating fluid machinery like turbines, pumps, compressors, and

topics.name: Bernoulli's equation
topics.description: Bernoulli's equation states that for steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit volume remains constant: P + ½ρV² + ρgz = constant. This can be interpreted as conservation of mechanical energy per unit volume or written per unit mass. Bernoulli's equation explains many phenomena like lift generation, ventu

topics.name: Boundary layers
topics.description: A boundary layer is the thin region adjacent to a solid surface where viscous effects are significant and velocity transitions from zero at the wall (no-slip condition) to the free-stream value. Boundary layers can be laminar (smooth, orderly flow) or turbulent (chaotic fluctuations), with transition depending on Reynolds number. Boundary layer thickness, displacement thickness, and momentum thick

topics.name: Bourdon gauges
topics.description: Bourdon gauges are mechanical pressure measuring devices consisting of a curved, flattened tube that straightens under internal pressure. One end is fixed and connected to the pressure source, while the free end connects to a pointer through a mechanical linkage. As pressure increases, the tube tends to straighten, moving the pointer across a calibrated dial. Bourdon gauges are robust, reliable, a

topics.name: Centrifugal pump characterization
topics.description: Centrifugal pump characterization involves experimentally determining performance curves relating head (pressure increase), power consumption, and efficiency to flow rate. A rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy to fluid, which converts to pressure in the volute or diffuser. Characteristic curves (head-capacity, power-capacity, efficiency-capacity) define operating range, best efficiency point,

topics.name: Compressible flow
topics.description: Compressible flow occurs when density changes are significant, typically at Mach numbers above 0.3 where gas velocity becomes comparable to sound speed. Unlike incompressible flow, pressure waves propagate at finite speed (speed of sound), creating phenomena like shock waves (abrupt property changes), expansion waves, and choked flow where mass flow rate becomes independent of downstream condition

topics.name: Control volume equations for continuity
topics.description: The continuity equation for a control volume expresses conservation of mass: the rate of mass accumulation within the control volume equals the net mass flow rate entering minus leaving. For steady flow, mass in equals mass out. Written as ∂/∂t(∫ρdV) + ∫ρ(V⋅n)dA = 0, it can be simplified for steady, one-dimensional flow to ṁin = ṁout or ρ₁A₁V₁ = ρ₂A₂V₂. For incompressible flow, this reduces to vol

topics.name: Cylinder in cross flow
topics.description: A cylinder in cross flow (flow perpendicular to the cylinder axis) demonstrates external flow phenomena including boundary layer development, flow separation, wake formation, and vortex shedding. At low Reynolds numbers, flow remains attached; at moderate Re, laminar boundary layers separate creating recirculating wake regions; at high Re, vortices shed alternately from each side (Kármán vortex st

topics.name: Design project
topics.description: A design project integrates fluid mechanics principles to solve an open-ended, realistic engineering problem. Students apply continuity, momentum, and energy equations; select appropriate components (pumps, valves, meters); size piping and equipment; consider costs, constraints, and optimization; and present professional documentation. Projects might include designing water distribution systems, v

topics.name: Differential continuity and momentum equations
topics.description: The differential forms of continuity and momentum equations describe fluid motion at a point rather than over a control volume. The continuity equation ∂ρ/∂t + ∇⋅(ρV) = 0 expresses mass conservation infinitesimally. The momentum equation (Navier-Stokes equations) ρ(∂V/∂t + V⋅∇V) = -∇P + μ∇²V + ρg relates acceleration to pressure gradient, viscous forces, and body forces. These coupled partial diff

topics.name: Dimensional similitude and model testing
topics.description: Dimensional similitude uses dimensional analysis and the Buckingham Pi theorem to relate physical systems of different scales. By identifying dimensionless groups (Reynolds number, Froude number, Mach number, etc.), engineers can design scaled models that predict full-scale prototype behavior. Complete similitude requires all dimensionless parameters to match between model and prototype, though th

topics.name: Energy
topics.description: The energy equation for a control volume applies the first law of thermodynamics to flowing fluids: the rate of energy change within the control volume plus net energy flux equals heat transfer rate minus work rate. Energy includes internal, kinetic, potential, and flow work (pressure-volume work). For steady flow through devices like turbines, pumps, and heat exchangers, this becomes the steady-f

topics.name: Fluid kinematics
topics.description: Fluid kinematics describes fluid motion without considering the forces causing the motion. It encompasses concepts like velocity and acceleration fields, streamlines (lines tangent to velocity vectors), pathlines (trajectories of fluid particles), and streaklines (locations of particles passing through a point). The material derivative relates local changes and convective changes in properties fol

topics.name: Fluid properties
topics.description: Fluid properties characterize material behavior and response to forces. Key properties include density (mass per volume), viscosity (resistance to shear deformation), surface tension (intermolecular forces at interfaces), vapor pressure (saturation pressure at given temperature), bulk modulus (resistance to compression), and thermal properties like specific heat and thermal conductivity. Understan

topics.name: Free air jet
topics.description: A free air jet is an unconfined flow issuing from a nozzle or orifice into ambient air, entraining surrounding fluid and creating a complex mixing region. The jet structure includes a potential core (where centerline velocity equals exit velocity), a mixing layer at the periphery with velocity gradients and turbulence, and a fully developed region where velocity profiles become self-similar. Jet s

topics.name: Hot wire anemometers
topics.description: Hot wire anemometers measure fluid velocity by monitoring heat transfer from a small electrically heated wire (or film) to the flowing fluid. As velocity increases, convective cooling increases, changing the wire's temperature and electrical resistance. The instrument operates either at constant temperature (varying current to maintain temperature, current relates to velocity) or constant current

topics.name: Hydrostatics
topics.description: Hydrostatics analyzes fluids at rest or moving as a rigid body without shear stresses. The fundamental equation of hydrostatics, dP/dz = -ρg, relates pressure changes to fluid depth, showing that pressure increases linearly with depth in constant-density fluids. This leads to calculating hydrostatic forces on submerged surfaces (flat or curved), determining centers of pressure, analyzing buoyancy

topics.name: Lift and drag
topics.description: Lift is the force component perpendicular to the free-stream velocity, while drag is the force component parallel to the flow direction, both resulting from pressure distribution and shear stress on a body. Lift generation on airfoils involves circulation and pressure differences between upper and lower surfaces, explained by Bernoulli's principle and the Kutta condition. Drag consists of pressure

topics.name: Linear momentum
topics.description: The linear momentum equation for a control volume applies Newton's second law to fluid flow: the sum of external forces on the control volume equals the rate of momentum change within the volume plus the net momentum flux leaving. Expressed as ΣF = ∂/∂t(∫ρVdV) + ∫ρV(V⋅n)dA, this vector equation is applied component by component. For steady flow, it simplifies to force equals net momentum outflow r

topics.name: Manometers
topics.description: Manometers are U-tube devices that measure pressure differences using the height difference of liquid columns. Based on hydrostatic principles, they relate pressure difference to ρgh where h is the height difference and ρ is the manometer fluid density. Different configurations include simple U-tube manometers, well-type manometers, differential manometers, and inclined manometers for higher sensi

topics.name: Numerical solutions
topics.description: Numerical solutions in fluid dynamics involve discretizing governing equations (continuity, momentum, energy) into algebraic equations solved computationally. Methods include finite difference, finite volume, finite element, and spectral methods applied to meshes or grids representing the flow domain. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) enables solving complex, realistic problems impossible to solv

topics.name: Orifice meters
topics.description: An orifice meter measures flow rate using a thin plate with a circular hole inserted in a pipe. The abrupt area contraction creates a high-velocity jet at the vena contracta (minimum flow area downstream of the orifice) with corresponding pressure drop. Flow rate is calculated from the pressure difference across the orifice using Q = C_d A₂√(2ΔP/ρ), where C_d is the discharge coefficient accountin

topics.name: Pipe flow
topics.description: Pipe flow examines fluid transport through closed conduits, covering both laminar and turbulent regimes. Laminar flow (Re

topics.name: Pipe flows
topics.description: Pipe flow analysis examines fluid transport through closed conduits, encompassing both laminar and turbulent regimes. For laminar flow (Re

topics.name: Pitot tubes
topics.description: A pitot tube measures fluid velocity by converting kinetic energy to pressure. It consists of a tube with an opening facing the flow (measuring stagnation pressure P₀) and static pressure taps (measuring static pressure P). Applying Bernoulli's equation, velocity is calculated from V = √(2(P₀-P)/ρ). Pitot-static tubes combine both measurements in one probe. These devices are widely used in aircraf

topics.name: Potential flow
topics.description: Potential flow theory analyzes inviscid, irrotational flows where velocity derives from a scalar potential function (V = ∇φ). The velocity potential satisfies Laplace's equation (∇²φ = 0), a linear PDE allowing superposition of elementary solutions like uniform flow, sources, sinks, doublets, and vortices. Combining these building blocks creates complex flow patterns around cylinders, spheres, and

topics.name: Pressure transducers
topics.description: Pressure transducers convert fluid pressure into electrical signals (voltage, current, or digital output) using various sensing principles including strain gauges, piezoelectric crystals, capacitive elements, or optical sensors. They offer advantages over mechanical gauges: fast response time, remote monitoring capability, easy data logging, and compatibility with automated control systems. Common

topics.name: Superposition
topics.description: Superposition in fluid mechanics exploits the linearity of governing equations (for incompressible, inviscid flow) to combine simple flow solutions into complex patterns. Elementary solutions include uniform flow, sources, sinks, doublets (source-sink pairs), and vortices. For example, superposing uniform flow with a doublet creates flow around a cylinder; adding circulation generates lift. This m

topics.name: Venturi meters
topics.description: A venturi meter measures flow rate in pipes using a converging-diverging section that accelerates then decelerates the flow. By measuring the pressure difference between the throat (narrow section) and inlet, flow rate is calculated using continuity and Bernoulli equations: Q = A₂√(2(P₁-P₂)/(ρ(1-(A₂/A₁)²))). The gradual area changes minimize energy losses compared to other flow meters. Venturi met

topics.name: Viscous flows
topics.description: Viscous flows involve significant friction effects where viscous forces are comparable to or dominate inertial forces. Viscosity causes velocity gradients and shear stresses within the fluid, dissipating mechanical energy into heat. The Reynolds number (Re = ρVL/μ) characterizes the relative importance of inertial to viscous forces. Low Reynolds number flows are dominated by viscosity (Stokes flow

ME 330.name: Engineering Materials
ME 330.description: Structures of polymers, metals, and ceramics as the basis for their mechanical behavior. Manipulation of structure through processes such as heat treatment and solidification. Mechanisms of material failure in service (yielding, fracture, fatigue, creep, corrosion, wear) and simple design techniques to avoid these failures. Strategies for material selection in design.
objectives: Be able to converse intelligently with materials experts.
Be aware of the major failure mechanisms of materials in service and the basic design methodologies associated with them.
Be able to choose an appropriate material for a given application, based on a working knowledge of how different materials behave (properties) and why (microstructure).
Be able to make basic design decisions regarding materials processes in order to produce the microstructure necessary to achieve a desired set of material properties.
Be able to conduct, analyze, and present the results from experiments with materials.
Be able to use the latest software to enter and collect experimental data.
Be able to write a good engineering report.
Be able to create and deliver a good engineering presentation.

topics.name: Aging
topics.description: Aging (precipitation hardening) is heat treatment following solution treatment and quenching, where supersaturated solid solution decomposes forming fine precipitate particles that strengthen material by obstructing dislocation motion. Natural aging occurs at room temperature; artificial aging uses elevated temperature accelerating precipitation. Optimal aging produces maximum strength when precip

topics.name: Annealing
topics.description: Annealing is a heat treatment involving heating metal to specific temperature, holding, then slowly cooling to soften material, relieve stresses, improve ductility, refine grain structure, or remove work hardening effects. Full annealing (heating above transformation temperature) completely recrystallizes the structure. Process annealing (lower temperature) partially softens cold-worked material e

topics.name: Bonding
topics.description: Atomic bonding determines how atoms connect in materials, fundamentally controlling properties. Primary bonds include ionic (electron transfer, ceramics), covalent (electron sharing, polymers/semiconductors), and metallic (delocalized electron sea, metals). Secondary bonds (van der Waals, hydrogen) are weaker intermolecular forces. Bond type dictates material characteristics: metals conduct due to

topics.name: Ceramics: ceramic microstructures
topics.description: Ceramic microstructures consist of crystalline grains, glass phases, and porosity. Grain size, shape, and distribution affect mechanical properties: fine grains improve strength (Hall-Petch), elongated grains can provide toughness. Glass phases at grain boundaries affect high-temperature properties and processing. Porosity (unavoidable in most ceramics) significantly reduces strength and stiffness

topics.name: Ceramics processing (particulate, slip casting, sintering, glass processing)
topics.description: Ceramic processing starts with powders, forming green bodies, then densifying through sintering. Particulate processing includes mixing, milling, and granulation. Slip casting pours ceramic slurry into porous molds, water absorption forms shape. Dry pressing compacts powder in dies. Sintering heats to below melting point, diffusion bonds particles reducing porosity. Glass processing melts raw mate

topics.name: Cold work and annealing
topics.description: Cold work is plastic deformation below recrystallization temperature, increasing strength and hardness through work hardening while reducing ductility. Applications include wire drawing, rolling, and sheet forming. Excessive cold work causes brittleness, requiring intermediate annealing to restore ductility for further forming. Annealing heats to allow recovery (stress relief) or recrystallization

topics.name: Commercial processes
topics.description: Commercial processes are industrial-scale manufacturing methods combining multiple steps optimized for efficiency, quality, and cost. Examples include integrated steel production (blast furnace, BOF, continuous casting, rolling), aluminum production (Bayer process, Hall-Héroult electrolysis, casting, forming), and semiconductor processing (crystal growth, wafer slicing, lithography). Understanding

topics.name: Composites: high-performance fibers (glass, carbon, aramid)
topics.description: High-performance fibers reinforce composites, providing superior strength and stiffness. Glass fibers offer good strength, low cost, electrical insulation. Carbon fibers provide exceptional stiffness, high strength-to-weight ratio, low thermal expansion, but are expensive. Aramid fibers (Kevlar) combine high strength, toughness, impact resistance. Fiber properties depend on structure: carbon fiber

topics.name: Composites processing
topics.description: Composites processing creates shaped parts from reinforcement and matrix, including hand layup (labor-intensive, low volume), spray-up (faster, moderate volume), filament winding (cylindrical parts), pultrusion (constant cross-section), resin transfer molding (RTM), and autoclave curing. Processing goals include achieving fiber content and orientation, eliminating voids, developing matrix properti

topics.name: Corrosion
topics.description: Corrosion is electrochemical degradation of materials (primarily metals) through environmental interaction. Types include uniform attack, galvanic (dissimilar metals), pitting (localized), crevice, intergranular, and stress corrosion cracking. Corrosion requires anode (metal dissolving), cathode (reduction reaction), electrolyte (conducting medium), and electrical connection. Prevention methods in

topics.name: Crystallography
topics.description: Crystallography studies the arrangement of atoms in crystalline materials forming periodic, repeating three-dimensional patterns called crystal lattices. Common structures include face-centered cubic (FCC), body-centered cubic (BCC), and hexagonal close-packed (HCP). Unit cells are the smallest repeating units defining the entire crystal. Crystal structure determines packing density, slip systems

topics.name: Diffusion and growth
topics.description: Diffusion is atomic migration from high to low concentration regions, enabling solid-state phase transformations, homogenization, and surface treatments (carburizing, nitriding). Fick's laws govern diffusion: flux proportional to concentration gradient, with diffusivity following Arrhenius relationship (exponentially increasing with temperature). Growth processes in phase transformations occur thr

topics.name: Dislocations and vacancies
topics.description: Dislocations are line defects where crystal structure is disrupted, enabling plastic deformation by allowing atomic planes to slip past each other incrementally rather than breaking all bonds simultaneously. Edge and screw dislocations are primary types. Vacancies are point defects where atomic sites are empty. These defects profoundly affect properties: dislocations enable ductility (metals defor

topics.name: Effect of processing on structure
topics.description: Processing history profoundly affects material structure and resulting properties. In metals: cooling rate determines grain size and phase distribution; deformation creates texture and residual stresses; heat treatment modifies phases and microstructure. In polymers: processing affects molecular orientation, crystallinity, and morphology. In composites: processing determines fiber distribution and

topics.name: Fiber orientation and length effects
topics.description: Fiber orientation and length significantly affect composite properties. Aligned continuous fibers maximize properties in one direction but create anisotropy. Random fiber orientation (short fibers, mats) provides more isotropic but lower average properties. Short/discontinuous fibers have reduced reinforcement efficiency due to load transfer at ends requiring critical length for effective reinforc

topics.name: Forging
topics.description: Forging shapes metal through compressive forces applied by hammers, presses, or dies, creating parts with superior mechanical properties due to refined grain structure and aligned grain flow. Open-die forging shapes between flat or simple dies; closed-die forging uses shaped cavity dies creating complex geometries. Hot forging (above recrystallization temperature) enables large deformation with lo

topics.name: Forming processes
topics.description: Forming processes shape metals through plastic deformation without material removal, including rolling (reducing thickness between rollers), forging (compressing with hammers/presses), extrusion (forcing through dies), drawing (pulling through dies), and sheet metal forming (stamping, deep drawing). These processes exploit metal ductility, offering high production rates, good mechanical properties

topics.name: Grain growth
topics.description: Grain growth is the increase in average grain size through boundary migration after recrystallization, driven by reduction in total grain boundary energy. Larger grains grow at the expense of smaller ones as boundaries migrate toward their centers of curvature. Grain growth occurs during prolonged annealing at elevated temperatures where atomic mobility is sufficient. Controlling grain size is cru

topics.name: Hardenability of steels
topics.description: Hardenability indicates a steel's ability to form martensite in the interior when quenched, differing from hardness (resistance to indentation). High hardenability steels form martensite deeply with slower cooling (oil/air quench), while low hardenability requires rapid cooling (water quench) and only surface hardens. Hardenability depends on alloy content and grain size, measured by Jominy end-qu

topics.name: Hardness, strength and compression
topics.description: Hardness measures resistance to localized plastic deformation (indentation), correlating with strength and wear resistance. Common tests include Rockwell, Brinell, Vickers, and Knoop using different indenters and loads. Compression testing applies compressive loads measuring deformation behavior. Most engineering materials are stronger in compression than tension since compression closes rather th

topics.name: Heat treatment of steel
topics.description: Steel heat treatment exploits iron-carbon phase diagram creating diverse microstructures with different properties. Processes include annealing (soft, ductile ferrite-pearlite), normalizing (refined grains), hardening (quenching to martensite - very hard/brittle), and tempering (reheating martensite for toughness). Carburizing and nitriding add carbon/nitrogen to surfaces for hard cases with tough

topics.name: Heat treatment processes
topics.description: Heat treatment alters material properties through controlled heating and cooling cycles without changing composition. Common processes include annealing (softening), normalizing (refining structure), hardening (quenching for strength), tempering (reducing brittleness), and aging (precipitation hardening). Each process has specific temperature, hold time, and cooling rate requirements. Heat treatme

topics.name: Heat treat solution
topics.description: Solution treatment (solutionizing) is the first step in precipitation hardening, involving heating alloy above solvus temperature to dissolve alloying elements into a single-phase solid solution, followed by rapid quenching to retain supersaturated solid solution at room temperature. This metastable state contains excess solute that will precipitate during subsequent aging. Solution treatment temp

topics.name: It and cct diagrams
topics.description: Isothermal Transformation (IT or TTT - Time-Temperature-Transformation) diagrams show transformation progress at constant temperatures, displaying C-curves indicating start and finish of phase transformations. Continuous Cooling Transformation (CCT) diagrams show transformations during continuous cooling at various rates, accounting for transformation suppression during cooling. These diagrams are

topics.name: Matrix materials and the role of the matrix
topics.description: The matrix in composites is the continuous phase surrounding and binding fibers, transferring loads between fibers, providing shape, protecting fibers from environment and damage, and determining processing methods. Common matrices include polymers (epoxy, polyester - lightweight, low temperature), metals (aluminum, titanium - higher temperature, toughness), and ceramics (silicon carbide, carbon -

topics.name: Nucleation
topics.description: Nucleation initiates phase transformations by forming small clusters of new phase within parent phase. Homogeneous nucleation occurs spontaneously in uniform material requiring significant undercooling to overcome surface energy penalty of creating interfaces. Heterogeneous nucleation occurs at preferential sites (grain boundaries, inclusions, container walls) requiring less undercooling since exi

topics.name: Other composites
topics.description: Beyond fiber-reinforced composites, other composite types include particle-reinforced (concrete, cermets, filled polymers), laminar (plywood, bimetals), and hybrid composites. Particle composites use particles improving stiffness, wear resistance, or reducing cost. Laminar composites stack layers exploiting individual layer properties or creating tailored properties through stacking sequence. Nano

topics.name: Phase diagrams
topics.description: Phase diagrams are graphical representations showing which phases are stable at different compositions and temperatures, essential tools for predicting material behavior during processing and service. Binary phase diagrams plot temperature versus composition for two-component systems, indicating phase boundaries, transformation temperatures, and relative amounts of phases present (lever rule). Com

topics.name: Phase diagrams and casting
topics.description: Phase diagrams guide casting by predicting solidification behavior: liquidus/solidus temperatures, freezing range, phases present during cooling, and segregation tendencies. Narrow freezing range (eutectic or near-pure metals) enables easy casting with less shrinkage porosity. Wide freezing range causes mushy zone, complicating feeding and increasing segregation. Eutectic compositions have lowest

topics.name: Polymer and composite materials
topics.description: Polymers and composites offer unique property combinations unattainable in traditional materials. Polymers provide low density, corrosion resistance, easy processing, but limited temperature range and lower stiffness. Composites combine reinforcement (fibers, particles) and matrix (polymer, metal, ceramic) tailoring properties: high strength-to-weight, directional properties, design flexibility. A

topics.name: Polymer processing (molding, extrusion)
topics.description: Polymer processing shapes polymers through thermomechanical operations exploiting their viscoelastic flow behavior at elevated temperatures. Injection molding forces molten polymer into closed molds for complex 3D parts. Extrusion pushes polymer through shaped dies creating continuous profiles (pipes, films, sheet). Both processes involve heating polymer above Tg (amorphous) or melting point (semi

topics.name: Polymer structure
topics.description: Polymer structure encompasses molecular architecture (linear, branched, crosslinked, networked chains) and arrangement (amorphous versus semicrystalline). Polymers are long-chain molecules (macromolecules) formed by repeating monomers through polymerization. Chain length (molecular weight) affects properties: longer chains increase strength, viscosity. Linear thermoplastics soften when heated; cro

topics.name: Polyphase materials
topics.description: Polyphase (multiphase) materials contain two or more distinct phases with different compositions and/or crystal structures, including steels (ferrite, cementite), aluminum alloys (α-aluminum, precipitates), and composites. Phase mixtures provide property combinations unattainable from single phases: steel combines soft ductile ferrite with hard cementite for balanced strength and ductility. Phase

topics.name: Properties and classification of materials
topics.description: Materials are classified into metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites based on bonding and structure. Metals have metallic bonding, crystalline structure, high conductivity, ductility, and moderate strength. Ceramics have ionic/covalent bonding, crystalline/amorphous structure, high hardness/brittleness, low conductivity, high melting points. Polymers have covalent backbone chains with secondar

topics.name: Properties of materials (mechanical, thermal, optical, electrical)
topics.description: Material properties characterize how materials respond to external stimuli, categorized as mechanical (strength, stiffness, toughness, hardness), thermal (conductivity, expansion, specific heat), optical (transparency, reflectivity, refractive index), and electrical (conductivity, resistivity, dielectric constant). These properties derive from atomic bonding, crystal structure, and microstructure.

topics.name: Quenching and heat transfer
topics.description: Quenching rapidly cools materials from elevated temperatures using media (water, oil, polymer solutions, air) to achieve desired microstructures or retain high-temperature phases. Cooling rate varies with location (surface versus core) creating property gradients and residual stresses. Heat transfer occurs through film boiling (slow cooling), nucleate boiling (rapid), and convection stages, with q

topics.name: Recrystallization
topics.description: Recrystallization is the formation of new strain-free grains in cold-worked material during heating, replacing deformed structure with new equiaxed grains. The process begins at the recrystallization temperature (typically 0.3-0.5 times melting point in Kelvin), where new grains nucleate at high-energy sites (grain boundaries, deformation bands) and grow consuming the deformed matrix. Recrystalliz

topics.name: Rolling
topics.description: Rolling passes metal between rotating cylindrical rolls, reducing thickness and elongating material. Hot rolling (above recrystallization temperature) shapes ingots into slabs, plates, and structural shapes with concurrent grain refinement. Cold rolling (below recrystallization) produces precise dimensions, smooth surfaces, and work-hardened products like sheet, foil, and strip. Rolling variables

topics.name: Solidification: cast micro- and macrostructures
topics.description: Solidification creates microstructure as liquid metal freezes, with cooling rate, composition, and geometry determining grain size, dendrite structure, and defect formation. Microstructure includes grain size (fine grains from rapid cooling or grain refiners), dendrite arm spacing (indicating cooling rate), and segregation (composition variations). Macrostructure shows columnar grains (directional

topics.name: Solidification processing (casting, solidification time, stresses of solidification)
topics.description: Solidification processing creates metal parts by pouring molten metal into molds. Solidification time depends on volume/surface area ratio (Chvorinov's rule), affecting cooling rate and microstructure. As metal contracts during cooling, stresses develop from differential solidification, constraint from mold/cores, and thermal contraction. Hot tears occur when tensile stresses exceed low strength o

topics.name: Stiffness and strength estimates—aligned continuous fibers
topics.description: For aligned continuous fiber composites, properties parallel to fibers follow rule-of-mixtures: composite modulus/strength equals volume-fraction-weighted average of fiber and matrix properties. Parallel loading efficiently transfers load to fibers, maximizing composite properties. Perpendicular properties are lower, approximated by series model or reciprocal rule-of-mixtures. This anisotropy mean

topics.name: Strengthening mechanisms
topics.description: Strengthening mechanisms increase material strength by impeding dislocation motion. Grain boundary strengthening (Hall-Petch) uses fine grains as barriers. Solid solution strengthening adds alloying atoms creating lattice distortions. Precipitation hardening forms fine precipitate particles obstructing dislocations. Work hardening increases dislocation density causing entanglement. Each mechanism

topics.name: Tensile stress-strain relations
topics.description: Tensile testing stretches specimens measuring stress-strain relationships, revealing elastic modulus (stiffness), yield strength (onset of plasticity), ultimate tensile strength (maximum stress), and ductility (elongation to fracture). The stress-strain curve shows linear elastic region (Hooke's law), yield point, work hardening region, necking, and fracture. Engineering stress (load/original area

topics.name: Tg, crystallization, morphology
topics.description: Glass transition temperature (Tg) is where amorphous polymers transition from rigid, glassy to soft, rubbery as temperature increases, marking onset of chain segment mobility. Below Tg, polymers are brittle; above, they're flexible. Crystallization in polymers is partial, never 100%, creating semicrystalline morphology with crystalline lamellae separated by amorphous regions. Spherulites are commo

topics.name: Toughness and impact
topics.description: Toughness measures energy absorption before fracture, combining strength and ductility. Charpy and Izod impact tests measure energy absorbed breaking notched specimens with pendulum, identifying ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) where behavior changes. Face-centered cubic metals remain ductile at low temperatures; body-centered cubic (steels) show DBTT. Impact toughness is critical

topics.name: Welding and allied processes
topics.description: Welding processes join materials, each with advantages: arc welding (versatile, economical), MIG/TIG (quality, control), laser/electron beam (precision, deep penetration), resistance welding (high-speed spot welding), friction welding (solid-state, dissimilar metals). Allied processes include brazing (lower temperature, filler wets surfaces) and soldering (electronics). Weld quality depends on pro

topics.name: Welding and joining processes
topics.description: Welding joins materials through coalescence using heat, pressure, or both, creating metallurgical bonds. Fusion welding (arc, gas, laser, electron beam) melts base metals with/without filler. Solid-state welding (friction, ultrasonic, diffusion) joins without melting. Brazing/soldering use lower-melting filler metals. Each process has advantages: arc welding is versatile and economical, laser weld

topics.name: Wire drawing
topics.description: Wire drawing produces wire, rod, and bar products by pulling material through progressively smaller conical dies, reducing cross-section while increasing length. The process is cold working (room temperature), work hardening the material while elongating grains in the drawing direction. Multiple drawing passes with intermediate annealing may be necessary for large reductions. Drawing force conside

topics.name: Work hardening and plasticity
topics.description: Work hardening (strain hardening) is the strengthening of metals through plastic deformation as dislocation density increases and dislocations obstruct each other's motion, requiring higher stress for continued deformation. Cold working processes (rolling, forging, drawing) at room temperature exploit work hardening to strengthen materials. The stress-strain curve's upward slope during plastic def

ME 340.name: Dynamics of Mechanical Systems
ME 340.description: Dynamic modeling of mechanical components and systems; time domain and frequency domain analysis of linear time invariant systems; multi-degree-of-freedom systems; linearization of nonlinear systems.
objectives: Be able to analyze various types of dynamic systems: mechanical, electrical, etc.
Be able to coordinate analytical concepts with experimental data seen on dynamic systems.
Also relate analytical concepts to everyday systems.
Utilize systems concepts to understand complicated systems from simpler subsystems.

topics.name: A nonlinear pendulum
topics.description: Unlike the linearized simple pendulum (sin θ ≈ θ), the nonlinear pendulum retains the full sine term in its equation of motion: θ̈ + (g/L)sin(θ) = 0. This seemingly minor change introduces rich behavior including amplitude-dependent frequency, non-sinusoidal oscillations for large angles, and chaotic motion under certain driving conditions. Studying nonlinear pendulums teaches the limitations of l

topics.name: Applications to beat generation and vibration absorbers
topics.description: Beat generation occurs when two closely-spaced natural frequencies are excited, creating periodic amplitude modulation where energy transfers between modes. This phenomenon can be exploited in vibration absorbers (tuned mass dampers) which are auxiliary mass-spring systems attached to a primary structure. When properly tuned, the absorber's natural frequency matches the problematic excitation freq

topics.name: Application to vibration isolation
topics.description: Vibration isolation involves designing systems to minimize the transmission of unwanted vibrations from a source to a sensitive component or from a component to its surroundings. By understanding frequency response, engineers can design isolation systems (typically using springs and dampers) that attenuate vibrations at problematic frequencies. Effective isolation requires operating above the syst

topics.name: A quarter-car model
topics.description: The quarter-car model simplifies vehicle suspension analysis by representing one wheel, suspension, and one-quarter of the vehicle mass as a two-degree-of-freedom system. The unsprung mass (wheel and suspension components) connects to ground through a tire spring, while the sprung mass (vehicle body portion) connects to the unsprung mass through the suspension spring and damper. This model capture

topics.name: Base excitation
topics.description: Base excitation refers to dynamic loading where the foundation or support structure of a system moves, inducing motion in the mounted components. Common in seismic analysis, vehicle dynamics, and vibration testing, base excitation problems analyze how vibrations transmit from a moving base to the system. The analysis differs from force excitation because the input is a displacement, velocity, or a

topics.name: Block diagrams and simulation
topics.description: Block diagrams provide graphical representations of dynamic systems using blocks for mathematical operations (integration, gain, summation) and arrows showing signal flow.
