This visual language simplifies understanding of complex system interconnections, feedback loops, and signal processing. Simulation involves numerically solving the equations represented by block diagrams to predict time-domain

topics.name: Boundary-value problems for cantilevered and clamped-clamped beams
topics.description: Beam vibration analysis requires solving the fourth-order PDE governing beam motion subject to boundary conditions at the ends. Cantilevered beams (fixed-free) have zero displacement and slope at the fixed end and zero moment and shear at the free end. Clamped-clamped beams (fixed-fixed) have zero displacement and slope at both ends. These boundary conditions profoundly affect natural frequencies

topics.name: Complex numbers
topics.description: Complex numbers, expressed as a + bi where i = √(-1), are essential mathematical tools in dynamics for representing sinusoidal motion and analyzing linear systems. Euler's formula (e^(iωt) = cos(ωt) + i sin(ωt)) allows compact representation of oscillatory behavior, converting differential equations into algebraic equations. Complex numbers naturally encode both magnitude and phase information, si

topics.name: Continuous systems
topics.description: Continuous systems have infinite degrees of freedom, requiring partial differential equations (PDEs) rather than ordinary differential equations for their description. Examples include vibrating strings, beams, membranes, and fluid flows where properties vary continuously over spatial dimensions. Unlike lumped-parameter models with discrete masses and springs, continuous systems exhibit spatial va

topics.name: Damping ratio and natural frequency
topics.description: The damping ratio (ζ) and natural frequency (ωn) are fundamental parameters characterizing second-order systems. Natural frequency represents the frequency at which an undamped system would oscillate freely, while the damping ratio quantifies the level of energy dissipation relative to critical damping. Together, these parameters determine whether a system exhibits underdamped (oscillatory), criti

topics.name: Dynamic simulation
topics.description: Dynamic simulation numerically solves system equations to predict behavior over time. Process: (1) formulate model (ODEs/DAEs), (2) specify initial conditions and parameters, (3) select integration method (Runge-Kutta, ode45), (4) run simulation, (5) analyze results. Tools: MATLAB/Simulink, Python, Modelica. Enables: design validation, parameter studies, scenario analysis, control testing. Conside

topics.name: Dynamic system classification
topics.description: Systems classified by properties: Linear vs. nonlinear (superposition applies or not). Time-invariant vs. time-varying (parameters constant or changing). Continuous vs. discrete time. Lumped vs. distributed parameters. Deterministic vs. stochastic. SISO (single input/output) vs. MIMO. Order: number of energy storage elements (determines ODE order). Stable vs. unstable. Understanding classification

topics.name: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
topics.description: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors are fundamental concepts from linear algebra crucial for analyzing MDOF systems. For a matrix A, eigenvalue λ and eigenvector v satisfy Av = λv. In dynamics, eigenvalues of the system matrix determine natural frequencies and decay rates, while eigenvectors define mode shapes. The eigenvalue problem transforms coupled differential equations into decoupled modal equation

topics.name: Equilibrium configurations
topics.description: Equilibrium configurations are system states where all velocities and accelerations are zero, representing balance between forces and moments. For conservative systems, equilibria occur at critical points of potential energy (where ∂V/∂qᵢ = 0 for all coordinates). Systems may have multiple equilibria, some stable (small perturbations decay) and others unstable (small perturbations grow). Finding e

topics.name: Exponentially decaying harmonic signals
topics.description: Exponentially decaying harmonic signals combine oscillatory and decay behavior, expressed as x(t) = Ae^(-ζωₙt)cos(ωdt + φ), where A is amplitude, ζωₙ is the decay rate, ωd is the damped natural frequency, and φ is phase. These signals characterize underdamped second-order system responses, appearing in vibrating structures, damped electrical circuits, and countless engineering applications. The ex

topics.name: Exponentially decaying signals
topics.description: Exponentially decaying signals are functions that decrease at a rate proportional to their current value, mathematically expressed as y(t) = y₀e^(-t/τ). These signals characterize first-order system responses and damped oscillations in second-order systems. The decay rate is determined by the time constant or damping coefficient, with larger values producing slower decay. Understanding exponential

topics.name: First and second order systems
topics.description: First and second order systems represent differential equations that govern the dynamic behavior of mechanical systems. First-order systems involve one energy storage element and are described by a single derivative, while second-order systems involve two energy storage elements and are described by second derivatives. These mathematical models predict how systems respond to inputs over time, with

topics.name: First-order systems
topics.description: First-order systems are governed by first-order ordinary differential equations of the form τ(dy/dt) + y = u(t), where the highest derivative is first order. These systems contain a single energy storage element (like thermal capacitance or fluid volume) and exhibit exponential approach to steady state without oscillation. Common examples include RC circuits, temperature diffusion, and fluid level

topics.name: Fluid flow
topics.description: Fluid flow modeling for dynamic systems: continuity (mass conservation), momentum (Navier-Stokes or Euler), energy equations. Simplified models: incompressible (ρ = constant, ∇·v = 0), inviscid (Euler), laminar pipe flow (Hagen-Poiseuille). Lumped parameter: resistance (pressure drop), capacitance (volume storage), inertance (fluid inertia). Fluid systems interact with mechanical (hydraulic actuat

topics.name: Free, step, and unit impulse responses of linear, second-order time-invariant systems
topics.description: These three canonical responses characterize second-order system behavior completely. Free response (initial condition response) shows natural oscillation at the damped natural frequency. Step response reveals how systems approach new equilibria, with characteristics like rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error depending on damping. Impulse response represents the system's fund

topics.name: Frequency domain analysis
topics.description: Frequency domain analysis examines system behavior by studying how systems respond to sinusoidal inputs at different frequencies, using Fourier transforms to convert time-domain signals into frequency-domain representations. This approach reveals how systems amplify or attenuate signals at various frequencies, making it invaluable for understanding filtering characteristics, resonance phenomena, a

topics.name: Frequency response
topics.description: Frequency response describes how a system's output amplitude and phase change relative to a sinusoidal input as the input frequency varies. Typically visualized using Bode plots (magnitude and phase versus frequency), frequency response reveals critical system characteristics including resonant frequencies, bandwidth, and stability margins. For linear time-invariant systems, the frequency response

topics.name: Harmonic excitation
topics.description: Harmonic excitation involves applying sinusoidal forcing functions to dynamic systems, mathematically represented as F(t) = F₀cos(ωt) or F₀sin(ωt). This is fundamental in vibration analysis because many real-world forces are periodic (engine vibrations, rotating machinery, electromagnetic forces) and because Fourier analysis allows decomposing arbitrary periodic inputs into harmonic components. Sy

topics.name: Harmonic excitation and resonance
topics.description: When continuous systems experience harmonic forcing at a natural frequency, resonance occurs with theoretically unbounded amplitude growth (bounded by damping in real systems). The spatial distribution of resonant vibration matches the corresponding mode shape. Distributed forcing (pressure, body forces) couples differently to various modes based on spatial correlation between forcing pattern and

topics.name: Heat transfer
topics.description: Heat transfer modeling: conduction (Fourier's law: q = -k∇T), convection (q = hA(Ts - T∞)), radiation (q = εσA(T⁴ - T∞⁴)). Energy balance: mcp dT/dt = Q̇ᵢₙ - Q̇ₒᵤₜ for lumped capacitance. Thermal resistance Rth = ΔT/Q̇ (analogous to electrical resistance). Thermal capacitance C = mcp. Time constant τ = RthC. Applications: temperature control, thermal management, coupled electromechanical systems.

topics.name: Impulse response and convolution integral
topics.description: The impulse response is the output of a system when subjected to an idealized instantaneous input (Dirac delta function). It completely characterizes a linear time-invariant system's behavior. The convolution integral uses the impulse response as a kernel to calculate the system's output for any arbitrary input by summing the weighted, time-shifted impulse responses. This powerful mathematical too

topics.name: Lagrange's equations
topics.description: Lagrange's equations provide a systematic, energy-based approach to deriving equations of motion for complex mechanical systems. By defining the Lagrangian L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy) and generalized coordinates qᵢ, the equations of motion follow from d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = Qᵢ, where Qᵢ are non-conservative generalized forces. This method automatically accounts for constraint forc

topics.name: Laplace transformation: properties, inverse transformation
topics.description: Laplace transform converts time-domain function f(t) to complex frequency domain: F(s) = ∫₀^∞ f(t)e^(-st)dt. Properties: linearity, differentiation (ℒ{f'} = sF(s) - f(0)), integration, shifting, convolution. Converts differential equations to algebraic equations. Inverse Laplace: f(t) = ℒ^(-1){F(s)} found via partial fractions, tables, or residue theorem. Advantages: handles initial conditions nat

topics.name: Linearization and stability
topics.description: Linearization approximates nonlinear systems by Taylor expansion about equilibrium points, retaining only first-order terms to obtain linear equations governing small perturbations. The resulting linear system's eigenvalues determine equilibrium stability: negative real parts indicate stable (perturbations decay), positive real parts indicate unstable (perturbations grow), and imaginary parts indi

topics.name: Linearization of nonlinear systems
topics.description: Linearization approximates nonlinear system near operating point (equilibrium) using Taylor series. For ẋ = f(x,u), expand about (x̄,ū): Δẋ ≈ A·Δx + B·Δu where A = ∂f/∂x|ₓ̄, B = ∂f/∂u|ₓ̄. Perturbation variables: Δx = x - x̄, Δu = u - ū. Linearized system analyzed using linear tools (eigenvalues, transfer functions, control design). Valid for small deviations. Local stability from linearization pre

topics.name: Matlab computations and graphing of real- and complex-valued functions
topics.description: MATLAB provides computational tools for numerical analysis and visualization of dynamic systems. Students learn to implement numerical integration schemes, eigenvalue solvers, and Fourier transforms while creating informative plots of time histories, frequency responses, and complex-plane representations. Graphing complex-valued functions requires understanding how to display four-dimensional info

topics.name: Measurement systems
topics.description: Measurement systems in dynamics utilize the principles of vibrating systems to sense and record physical quantities like displacement, velocity, and acceleration. Accelerometers, for example, are essentially spring-mass-damper systems where the relative motion between the mass and housing indicates the base acceleration. Understanding the frequency response of these instruments is crucial for accu

topics.name: Mechanical/electromechanical systems
topics.description: Mechanical systems: springs (F = kx), dampers (F = cẋ), masses (F = mẍ). Electromechanical: motors (torque from current), generators (voltage from motion), sensors (voltage from displacement/velocity). Coupling: electromagnetic force/torque relates electrical and mechanical domains. DC motor model: Vₐ = Raᵢₐ + La dᵢₐ/dt + Kₑω (electrical), T = Kₜᵢₐ = Jα + Bω + Tₗ (mechanical). Transducers convert

topics.name: Modal decompositions
topics.description: Modal decomposition expresses arbitrary motion of continuous systems as infinite series of modal contributions: x(y,t) = Σ qᵢ(t)φᵢ(y), where φᵢ(y) are mode shapes and qᵢ(t) are time-varying modal coordinates. This transformation converts the PDE into infinite uncoupled ordinary differential equations, one per mode, vastly simplifying analysis. Truncating to dominant modes provides accurate approxi

topics.name: Modeling of dynamic systems
topics.description: Dynamic system modeling derives mathematical descriptions from physical principles. Process: (1) identify components and interactions, (2) apply conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy), (3) write governing equations, (4) linearize if needed, (5) validate against experiments. Lumped parameter models use ODEs (discrete components). Distributed models use PDEs (continuous). Multi-domain systems (m

topics.name: Mode shapes and resonance
topics.description: Mode shapes are the spatial deformation patterns associated with each natural frequency in multi-DOF and continuous systems. At resonance, when excitation frequency matches a natural frequency, the system vibrates in the corresponding mode shape with theoretically infinite amplitude (in undamped systems) or very large amplitude (with damping). Understanding mode shapes is critical for avoiding des

topics.name: Multi-degree-of-freedom systems
topics.description: Multi-degree-of-freedom (MDOF) systems require multiple independent coordinates to describe their configuration completely. Unlike single-DOF systems described by scalar differential equations, MDOF systems involve coupled matrix differential equations where motion in one coordinate affects others. These systems exhibit multiple natural frequencies and mode shapes, with each mode representing a ch

topics.name: Natural frequencies and modal oscillations
topics.description: Natural frequencies are the discrete frequencies at which systems oscillate freely, determined by system properties (mass, stiffness, geometry) rather than external forcing. Modal oscillations refer to vibration in a single mode shape at the corresponding natural frequency. Each mode represents an independent oscillation pattern where all points move synchronously at the mode's natural frequency.

topics.name: Natural frequencies and normal modes
topics.description: Natural frequencies are the characteristic frequencies at which MDOF systems oscillate freely without external forcing, while normal modes (mode shapes) are the specific spatial patterns of motion at each natural frequency. During normal mode oscillation, all points move sinusoidally at the same frequency and pass through equilibrium simultaneously. Each mode shape represents an independent oscill

topics.name: Nonlinear systems
topics.description: Nonlinear systems contain terms that violate superposition, including products of variables, higher-order terms, or nonlinear functions. Unlike linear systems with predictable, scalable responses, nonlinear systems exhibit phenomena like amplitude-dependent frequency, multiple equilibria, limit cycles, chaos, and jump phenomena. Many real systems are inherently nonlinear (large-angle pendulums, ge

topics.name: Numerical experiments with a mechanical suspension
topics.description: This computational laboratory uses simulation to explore mechanical suspension system behavior, typically modeling a vehicle's spring-mass-damper system. Students conduct numerical experiments varying parameters like spring stiffness, damping coefficient, and mass to observe effects on ride comfort, handling, and vibration isolation. By comparing responses to different road inputs (bumps, potholes

topics.name: Partial fractions
topics.description: Partial fraction decomposition is a technique for breaking complex rational functions into sums of simpler fractions, essential for solving differential equations using Laplace transforms. In dynamics, this method allows engineers to invert transformed solutions back to the time domain by decomposing transfer functions into recognizable forms with known inverse transforms. Each partial fraction co

topics.name: Physical experiments with a cantilevered beam
topics.description: This laboratory provides hands-on experience with continuous system dynamics using a physical cantilevered beam (fixed at one end, free at the other). Students excite the beam and measure vibration to identify natural frequencies and visualize mode shapes experimentally. Techniques include impact testing, frequency sweeps, and modal testing with accelerometers or strain gauges. Comparing measured

topics.name: Physical experiments with a leaking tank and a hydraulic motor
topics.description: These laboratory experiments provide hands-on experience with first-order system behavior using fluid systems. A leaking tank demonstrates how liquid level decreases exponentially as flow rate depends on current level (height), exemplifying a first-order system with observable time constant. The hydraulic motor experiment shows how fluid flow drives rotation, with speed dynamics governed by first-

topics.name: Physical experiments with a single-degree-of-freedom spring-mass-damper system
topics.description: This laboratory experiment provides hands-on experience with canonical second-order systems using a physical apparatus with adjustable mass, spring stiffness, and damping. Students excite the system with various inputs (initial displacement, step force, impulse, harmonic forcing), measure responses, and identify parameters by comparing theory to measurements. Experiments demonstrate phenomena like

topics.name: Physical experiments with a two-degree-of-freedom spring-mass-damper system
topics.description: This laboratory extends single-DOF experiments to coupled systems with two masses, springs, and dampers. Students observe phenomena unique to MDOF systems including modal behavior, beat generation, and energy transfer between degrees of freedom. Experiments demonstrate how coupling coordinates leads to interactions, how two natural frequencies emerge from one-DOF components, and how mode shapes ma

topics.name: Principles of conservation - mass, energy
topics.description: Conservation principles form foundation for dynamic system modeling. Mass conservation: dm/dt = ṁᵢₙ - ṁₒᵤₜ (continuity equation). Energy conservation (first law): dE/dt = Q̇ - Ẇ + Σ(ṁh)ᵢₙ - Σ(ṁh)ₒᵤₜ, where Q̇ is heat transfer, Ẇ is power output, h is specific enthalpy. For closed systems: dE/dt = Q̇ - Ẇ. Mechanical energy: kinetic + potential. Momentum conservation: F = dp/dt. These principles gen

topics.name: Second-order systems
topics.description: Second-order systems are governed by second-order differential equations of the form: ẍ + 2ζωₙẋ + ωₙ²x = f(t), involving acceleration, velocity, and position terms. These systems contain two energy storage elements (like mass and spring) and can exhibit oscillatory behavior. Characterized by natural frequency ωₙ and damping ratio ζ, second-order systems show diverse responses: underdamped (oscilla

topics.name: Simulation and physical experiments with a double pendulum
topics.description: The double pendulum (two pendulums connected in series) exemplifies rich nonlinear dynamics despite simple construction. For small angles, linearization yields four natural modes, but large-angle motion exhibits chaos - extreme sensitivity to initial conditions producing unpredictable long-term behavior. Students simulate the system numerically and compare to physical experiments, observing how sm

topics.name: Simulations with a finite-element model
topics.description: Finite element analysis (FEA) discretizes continuous systems into finite elements (small regions) connected at nodes, converting PDEs into large systems of ODEs solvable computationally. For beam vibration, elements represent beam segments with displacement and rotation DOFs at each node. FEA captures complex geometries, material variations, and boundary conditions impractical for analytical solut

topics.name: Simulink realizations
topics.description: Simulink is MATLAB's graphical programming environment for modeling, simulating, and analyzing dynamic systems using block diagrams. Students construct virtual system models by connecting blocks representing mathematical operations, creating executable simulations without writing code. Simulink automatically solves the underlying differential equations numerically and provides rich visualization t

topics.name: Solutions of differential equations by laplace transform
topics.description: Laplace transform method: (1) transform differential equation, (2) solve algebraic equation for F(s), (3) inverse transform to get f(t). Initial conditions automatically incorporated. Works for linear ODEs with constant coefficients. Example: mẍ + cẋ + kx = f(t) becomes ms²X(s) + csX(s) + kX(s) = F(s) + initial condition terms. Solve for X(s), then x(t) = ℒ^(-1){X(s)}. Particularly useful for piec

topics.name: State(phase) space representation
topics.description: State space representation uses first-order differential equations: ẋ = Ax + Bu (state equation), y = Cx + Du (output equation), where x is state vector (position, velocity, etc.), u is input, y is output. State dimension n equals system order. Matrices A, B, C, D characterize system. Advantages: handles MIMO (multiple input/multiple output), nonlinear extension, modern control theory foundation,

topics.name: Steady-state response
topics.description: Steady-state response is the system behavior that persists after initial transients decay, representing the long-term response pattern. For linear systems under harmonic excitation, the steady-state response is sinusoidal at the forcing frequency with amplitude and phase determined by the system's frequency response function. The steady state is independent of initial conditions, depending only on

topics.name: System identification
topics.description: System identification involves determining mathematical models of dynamic systems from experimental input-output data. Rather than deriving models from first principles, engineers apply known inputs, measure outputs, and use parameter estimation techniques to identify system parameters like time constants, natural frequencies, and damping ratios. Common approaches include time-domain fitting of st

topics.name: Time- and frequency-domain block diagrams with integrators amplifiers
topics.description: Block diagrams can represent systems in both time domain (using integrators ∫dt, differentiators d/dt, and gains) and frequency domain (using transfer functions and complex gains). Integrators are fundamental building blocks converting acceleration to velocity to displacement, while amplifiers scale signals. Understanding the equivalence between time-domain differential equations and frequency-dom

topics.name: Time constant
topics.description: The time constant is a characteristic parameter of first-order systems that quantifies how quickly the system responds to changes. Mathematically denoted as τ (tau), it represents the time required for the system's response to reach approximately 63.2% of its final steady-state value. After about five time constants, the system is considered to have reached its steady state. The time constant is i

topics.name: Time domain analysis of linear time invariant systems
topics.description: LTI system analysis in time domain: impulse response h(t) = ℒ^(-1){G(s)} characterizes system completely. Output y(t) = h(t)*u(t) (convolution). Step response s(t) = ∫h(τ)dτ. Transient response: natural modes from poles (exponentials, damped sinusoids). Steady-state response to sinusoidal input: magnitude and phase from frequency response. Performance metrics: rise time, settling time, overshoot,

topics.name: Transfer functions - poles and zeroes
topics.description: Transfer function G(s) = Y(s)/U(s) relates output Y to input U in Laplace domain (zero initial conditions). Rational function: G(s) = N(s)/D(s). Poles are roots of denominator D(s) = 0, govern stability and transient response. Zeros are roots of numerator N(s) = 0. System stable if all poles in left half-plane (Re(s)

topics.name: Under-, critically-, and over-damped systems
topics.description: These three damping regimes categorize second-order system behavior based on damping ratio ζ. Underdamped systems (ζ

ME 370.name: Mechanical Design I
ME 370.description: Kinematics and dynamics of machinery, including introduction to user-centered design and design thinking, analytical and computer-aided design of kinematics, dynamic force analysis, principle of virtual work, cam and gear design, and balancing. Project-based learning of multi-mechanism system design, analysis, fabrication, and evaluation.
objectives: Understand mechanical machines as assemblies of kinematic mechanisms.
Perform position, velocity, acceleration, dynamic force, and balancing analyses on planar mechanisms.
Design planar mechanisms that execute desired motions with acceptable dynamic forces.
Understand how truss and frame structures carry static loads, via tension, compression, bending, and torsion.
Design simple truss and frame structures that have adequate load-carrying ability and stiffness.
Analyze truss and frame structures for stress and deflection, using finite element methods.
Present design and analysis of mechanical machines or structures in written and/or oral reports.

topics.name: Balancing of mechanisms
topics.description: Balancing eliminates or reduces undesired inertia forces and moments in mechanisms through strategic mass redistribution or counterweight addition. Static balancing addresses forces (center of mass fixed), while dynamic balancing addresses both forces and moments. Unbalanced mechanisms generate vibrations, noise, bearing wear, and structural fatigue. Balancing techniques include adding counterweig

topics.name: Cam design
topics.description: Cam design creates mechanical devices converting rotary motion to oscillating or reciprocating motion through shaped profiles. Design involves selecting cam type (plate, cylindrical), follower type (flat, roller, knife-edge), and creating cam profile producing desired follower motion (displacement, velocity, acceleration). Common motion programs include constant velocity, parabolic, cycloidal, and

topics.name: Critical design reviews
topics.description: Critical design reviews are formal evaluations where teams present designs to stakeholders, experts, or instructors for feedback and approval before proceeding. Reviews assess technical feasibility, requirement satisfaction, risk identification, resource adequacy, and schedule realism. Participants ask challenging questions, identify flaws, suggest improvements, and verify readiness for next phase

topics.name: Design thinking
topics.description: Design thinking is a human-centered, iterative problem-solving methodology emphasizing empathy, ideation, prototyping, and testing. The process involves understanding user needs through observation and interviews (empathize), defining the core problem (define), generating diverse solutions (ideate), building tangible representations (prototype), and testing with users (test). Design thinking encou

topics.name: Design thinking: empathy
topics.description: Empathy in design thinking means deeply understanding users' experiences, needs, frustrations, and contexts through observation, interviews, and immersion. It involves suspending assumptions, actively listening, observing body language and environment, and asking probing questions. Empathy tools include empathy maps (thinking, feeling, seeing, saying), user journey maps, and personas. The goal is

topics.name: Engineering design competition
topics.description: Engineering design competitions challenge students to design, build, and test solutions meeting specific performance criteria within constraints (time, budget, materials, rules). Competitions develop practical skills: project management, teamwork, fabrication, testing, iteration, and presentation. Common formats include robot competitions, bridge building, efficiency challenges, and product design

topics.name: Graphical linkage synthesis
topics.description: Graphical linkage synthesis uses geometric constructions to design linkages achieving specified motion requirements, such as guiding a point through prescribed positions or coordinating input-output relationships. Methods include two-position synthesis (finding linkage passing through two poses), three-position synthesis, and function generation (coordinating angular relationships). Graphical tech

topics.name: Human centered design project
topics.description: Human-centered design projects apply design thinking principles to real-world problems, focusing intensely on end-user needs, contexts, and experiences. Projects involve extensive user research (interviews, observations, immersion), defining meaningful problem statements, ideating numerous solutions, creating prototypes, and testing with actual users. The process iterates based on user feedback, e

topics.name: Ideation methods
topics.description: Ideation methods generate numerous diverse solution concepts before converging on promising directions. Techniques include brainstorming (quantity over quality, defer judgment, build on others' ideas), SCAMPER (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to other uses, Eliminate, Reverse), mind mapping (visual idea association), sketching, analogy thinking, and worst possible idea (then inverting). Ef

topics.name: Impact dynamics
topics.description: Impact dynamics analyzes collisions between mechanism components or with external objects, involving rapid force exchanges over short time periods. Analysis uses impulse-momentum principles, coefficient of restitution (energy loss during impact), and sometimes energy methods. Impacts can be elastic (energy conserved), plastic (maximum energy loss), or partially elastic. Understanding impact dynami

topics.name: Kinematic fundamentals
topics.description: Kinematic fundamentals cover basic concepts for analyzing mechanism motion: degrees of freedom (independent motions), mobility (Grübler's equation), kinematic pairs (joints like revolute, prismatic, cam), links (rigid bodies), and kinematic chains (interconnected links). Understanding these building blocks enables classifying mechanisms, determining mobility, and selecting appropriate joints for d

topics.name: Linkage dynamics
topics.description: Linkage dynamics specifically analyzes forces in linkage mechanisms, determining bearing reactions, required input torques, and internal forces considering link masses and inertias. Analysis uses Newton-Euler approach applying force and moment balance to each link, or energy methods like Lagrangian mechanics. Inertia forces from link accelerations create dynamic forces exceeding static loads, requ

topics.name: Low-fidelity prototyping
topics.description: Low-fidelity prototyping creates quick, inexpensive, rough representations of ideas using simple materials (paper, cardboard, foam, LEGO) enabling rapid iteration and user feedback. Low-fidelity prototypes communicate concepts without investing in polish, allowing testing multiple ideas quickly and failing cheaply. They help stakeholders visualize concepts, identify flaws early, and refine underst

topics.name: Machine dynamics
topics.description: Machine dynamics analyzes forces and torques in moving machinery, relating motion (kinematics) to causing forces using Newton's laws and Euler equations. This includes determining input torques/forces required for desired motion, computing bearing reactions, identifying maximum forces for component sizing, and analyzing vibrations. Machine dynamics considers both rigid body effects (mass, inertia)

topics.name: Mechanisms and gears
topics.description: Mechanisms and gears transform motion and transmit power through interconnected rigid bodies. Mechanisms include linkages (four-bar, slider-crank), cams, and gear trains. Gears provide precise speed ratios, high efficiency, and compact power transmission. Gear types include spur (parallel shafts), helical (smoother, higher capacity), bevel (intersecting shafts), and worm (large ratios, non-backdri

topics.name: Motion input
topics.description: Motion input involves specifying and analyzing how mechanisms are driven - typically through motors, actuators, or manual inputs providing angular or linear displacement, velocity, or acceleration. Understanding input characteristics enables matching prime movers to mechanisms, determining required power, and designing control strategies. Input types include constant velocity (motors with flywheel

topics.name: Planar kinematics
topics.description: Planar kinematics studies motion of mechanisms constrained to move in a single plane, analyzing position, velocity, and acceleration of links and joints without considering forces. This includes determining displacement paths, angular velocities, linear velocities, and accelerations of mechanism components. Planar mechanisms are common in machinery (linkages, cams, gears) where motion occurs in pa

topics.name: Position, velocity, and acceleration analysis
topics.description: Position, velocity, and acceleration analysis determines kinematic quantities at all points in a mechanism for any given input. Position analysis solves for link angles and point locations using loop-closure equations or graphical methods. Velocity analysis uses differentiation, instant centers, or velocity polygons. Acceleration analysis requires second derivatives or acceleration polygons, accou

topics.name: Programming labs using python to compute kinematics and dynamic force analysis
topics.description: Programming labs teach using Python for computational mechanism analysis, automating tedious calculations for position, velocity, acceleration, and forces. Students implement algorithms for kinematic analysis (solving loop equations, computing velocities), dynamic analysis (applying Newton-Euler equations), and visualization (animating mechanism motion, plotting results). Programming enables analy

topics.name: Rotating machinery
topics.description: Rotating machinery encompasses devices with primary rotating components: turbines, pumps, motors, generators, compressors, and machine tools. Analysis includes understanding rotordynamics (critical speeds, whirl, vibration), balancing (static and dynamic), bearing loads, power transmission, and efficiency. Rotating machinery requires attention to centrifugal effects, gyroscopic moments, torsional

topics.name: Virtual work approach
topics.description: The virtual work approach analyzes machine dynamics using energy principles rather than force balance. Virtual work is the work done by forces through virtual (imaginary, infinitesimal) displacements consistent with constraints. For systems in equilibrium (including dynamic equilibrium using d'Alembert's principle), virtual work equals zero. This method is powerful for mechanisms with many compone

TAM 412.name: Intermediate Dynamics
TAM 412.description: Lagrangian mechanics of dynamical systems with an emphasis on vibrations; constraints and generalized coordinates; motion in accelerating frames; conservation laws and invariance of the Lagrangian; particle motion in one dimension, the two-body problem, and central-force motion; free and forced vibration of linearized single-degree-of-freedom and multi-degree-of-freedom discrete systems; weakly nonlinear vibrations; parametric resonance; introduction to Hamiltonian dynamics; rigid-body motions.
objectives: Be able to identify suitable generalized coordinates for configuration-constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies.

Be able to express the kinetic energy, potential energy and Lagrangian of a system of particles and rigid bodies in terms of the chosen generalized coordinates and corresponding generalized velocities and the inertial properties for each body (which may require additional computations to determine).
Be able to write down appropriate configuration constraint equations.
Be able to deduce Lagrange’s equations of motion from the Lagrangian treating the applicable constraints appropriately, possibly through the introduction of Lagrange multipliers.
Be able to numerically integrate the equations of motion for configuration-constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies.

Be able to set up the equations of motion for small, free and forced, oscillations about an equilibrium for both single- and multi-degree-of-freedom systems, and calculate the linearized characteristic frequencies and normal modes of free oscillations.
Be able to identify ignorable coordinates and their associated integrals of the motion.
Be able to study forced resonances of single- and multi-degree-of-freedom systems and design for vibration isolation.
Be able to analytically and/or numerically compute the free and forced response of single-and multi-degree-of-freedom oscillators subject to general transient or periodic forces.

topics.name: Angular momentum-rotation
topics.description: When the Lagrangian is rotationally symmetric about an axis (∂L/∂θ = 0), the corresponding angular momentum component about that axis is conserved. This angular momentum-rotation relationship states that rotational invariance implies angular momentum conservation. For example, central force problems (like planetary orbits) have spherical symmetry, conserving total angular momentum. Figure skaters

topics.name: Angular velocities
topics.description: Angular velocity ω is a vector describing instantaneous rotation rate and axis: magnitude gives rotation speed (rad/s), direction indicates rotation axis via right-hand rule. For rigid bodies, angular velocity describes how the body's orientation changes: v = ω × r relates any point's velocity to its position r from the rotation axis. Angular velocities add vectorially when rotations combine, thou

topics.name: Automated formulation of configuration constraints
topics.description: Systematic computational methods for deriving constraint equations from mechanical system descriptions, typically used in multibody dynamics software. Given joint types (revolute, prismatic, spherical, etc.) and connectivity, algorithms automatically generate holonomic and nonholonomic constraint equations. This automation enables rapid model development, reduces human error, and handles complex s

topics.name: Configuration constraints and redundant coordinates
topics.description: Configuration constraints are equations relating generalized coordinates, often arising when using more coordinates than necessary for convenience. Redundant (dependent) coordinates exceed the minimum needed to specify configuration, with constraint equations relating them. Using redundant coordinates can simplify kinematics at the cost of introducing constraint forces or Lagrange multipliers in d

topics.name: Conservative systems
topics.description: Conservative systems have forces derivable from a potential energy function V, where F = -∇V. Such forces are path-independent: work depends only on endpoints, not trajectory. In conservative systems, total mechanical energy E = T + V is conserved. This conservation law provides first integrals of motion, reducing problem complexity and enabling energy methods for solution. Common conservative for

topics.name: Coordinate systems and transformation equations
topics.description: Coordinate systems provide frameworks for describing positions and orientations using numbers. Common systems include Cartesian (x,y,z), cylindrical (r,θ,z), spherical (r,θ,φ), and body-fixed coordinates. Transformation equations convert quantities between coordinate systems, essential when problems have natural symmetries in non-Cartesian systems. Transformations involve rotation matrices, Jacobi

topics.name: D'alembert's principle of virtual work
topics.description: D'Alembert's principle extends the virtual work principle to dynamics by treating inertial terms (ma) as forces. For a system, the principle states: Σ(F_i - m_i a_i)·δr_i = 0 for all virtual displacements δr_i consistent with constraints. This formulation eliminates constraint forces (since they do no virtual work) and provides the foundation for deriving Lagrange's equations. The principle transf

topics.name: Derivation of hamilton's principle of least action
topics.description: Hamilton's principle of least action states that the actual path taken by a system between two configurations minimizes (or makes stationary) the action integral S = ∫L dt, where L is the Lagrangian. The principle derives from d'Alembert's principle through calculus of variations, showing that requiring δS = 0 for all variations yields Euler-Lagrange equations. This variational principle provides

topics.name: Dynamically equivalent bodies
topics.description: Dynamically equivalent bodies have identical mass, center of mass location, and inertia tensor, producing identical responses to applied forces and moments despite different physical appearances. This equivalence allows replacing complex mass distributions with simpler equivalent systems (like point masses and massless rods) for analysis. The concept is powerful for model reduction: a connecting r

topics.name: Eigenvalue and eigenvector decompositions of symmetric matrices
topics.description: Symmetric matrices (like mass and stiffness matrices in dynamics) can be diagonalized by orthogonal transformations: K = ΦΛΦᵀ, where Φ contains orthonormal eigenvectors (mode shapes) and Λ is diagonal with eigenvalues (related to natural frequencies). This spectral decomposition reveals fundamental system properties: eigenvalues determine stability and resonance, eigenvectors define independent os

topics.name: Elimination of ignorable coordinates using routhians
topics.description: The Routhian function R is a hybrid Lagrangian-Hamiltonian formulation eliminating ignorable (cyclic) coordinates by Legendre transformation with respect to their velocities only: R = L - Σpᵢq̇ᵢ (sum over cyclic coordinates), where pᵢ are constant conjugate momenta. The Routhian depends on remaining coordinates and velocities plus cyclic coordinate momenta as parameters. Euler-Lagrange equations i

topics.name: Energy-time
topics.description: When the Lagrangian has no explicit time dependence (∂L/∂t = 0), representing time-translation symmetry, the Hamiltonian H = Σpᵢq̇ᵢ - L (total energy for natural systems) is conserved. This energy-time relationship connects temporal invariance to energy conservation: if physics is the same today as tomorrow, energy is conserved. For conservative systems with velocity-independent constraints, H = T

topics.name: Euler angles
topics.description: Euler angles (typically φ, θ, ψ) are three successive rotations about specified axes that transform from inertial to body-fixed coordinates, parametrizing arbitrary 3D orientations. Multiple conventions exist (e.g., 3-1-3, 3-2-1 sequences), each with advantages for specific applications. Euler angles provide intuitive orientation description but suffer from singularities (gimbal lock) where two ax

topics.name: Euler-lagrange's equation of motion for unconstrained and constrained systems of particles and rigid bodies
topics.description: The Euler-Lagrange equations provide systematic derivation of equations of motion from the Lagrangian L = T - V: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = Qᵢ, where qᵢ are generalized coordinates and Qᵢ are non-conservative generalized forces. For constrained systems, Lagrange multipliers λ_j enforce constraint equations: the augmented form includes additional terms. These equations automatically yield correct equ

topics.name: Euler-lagrange's equations in terms of lagrangians
topics.description: The compact formulation of Euler-Lagrange equations using the Lagrangian L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy) for conservative systems: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0. This elegant formulation automatically incorporates conservative forces through the potential energy term, requiring only scalar energy functions rather than vector force analysis. The Lagrangian approach exploits variational prin

topics.name: Forces of constraint
topics.description: Constraint forces maintain system constraints (like a ball staying on a surface or a door rotating about hinges), doing no work through virtual displacements consistent with constraints. In Lagrangian mechanics, generalized coordinates automatically satisfy constraints, making constraint forces unnecessary for equations of motion. However, constraint forces can be calculated post-solution using La

topics.name: Foundations of hamiltonian mechanics
topics.description: Hamiltonian mechanics reformulates dynamics using generalized coordinates q and conjugate momenta p (instead of coordinates and velocities), with the Hamiltonian H(q,p,t) = T + V for natural systems representing total energy. Hamilton's equations are first-order: q̇ = ∂H/∂p, ṗ = -∂H/∂q, doubling variables but gaining symmetry and geometric structure. This formulation reveals phase space structure,

topics.name: Foundations of lagrangian mechanics
topics.description: Lagrangian mechanics provides an alternative, energy-based formulation of classical mechanics using generalized coordinates and the Lagrangian function L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy). Instead of analyzing forces and accelerations in Cartesian coordinates, Lagrangian mechanics derives equations of motion through variational principles (principle of least action). This approach automatic

topics.name: Free and forced vibrations
topics.description: Free vibrations occur without external forcing after initial disturbance, characterized by natural frequencies and decay rates determined by system properties. The motion is a superposition of normal modes: q(t) = Σcᵢφᵢe^(λᵢt), where φᵢ are mode shapes and λᵢ are eigenvalues. Forced vibrations result from sustained external excitation, with steady-state response depending on forcing frequency rela

topics.name: Free oscillations
topics.description: Unforced motion resulting from initial conditions in conservative or damped systems. For linear systems, free oscillations are superpositions of normal modes with amplitudes and phases determined by initial conditions. In undamped systems, oscillations persist indefinitely at natural frequencies. Damping causes exponential decay, with decay rates determined by eigenvalue real parts. Analyzing free

topics.name: Frictional contact
topics.description: Analysis of systems with friction forces at contact surfaces, including Coulomb friction (F_friction ≤ μN, opposing motion), stiction (preventing motion below threshold), and rolling resistance. Friction introduces nonlinearity and potential discontinuities (stiction transitions, stick-slip motion), complicating analysis. Contact dynamics requires determining contact states (sliding, sticking, sep

topics.name: Fundamentals of rigid body mechanics
topics.description: Rigid body mechanics analyzes objects with fixed internal geometry (distances between points remain constant). The fundamental principle separates motion into translation of the center of mass (governed by ΣF = ma_cm) and rotation about the center of mass (governed by ΣM = Iω̇). The moment of inertia tensor I relates angular acceleration to applied moments, depending on mass distribution and rotat

topics.name: Generalized coordinates and degrees of freedom
topics.description: Generalized coordinates are independent variables completely specifying a system's configuration, chosen to exploit problem structure and automatically satisfy constraints. The minimum number of generalized coordinates equals the system's degrees of freedom. Unlike Cartesian coordinates fixed to spatial directions, generalized coordinates can be angles, arc lengths, or other parameters natural to

topics.name: Generalized forces
topics.description: Generalized forces are quantities conjugate to generalized coordinates in expressing virtual work: δW = ΣQᵢδqᵢ. They have units of energy per unit coordinate change - forces for length coordinates, torques for angular coordinates. Generalized forces incorporate all applied forces, calculated by taking the virtual work and extracting coefficients of virtual displacements. This concept enables syste

topics.name: Generalized momenta
topics.description: Generalized momenta p_i = ∂L/∂q̇_i are quantities conjugate to generalized coordinates in Lagrangian mechanics, representing 'motion content' associated with coordinate q_i. For Cartesian coordinates, p reduces to linear momentum; for angular coordinates, to angular momentum. When a coordinate is ignorable (absent from L), its conjugate momentum is conserved. Generalized momenta form the basis for

topics.name: Generalized velocities and virtual displacements
topics.description: Generalized velocities are time derivatives of generalized coordinates, describing system motion in configuration space. Virtual displacements are infinitesimal, instantaneous changes in coordinates consistent with constraints but not necessarily physically realizable (frozen time, constraints maintained). Virtual displacements form the basis for variational principles: for a system in equilibrium

topics.name: Hamilton's canonical equations
topics.description: Hamilton's canonical equations are a pair of first-order differential equations describing system dynamics: q̇ᵢ = ∂H/∂pᵢ and ṗᵢ = -∂H/∂qᵢ, where H is the Hamiltonian, q are generalized coordinates, and p are conjugate momenta. These equations are symmetric, revealing phase space structure and symplectic geometry. They double the number of variables compared to Lagrange's second-order equations but

topics.name: Ignorable coordinates and conservation laws of lagrangian mechanics
topics.description: Ignorable (cyclic) coordinates are generalized coordinates absent from the Lagrangian (∂L/∂qᵢ = 0), though their velocities appear. For such coordinates, the conjugate generalized momentum pᵢ = ∂L/∂q̇ᵢ is conserved. This connection between symmetries (coordinate absence) and conservation laws exemplifies Noether's theorem. Common examples: absence of θ in rotationally symmetric systems conserves a

topics.name: Inverse dynamics
topics.description: Inverse dynamics determines forces and torques required to produce specified motion, opposite to forward dynamics (finding motion from forces). Given trajectory q(t), inverse dynamics computes F = Mq̈ + C(q,q̇)q̇ + K(q) required to generate it. This is crucial for robot control (computing joint torques for desired motion), animation (making motion look physically realistic), and biomechanics (esti

topics.name: Kinetic energy
topics.description: Kinetic energy for rigid bodies includes both translational and rotational contributions: T = ½mv_cm² + ½ω·Iω, where the first term is center-of-mass translation and the second is rotation about the center of mass. The rotational term involves the inertia tensor and angular velocity. This energy decomposition simplifies analysis using the work-energy theorem and is central to Lagrangian mechanics

topics.name: Lagrange multipliers
topics.description: Lagrange multipliers are additional variables λ_j introduced to enforce constraint equations in optimization or dynamics problems. In Lagrangian mechanics with constraints g_j(q) = 0, multipliers represent constraint forces (forces of reaction) and enter augmented Lagrange equations. The method converts constrained problems into unconstrained ones with additional variables, enabling systematic sol

topics.name: Legendre transformations
topics.description: Legendre transformations are mathematical operations that convert between different but equivalent formulations of physics problems by changing independent variables. In mechanics, they transform the Lagrangian L(q,q̇,t) depending on coordinates and velocities into the Hamiltonian H(q,p,t) depending on coordinates and momenta. The transformation is defined as H = Σpᵢq̇ᵢ - L, where pᵢ = ∂L/∂q̇ᵢ. Th

topics.name: Matrix representations of equations of motion
topics.description: Expressing equations of motion in matrix form: Mq̈ + Cq̇ + Kq = F, where M is the mass matrix, C is damping, K is stiffness, q is the coordinate vector, and F is the force vector. This compact notation facilitates numerical solution, eigenvalue analysis, and system identification. Mass matrices may be diagonal (uncoupled inertias) or full (coupled through kinetic energy). Stiffness matrices arise

topics.name: Moments and ellipsoids of inertia
topics.description: Moments of inertia quantify mass distribution relative to rotation axes: I = ∫r²dm, determining rotational resistance. The inertia tensor is a symmetric 3×3 matrix containing moments (diagonal elements) and products of inertia (off-diagonal elements), relating angular momentum to angular velocity: L = Iω. The inertia ellipsoid provides geometric visualization: principal axes are ellipsoid axes whe

topics.name: Momentum-translation
topics.description: When the Lagrangian doesn't depend on a Cartesian coordinate (spatial translation symmetry, ∂L/∂xᵢ = 0), the corresponding momentum component pᵢ is conserved. This momentum-translation relationship states that if physics is invariant under spatial translation (no preferred position), linear momentum is conserved. For example, in the absence of external forces, a system's center-of-mass momentum is

topics.name: Moving frames
topics.description: Moving (non-inertial) reference frames accelerate or rotate relative to inertial frames. Dynamics in moving frames introduces fictitious forces: centrifugal, Coriolis, Euler, and translational inertial forces. These apparent forces arise from the frame's motion, not physical interactions, but must be included for correct force balance. Moving frames are essential for analyzing Earth-based systems

topics.name: Normal coordinates and modal analysis
topics.description: Normal coordinates are linear combinations of generalized coordinates that decouple equations of motion, transforming coupled oscillators into independent single-DOF systems. The transformation q = Φη uses modal matrix Φ whose columns are normalized mode shapes, converting Mq̈ + Kq = 0 to η̈ + Ω²η = 0, where Ω is a diagonal matrix of natural frequencies. Modal analysis uses this decoupling to solv

topics.name: Potential energy and relation to generalized forces
topics.description: Potential energy V is stored energy depending on configuration: gravitational (mgh), elastic (½kx²), etc. The relation to generalized forces is Q_i = -∂V/∂q_i: conservative forces derive from potential energy's negative gradient with respect to generalized coordinates. This relationship allows replacing explicit force analysis with energy function specification in Lagrangian mechanics. For conserv

topics.name: Small oscillations about positions of equilibrium
topics.description: Analyzing motion near stable equilibria by Taylor-expanding potential energy to second order: V ≈ V₀ + ½ΣΣ(∂²V/∂qᵢ∂qⱼ)qᵢqⱼ, where equilibrium is at qᵢ = 0 and first derivatives vanish. The resulting linearized equations of motion become coupled harmonic oscillators, analyzed through eigenvalue problems to find normal modes and natural frequencies. This approach predicts small-amplitude vibrations

topics.name: Small oscillations about steady-state motion
topics.description: Analyzing vibrations about non-equilibrium steady motions (like spinning shafts or orbiting satellites) by linearizing about the steady trajectory. The approach parallels small oscillations about equilibrium but accounts for gyroscopic effects and time-varying linearization. Perturbation coordinates describe deviations from steady motion: q = q_steady + δq. Linearized equations often have constant

topics.name: Stability
topics.description: Stability characterizes whether small perturbations from equilibrium or steady motion decay (stable), persist (neutrally stable), or grow (unstable). Linear stability analysis examines eigenvalues of the linearized system: negative real parts indicate stability, positive indicate instability. For equilibria, stability relates to potential energy: local minima are stable, maxima unstable, saddles c

topics.name: Work and kinetic energy
topics.description: Work is energy transferred by forces acting through displacements: W = ∫F·ds. Kinetic energy T represents energy of motion: T = ½mv² for particles, more complex for rigid bodies including rotational kinetic energy. The work-energy theorem states that net work equals change in kinetic energy. In Lagrangian mechanics, kinetic energy's functional dependence on velocities and positions is central to f

MATH 463.name: Statistics and Probability I
MATH 463.description: Introduction to mathematical statistics that develops probability as needed; includes the calculus of probability, random variables, expectation, distribution functions, central limit theorem, point estimation, confidence intervals, and hypothesis testing. Offers a basic one-term introduction to statistics and also prepares students for STAT 410.
objectives: Students will gain an ability to apply knowledge of probability and statistics to problems in science and engineering.
Students in Computer Science will gain an ability to analyze a problem, and identify and define the computing requirements appropriate to its solution using probability and statistics.

ME 320.name: Heat Transfer
ME 320.description: Principles and application of heat transfer by conduction, convection, and thermal radiation.
objectives: Be able to analyze temperature distributions, heat fluxes and heat transfer rates in conduction heat transfer.
Be able to analyze flow and convective heat transfer rates in simple geometries.
Be able to determine heat transfer rates by radiation.
Have a working knowledge of temperature measurement devices.
Be able to analyze and document methodology and results of laboratory experiments.
Be able to design thermal equipment (e.g., heat exchangers) considering performance, cost, and durability.

topics.name: Conduction
topics.description: Conduction is heat transfer through matter by molecular interactions and energy carrier diffusion (electrons in metals, phonons in insulators) without bulk motion. Governed by Fourier's law q'' = -k∇T, thermal conductivity k is a material property varying with temperature and structure. Conduction analysis solves the heat diffusion equation with appropriate boundary/initial conditions for steady o

topics.name: Convection
topics.description: Convection is heat transfer between a surface and moving fluid, combining molecular conduction with bulk fluid motion (advection). Characterized by the heat transfer coefficient h in Newton's law of cooling (q = hA(T_s - T_∞)), convection depends on fluid properties (viscosity, conductivity, density), flow velocity and regime (laminar/turbulent), and geometry. Natural convection arises from buoyan

topics.name: Directional characteristics of thermal radiation
topics.description: Thermal radiation emission and reflection vary with direction relative to the surface normal. Directional (hemispherical) emissivity ε(θ) describes emission into a specific direction at angle θ. Diffuse surfaces emit/reflect equally in all directions following Lambert's cosine law (intensity proportional to cos θ). Specular surfaces reflect like mirrors with incident and reflected angles equal. Re

topics.name: Extended surface heat transfer
topics.description: Extended surfaces (fins) are appendages attached to primary surfaces to enhance heat transfer by increasing surface area exposed to the surrounding fluid. Fin analysis determines temperature distribution and heat transfer rate using an energy balance that accounts for conduction along the fin and convection from the surface. Fin efficiency (ηf) and fin effectiveness (εf) quantify performance. Comm

topics.name: External and internal flows
topics.description: External flows involve fluid moving over external surfaces (flat plates, cylinders, spheres) without geometric confinement, developing velocity and thermal boundary layers from leading edges. Internal flows occur within confined geometries (pipes, ducts) where flow is constrained by walls. For internal flows, developing regions transition to fully developed flow where velocity and temperature prof

topics.name: Graybody exchange
topics.description: Graybody radiation exchange analyzes heat transfer between surfaces in an enclosure where surfaces are gray (wavelength-independent properties) and diffuse. The radiosity method tracks leaving radiation (emitted plus reflected) and incident radiation on each surface. Net radiation heat transfer depends on emissivities, surface temperatures, view factors, and enclosure geometry. Two-surface enclosu

topics.name: Heat exchangers
topics.description: Heat exchangers transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures without direct mixing. Common types include shell-and-tube, plate, compact (finned), and regenerative configurations. Analysis methods include LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) and effectiveness-NTU (Number of Transfer Units) approaches. Flow arrangements (parallel, counterflow, crossflow) affect perf

topics.name: Heat transfer
topics.description: Heat transfer is the fundamental study of thermal energy transport driven by temperature differences, encompassing three modes: conduction (molecular diffusion), convection (bulk fluid motion), and radiation (electromagnetic waves). The field develops governing equations, analytical and numerical solution methods, and practical correlations for predicting heat transfer rates in engineering systems

topics.name: Modes of heat transfer
topics.description: The three fundamental modes of heat transfer are conduction (heat diffusion through matter via molecular interactions), convection (heat transport by bulk fluid motion), and radiation (electromagnetic energy emission from surfaces). Conduction follows Fourier's law (q = -k∇T), convection follows Newton's law of cooling (q = hA(T_s - T_∞)), and radiation follows Stefan-Boltzmann law (q = εσAT⁴). Re

topics.name: Numerical methods in conduction
topics.description: Numerical methods solve complex conduction problems that lack analytical solutions due to irregular geometries, temperature-dependent properties, or complex boundary conditions. Finite difference methods discretize the heat equation into algebraic equations on a grid or mesh. Explicit methods (forward-time) are simple but stability-limited, while implicit methods (backward-time, Crank-Nicolson) ar

topics.name: One-dimensional heat conduction
topics.description: One-dimensional heat conduction describes thermal energy transport through materials where temperature varies in only one spatial direction and other directions are negligible. Governed by Fourier's law (q = -kA dT/dx), it applies to plane walls, cylindrical shells, and spherical shells under appropriate conditions. Analysis includes determining temperature distributions, heat transfer rates, and

topics.name: Overview of convection heat transfer
topics.description: Convection heat transfer combines conduction within a fluid with bulk fluid motion (advection), transporting thermal energy from surfaces to fluids or vice versa. Described by Newton's law of cooling (q = hA(T_s - T_∞)), the convection coefficient h depends on fluid properties, flow velocity, geometry, and flow regime (laminar or turbulent). Convection types include forced (externally driven flow)

topics.name: Overview of radiation heat transfer
topics.description: Radiation heat transfer involves electromagnetic wave emission from surfaces due to thermal motion of charged particles, enabling energy transport without a medium. All surfaces above absolute zero emit radiation following the Stefan-Boltzmann law (E = σT⁴). Radiation spans wavelengths from ultraviolet through visible to infrared. Key concepts include blackbody radiation (perfect emitter/absorber)

topics.name: Radiation
topics.description: Radiation heat transfer involves electromagnetic wave emission, absorption, and transmission, enabling energy transport across vacuum or transparent media. All matter above absolute zero radiates according to temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann law: E = εσT⁴) with emission spectrum described by Planck's law for blackbodies. Real surfaces are characterized by emissivity (emission), absorptivity (absorpti

topics.name: Steady-state
topics.description: Steady-state heat transfer occurs when temperatures at all locations remain constant with time, though spatial temperature variations exist. Heat flow rates are constant, and energy storage terms in governing equations vanish. This simplifies analysis significantly - ordinary differential equations replace partial differential equations. Examples include heat conduction through building walls, ste

topics.name: Temperature and measurement devices
topics.description: Temperature quantifies thermal energy level and determines heat flow direction. Measurement devices include thermocouples (thermoelectric voltage from junction of dissimilar metals), resistance temperature detectors (RTDs: resistance varies with temperature), thermistors (semiconductor resistance devices), infrared sensors (measure thermal radiation), and liquid-in-glass thermometers. Each has spe

topics.name: Temperature measurement
topics.description: Temperature measurement techniques quantify thermal state using various physical principles. Thermocouples generate thermoelectric voltage at junctions of dissimilar metals. RTDs (Resistance Temperature Detectors) exploit temperature-dependent electrical resistance of metals like platinum. Thermistors use semiconductor resistance changes. Infrared thermometers measure thermal radiation from surfac

topics.name: Transient one-dimensional heat conduction
topics.description: Transient (unsteady) conduction occurs when temperatures change with time, described by the heat diffusion equation ∂T/∂t = α∇²T, where α is thermal diffusivity. Solutions depend on initial conditions, boundary conditions, and geometry. The lumped capacitance method applies when internal resistance is negligible (Bi

topics.name: View factors
topics.description: View factors (configuration factors or shape factors) quantify the geometric relationship between surfaces exchanging radiation, representing the fraction of radiation leaving surface i that directly strikes surface j. Denoted F_ij, they depend only on geometry and orientation, not surface properties. Key relations include reciprocity (A_i F_ij = A_j F_ji), summation (ΣF_ij = 1 for surface i), and

topics.name: Wavelength-dependent surface properties
topics.description: Real surfaces exhibit wavelength-selective radiation properties where emissivity, absorptivity, and reflectivity vary with wavelength. Spectral emissivity ελ describes emission at specific wavelengths, while total emissivity averages over the entire spectrum weighted by blackbody distribution. This wavelength dependence enables selective surfaces for solar collectors (high absorptivity in solar sp

ME 360.name: Signal Processing
ME 360.description: of transducers and actuators. Signal conditioning, grounding, and shielding. Analog and digital signal processing and feedback control methods with emphasis on frequency domain techniques. Frequency response of continuous and discrete systems.
objectives: Understand the operating principle and the application context of basic transducers.
Understand the operating principle and the application context of basic actuators.
Be able to understand, specify, and use data collection systems.
Be able to analyze frequency domain properties of the collected data and relate it to physical time domain phenomena in the system.
Be able to understand, specify, and use industrial controllers.

topics.name: Analog filters
topics.description: Continuous-time circuits selectively attenuating or passing signal frequency components using resistors, capacitors, inductors, and operational amplifiers. Filter types include lowpass (passing low frequencies, blocking high), highpass (opposite), bandpass (passing range around center frequency), and bandstop (rejecting specific band). Design specifications include cutoff frequency, passband rippl

topics.name: Anti-aliasing filters
topics.description: Analog lowpass filters placed before analog-to-digital converters to remove signal frequency components above the Nyquist frequency, preventing aliasing distortion in sampled data. Filter requirements include cutoff frequency slightly below Nyquist limit, sharp transition band to maximize usable signal bandwidth while ensuring adequate attenuation in stopband, and minimal phase distortion for pres

topics.name: Continuous time systems
topics.description: Systems processing signals that exist at all points in time, characterized by differential equations relating input and output signals, with properties including linearity (superposition), time-invariance (consistent behavior over time), causality (output depends only on present and past inputs), and stability (bounded outputs for bounded inputs). Analysis involves impulse response characterizing

topics.name: Discrete fourier transform, windowing
topics.description: Practical computation of frequency spectra for finite-length discrete signals through the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), efficiently calculated using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithms. DFT produces N frequency samples for N time samples, assuming periodic extension of finite-length data. Windowing applies tapered weighting functions (Hamming, Hann, Blackman) to finite-length signals reduc

topics.name: Discrete time fourier transform
topics.description: The frequency domain representation of discrete-time signals x[n] as continuous periodic functions X(e^jω) of normalized frequency, relating discrete samples to continuous frequency spectrum with periodicity of 2π. DTFT enables analysis of digital signal frequency content, filter design in frequency domain, and understanding effects of sampling on signal spectra. The transform is periodic because

topics.name: Discrete time systems and z-transforms
topics.description: Mathematical framework for analyzing digital systems processing discrete-time signals through difference equations, with z-transform providing frequency-domain representation analogous to Laplace transform for continuous systems. Z-transform converts difference equations to algebraic equations in z-domain, where z=e^(jωT) relates to continuous frequency. Transfer functions H(z) characterize system

topics.name: Electric motors (dc, ac, stepper)
topics.description: Actuators converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy through electromagnetic interactions between stator fields and rotor conductors. DC motors use commutators for continuous rotation with speed proportional to voltage and torque proportional to current, offering simple control. AC induction motors use rotating stator fields inducing rotor currents, robust but requiring variable

topics.name: Fourier series
topics.description: Mathematical representation of periodic signals as infinite sums of harmonic sinusoids with frequencies at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency, enabling decomposition of complex periodic waveforms into constituent frequency components. Each harmonic has an amplitude and phase determined by Fourier coefficients calculated through integration over one period. Fourier series reveals that a

topics.name: Fourier transform
topics.description: Extension of Fourier series to non-periodic signals, representing arbitrary time-domain signals as continuous superpositions of sinusoids with infinitesimal frequency spacing. The Fourier transform X(jω) decomposes signal x(t) into frequency spectrum showing amplitude and phase versus frequency, while inverse transform reconstructs time signal from frequency components. Properties include linearit

topics.name: Grounding and shielding techniques
topics.description: Methods for minimizing electromagnetic interference, ground loops, and noise coupling in measurement systems through proper ground connection topology, cable shielding, and signal routing. Single-point grounding prevents ground loops (current flow through ground creating voltage differences), while multipoint grounding reduces high-frequency impedance. Shielding uses conductive enclosures grounded

topics.name: Hydraulic actuators
topics.description: High-force actuators using incompressible fluid (oil) pressure to drive pistons in cylinders or rotate hydraulic motors, providing excellent power-to-weight ratios and precise force control. Hydraulic systems generate high forces (hundreds of kilonewtons) through Pascal's principle where pressure acts equally throughout confined fluid. Components include pumps generating pressure, directional cont

topics.name: Impulse response and convolution for continuous time systems
topics.description: The impulse response h(t) characterizes a linear time-invariant system's output to an ideal impulse (infinitely narrow, unit area), completely determining system behavior for any input through convolution. Convolution integral y(t) = ∫x(τ)h(t-τ)dτ computes output as weighted superposition of time-shifted impulse responses, with weights given by input signal values. Graphical interpretation involve

topics.name: Instrumentation amplifiers
topics.description: Precision differential amplifiers specifically designed for sensor signal conditioning with very high input impedance (preventing sensor loading), extremely high common-mode rejection ratio (rejecting interference), low input offset voltage and drift, and precise gain control. Three-op-amp architecture provides high impedance buffering on both inputs with gain set by single external resistor while

topics.name: Instrumentation amplifiers and transducer signal conditioning
topics.description: Specialized amplifier circuits with high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), low noise, and precise gain for amplifying small differential signals from sensors in presence of large common-mode voltages and noise. Instrumentation amplifiers typically use three-op-amp architecture enabling gain setting with single resistor while maintaining high input impedance on both inputs.

topics.name: Introduction to analog filter design
topics.description: Systematic methods for designing continuous-time filters meeting specifications including passband/stopband frequencies, maximum passband ripple, minimum stopband attenuation, and phase linearity. Design process involves specifying requirements, selecting approximation function (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel, Elliptic), determining filter order meeting specifications, calculating transfer functio

topics.name: Introduction to control system implementation
topics.description: Practical aspects of realizing control algorithms in hardware including discrete-time implementation through difference equations on microcontrollers or DSPs, sampling rate selection balancing performance and computational load, anti-aliasing and reconstruction filtering, actuator saturation and rate limiting, integral windup prevention, and real-time computational constraints. Implementation cons

topics.name: Introduction to digital filter design
topics.description: Methods for creating discrete-time filters processing sampled signals through difference equations or convolution with finite impulse response. FIR filters have finite-length impulse responses, guaranteed stability, and linear phase, designed using windowing or optimization methods. IIR filters have infinite impulse responses, higher efficiency requiring fewer coefficients but potential instabilit

topics.name: Introduction to feedback control system, loop stability and pid control
topics.description: Control system architectures using sensor measurements to adjust actuator inputs maintaining desired system outputs despite disturbances. Feedback compares measured output to reference setpoint, generating error signal driving controller computing actuator command. Stability requires careful loop gain design preventing oscillations, analyzed using Nyquist criterion, Bode plots, or root locus metho

topics.name: Laboratory instruments
topics.description: Electronic test equipment for signal generation, measurement, and analysis including oscilloscopes (displaying voltage waveforms versus time), function generators (creating test signals), multimeters (measuring voltage, current, resistance), spectrum analyzers (frequency domain display), and data acquisition systems (computerized multi-channel recording). Oscilloscopes reveal signal shape, timing,

topics.name: Laplace transforms
topics.description: Mathematical transformation converting time-domain differential equations to algebraic equations in complex frequency domain (s-domain), enabling simplified analysis of linear time-invariant systems. Laplace transform converts differentiation to multiplication by s and integration to division by s, transforming convolution to multiplication. Transfer functions H(s) = Y(s)/X(s) characterize system

topics.name: Load cells
topics.description: Force and weight measurement transducers using strain gages bonded to elastic elements (beams, columns, rings) that deform predictably under load. Common configurations include bending beam (shear/bending sensitive), column (compression), S-beam (tension and compression), and pancake (low profile). Strain gages in full Wheatbridge configuration provide temperature compensation and maximize signal

topics.name: Modeling and identification of an electric motor using step response methods
topics.description: Experimental and analytical techniques determining motor parameters (resistance, inductance, inertia, friction, torque constant) from measured responses to step voltage inputs. Step response reveals first-order (armature electrical) and second-order (mechanical) dynamics through time constant extraction and curve fitting. Identification process includes applying step input, measuring voltage and c

topics.name: Noise reduction techniques
topics.description: Methods for minimizing unwanted signal corruption from various noise sources including electromagnetic interference, ground loops, thermal noise, shot noise, and quantization noise. Techniques include shielding and grounding to prevent pickup, twisted pair wiring for balanced transmission rejecting common-mode interference, filtering to remove out-of-band noise, averaging multiple measurements to

topics.name: Pid control of a hydraulic cylinder
