topics.description: Feedback control system regulating hydraulic actuator position, velocity, or force by measuring cylinder displacement with LVDT or potentiometer, computing PID control law based on error signal, and commanding servo valve opening proportional to controller output. Hydraulic system dynamics include valve flow characteristics, cylinder flow continuity, fluid compressibility effects, and load dynamic

topics.name: Piezoelectric and piezoresistive accelerometers
topics.description: Accelerometers measuring vibration and shock using piezoelectric crystals generating charge proportional to applied stress from seismic mass acceleration, or piezoresistive elements changing resistance under stress. Piezoelectric accelerometers offer high sensitivity, wide frequency range (DC-limited), and rugged construction for general vibration measurement. Piezoresistive accelerometers measure

topics.name: Pneumatic actuators
topics.description: Devices generating mechanical motion and force using compressed air expanding in cylinders driving pistons, rotating vanes, or inflating bladders. Pneumatic cylinders convert air pressure to linear force following F=P×A, with double-acting cylinders powered in both directions and single-acting using springs for return. Advantages include high power-to-weight ratio, inherent compliance, spark-free

topics.name: Pressure sensors
topics.description: Transducers measuring fluid pressure through diaphragm deflection detected using piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, or optical sensing mechanisms. Piezoresistive sensors use diffused or bonded strain gages on diaphragms, capacitive sensors measure gap changes, piezoelectric sensors detect dynamic pressure through crystal charge generation. Sensor types include absolute (referenced to vacuu

topics.name: Response to periodic signals
topics.description: Analysis of system steady-state output when driven by periodic inputs, including sinusoidal responses characterized by frequency response function and multi-harmonic periodic signals analyzed using Fourier series. For linear time-invariant systems, each harmonic component is scaled and phase-shifted independently according to system frequency response at that frequency. Total response is superposi

topics.name: Review of harmonic signals
topics.description: Foundational mathematical treatment of sinusoidal waveforms characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase, forming the building blocks of signal processing through Fourier decomposition. Harmonic signals include sine and cosine functions expressible as exponential functions through Euler's formula, exhibiting periodic behavior with fundamental frequency and integer harmonics (overtones). Unders

topics.name: Rtd's
topics.description: Resistance Temperature Detectors measuring temperature through predictable resistance increase of pure metals (typically platinum, nickel, copper) with temperature. Platinum RTDs (Pt100, Pt1000) offer excellent accuracy, stability, and linearity following Callendar-Van Dusen equation, with positive temperature coefficient around 0.385%/°C. Four-wire measurement technique eliminates lead wire resis

topics.name: Sampling theory and introduction to aliasing
topics.description: The process of converting continuous-time signals to discrete-time sequences by measuring values at regular intervals, governed by Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem requiring sampling rate exceeding twice the highest signal frequency to enable perfect reconstruction. Aliasing occurs when sampling rate is insufficient, causing high-frequency components to masquerade as lower frequencies in sampled d

topics.name: Signal conditioning and a/d conversion
topics.description: Comprehensive process preparing analog sensor outputs for digitization including amplification to match ADC input range, filtering to remove noise and prevent aliasing, offset adjustment for proper ranging, isolation for safety and ground loop prevention, and linearization for nonlinear sensors. Conditioning circuits interface low-level sensor signals (millivolts from thermocouples, microvolt chan

topics.name: Signal conversion techniques (a/d, d/a)
topics.description: Methods for converting between analog continuous-amplitude signals and digital discrete-value representations, including analog-to-digital conversion (ADC) through sampling, quantization to finite precision levels, and encoding to binary values, and digital-to-analog conversion (DAC) reconstructing continuous signals from discrete values through zero-order hold or interpolation. ADC specifications

topics.name: Signal reconstruction
topics.description: The process of recovering continuous-time signals from discrete sample sequences through interpolation, ideally using sinc function interpolation (Shannon-Whittaker) for bandlimited signals sampled above Nyquist rate, practically using zero-order hold (staircase), first-order hold (linear interpolation), or higher-order polynomial interpolation. Reconstruction filters smooth the staircase output f

topics.name: Speed control of a dc electric motor
topics.description: Closed-loop feedback control system regulating motor rotational velocity by measuring speed with encoder or tachometer, comparing to reference setpoint, and adjusting armature voltage through PWM amplifier based on controller output. Control strategies include PID regulation balancing tracking performance, disturbance rejection, and stability. System dynamics include electrical time constant (L/R)

topics.name: Strain gage measurements
topics.description: Complete measurement system capturing strain-induced resistance changes from gages bonded to structures, including Wheatstone bridge excitation providing sensitivity and temperature compensation, bridge balancing for nulling offsets, amplification of millivolt-level bridge outputs, completion resistors for quarter- and half-bridge configurations, and shunt calibration verifying system gain. Bridge

topics.name: Strain gages
topics.description: Resistive transducers bonded to structural surfaces measuring strain through resistance change proportional to deformation, based on piezoresistive effect where stretching increases resistance and compression decreases it. Typical metallic foil gages have gage factors around 2, meaning 0.2% resistance change per 1000 microstrain. Gages configured in Wheatbridge circuits enable temperature compensa

topics.name: System identification with frequency response techniques using dynamic signal analyzer
topics.description: Experimental method for determining system transfer functions by applying swept-sine or random excitation signals while measuring input and output with dynamic signal analyzers computing frequency response functions (FRFs) through FFT-based cross-spectral analysis. The technique measures magnitude and phase versus frequency, revealing resonances, damping characteristics, and system order. Coherenc

topics.name: Systems frequency response
topics.description: The steady-state output of a system to sinusoidal inputs across a range of frequencies, characterized by magnitude (gain) and phase shift as functions of frequency. Frequency response H(jω) is obtained by evaluating transfer function H(s) at s=jω, revealing resonant peaks, filtering characteristics, and bandwidth limitations. Bode plots display magnitude in decibels and phase in degrees versus log

topics.name: Thermocouples
topics.description: Temperature sensors based on Seebeck effect generating voltage at junction of dissimilar metals proportional to temperature difference between measurement junction and reference junction. Common types (J, K, T, E, R, S, B) use standardized metal combinations spanning -200°C to +1800°C with different sensitivities (Seebeck coefficients around 40 μV/°C for type K). Cold junction compensation account

topics.name: Transducers
topics.description: Devices converting physical quantities (force, pressure, temperature, displacement, acceleration, flow) into electrical signals (voltage, current, resistance, capacitance) or vice versa for measurement and actuation. Sensor transducers exploit physical principles including piezoresistivity (resistance change with strain), piezoelectricity (charge generation from stress), thermoelectricity (voltage

ME 371.name: Mechanical Design II
ME 371.description: material failure modes, including yielding, fracture, and fatigue. Design and selection of machine elements: threaded fasteners, springs, rolling-element bearings, fluid film lubrication, gears and friction drives.
objectives: Ability to utilize computer-aided design and engineering tools in conducting linear stress analysis.
Analyze structures for various failure modes (yielding, fracture, fatigue, and creep) to predict failure loads and service lifetimes.
Design structures that can withstand service loads and have adequate lifetimes.
Be familiar with common structural materials and their properties.
Design and/or select springs for desired stiffness, energy absorption, and lifetime.
Analyze bolted and welded joints for load-carrying ability and service lifetime.
Design joints that can withstand service loads and have adequate lifetimes.
Analyze power transmission components (gears, clutches, brakes, bearings) and systems for kinematics, service lifetimes, and supporting loads.
Select and size power transmission components to achieve suitable kinematics, service lifetime, and transmission of power.

topics.name: Behavior of composite materials
topics.description: Composite material behavior differs from isotropic metals due to anisotropy (direction-dependent properties), heterogeneity (fiber-matrix structure), and unique failure modes (fiber breakage, matrix cracking, delamination, fiber-matrix debonding). Analysis requires considering fiber orientation, laminate stacking sequence, and hygrothermal effects (moisture, temperature). Composite advantages incl

topics.name: Bolts and bolted joints
topics.description: Bolts create demountable joints through threaded fasteners loaded in tension or shear. Design considerations include bolt strength (grades SAE or ISO), thread engagement, preload (controlled via torque or turn-of-nut), joint stiffness ratio, and factors of safety against yielding or fatigue. Joint analysis considers bolt tension, clamping force maintaining joint integrity, external loads (tension,

topics.name: Cae evaluate the strength of unknown components
topics.description: Computer-Aided Engineering (CAE) evaluates component strength when geometry is known but materials, loads, or design details are uncertain. Techniques include reverse engineering (measuring geometry, identifying materials through testing), load estimation (usage scenarios, similar components, customer requirements), parametric studies (varying uncertain parameters), and validation through testing

topics.name: Demonstrate the strengths and weaknesses of elastic fea
topics.description: Elastic Finite Element Analysis (FEA) predicts stress and deformation assuming linear elastic material behavior, valid for stresses below yield. Strengths include accuracy for most structural problems, computational efficiency, well-established methods, and extensive commercial software. Weaknesses include inability to capture plasticity, nonlinear geometry (large deformations), contact nonlineari

topics.name: Design against fatigue
topics.description: Design against fatigue prevents progressive damage from cyclic loading causing crack nucleation, growth, and eventual fracture at stresses below yield strength. Analysis uses S-N curves (stress versus cycles to failure), modified Goodman or Gerber diagrams (combining mean and alternating stress), and Miner's rule (cumulative damage). Design factors affecting fatigue include surface finish, size, s

topics.name: Design against fracture
topics.description: Design against fracture prevents catastrophic separation by ensuring components can tolerate crack-like flaws without sudden propagation. Fracture mechanics uses stress intensity factor (K) comparing applied stress and crack size to material toughness (KIc). Linear elastic fracture mechanics (LEFM) assumes brittle behavior; elastic-plastic fracture mechanics handles ductile tearing. Design approac

topics.name: Design against yielding
topics.description: Design against yielding prevents permanent deformation by ensuring maximum stress remains below yield strength with appropriate safety factor. Analysis uses yield criteria for uniaxial loading (σ

topics.name: Design and construct a compact mechanical power transmission system
topics.description: Designing compact power transmission systems requires integrating multiple components (motor, gears, shafts, bearings, couplings) within tight space constraints while meeting power, speed, efficiency, and reliability requirements. Design challenges include heat dissipation in confined spaces, maintaining alignment with close tolerances, selecting compact components (planetary gears, high-pole moto

topics.name: Fluid film lubrication
topics.description: Fluid film lubrication separates surfaces with pressurized fluid film preventing direct contact, providing extremely low friction and wear. Hydrodynamic lubrication generates pressure through converging wedge geometry in relative motion (journal bearings, thrust bearings). Hydrostatic lubrication supplies pressurized fluid externally, supporting loads even at zero speed. Analysis uses Reynolds equ

topics.name: Friction drives (clutches, brakes, traction drives, etc.)
topics.description: Friction drives transmit torque or control motion through friction between contacting surfaces, including clutches (engaging/disengaging power transmission), brakes (dissipating kinetic energy), and traction drives (continuously variable transmission). Design considerations include required torque capacity (friction coefficient × normal force × radius), heat dissipation from sliding, wear life, ac

topics.name: Gear forces
topics.description: Gear forces arise from power transmission through meshing teeth, including tangential force (transmitting torque), radial force (separating gears), and for helical/bevel gears, axial thrust forces. Forces depend on transmitted torque, pitch radius, pressure angle, and helix angle. Force analysis determines bearing reactions, shaft bending moments, and required mounting rigidity. High forces can ca

topics.name: Gears and gear trains
topics.description: Gears transmit power and motion between shafts through meshing teeth, providing precise speed ratios, high efficiency, and compact packaging. Gear trains are systems of multiple gears achieving desired speed ratios or multiple outputs. Common configurations include simple (one stage), compound (multiple stages on common shafts), planetary (central, ring, and planet gears), and differential (two in

topics.name: Gear selection
topics.description: Gear selection matches gear types, materials, sizes, and quality grades to application requirements considering power, speed, ratio, duty cycle, accuracy, noise, cost, and space. Selection process includes determining torque and speed, selecting type (spur, helical, bevel, worm) based on shaft arrangement and performance needs, sizing for bending and contact stress using AGMA standards, choosing m

topics.name: Kinematics of gear trains
topics.description: Kinematics of gear trains analyzes motion relationships in gear systems, determining output speeds from input speeds and gear ratios. For simple trains, velocity ratio equals product of driving gear teeth divided by driven gear teeth. Compound trains have multiple stages, with overall ratio being the product of individual stage ratios. Planetary (epicyclic) gear trains are more complex, requiring

topics.name: Machine components
topics.description: Machine components are standardized mechanical elements performing specific functions: power transmission (gears, belts, chains, shafts), support and guidance (bearings, slides), fastening (bolts, pins, welds), motion control (springs, clutches, brakes), and sealing (gaskets, O-rings). Understanding component selection involves matching characteristics to application requirements, considering stan

topics.name: Material failure modes
topics.description: Material failure modes describe how components fail under service conditions, including yielding (permanent deformation), fracture (separation), fatigue (cyclic loading damage), creep (time-dependent deformation at temperature), wear (surface material loss), corrosion (chemical/electrochemical degradation), and buckling (instability). Each mode has distinct mechanisms, predictive theories, and pre

topics.name: Multiaxial stress
topics.description: Multiaxial stress occurs when components experience simultaneous stresses in multiple directions (normal and shear), requiring tensor representation and appropriate failure criteria. Real components typically experience complex 3D stress states from combined loading (tension, torsion, bending, pressure). Principal stresses are the extreme normal stresses on planes with zero shear stress. Failure p

topics.name: Rolling element bearings
topics.description: Rolling element bearings support rotating shafts using balls or rollers between races, providing low friction, high load capacity, and long life. Types include deep groove ball (radial loads, moderate thrust), angular contact ball (combined loads), cylindrical roller (high radial loads), tapered roller (high radial and thrust), and needle (compact, high radial). Selection considers load magnitude/

topics.name: Springs
topics.description: Springs store and release elastic energy, providing controlled forces, absorbing shocks, maintaining contact, and isolating vibrations. Common types include helical compression/extension springs, torsion springs, Belleville washers, and leaf springs. Design parameters include wire diameter, coil diameter, number of coils, and material, determining spring rate (force per deflection), maximum stress

topics.name: Stress concentrations and notch sensitivity
topics.description: Stress concentrations are localized stress elevations at geometric discontinuities (holes, fillets, notches, threads), where peak stress exceeds nominal stress by stress concentration factor (Kt). Notch sensitivity (q) indicates material's susceptibility to stress concentrations, varying from 0 (insensitive, like cast iron) to 1 (fully sensitive, like high-strength steel). Fatigue strength reducti

topics.name: Use beams, solid and shell elements where appropriate to estimate component strength
topics.description: Different finite element types suit different geometries: beam elements for slender structures (shafts, frames), solid elements for 3D bulk components, and shell elements for thin-walled structures (pressure vessels, sheet metal). Element selection balances accuracy with computational cost. Beams are most efficient but require simplified geometry; solids handle complex geometry but are computation

TAM 445.name: Continuum Mechanics
TAM 445.description: Tensor algebra and analysis; kinematics of continua; mass, force, stress, and the general balance laws of continuum mechanics; introduction to constitutive equations.
objectives: Be able to calculate with tensors from both the component and direct points of view.
Be able to calculate velocity and acceleration fields of deforming continua from both the material/referential and spatial points of view.
Be able to state and apply the basic balance laws of conservation of mass, balance of momentum, and balance of moment of momentum in both local and global forms.
Be able to state constitutive equations from some of the simpler theories, such as Newtonian fluids, and apply them in the formulation of boundary/initial-value problems.

topics.name: Balance laws
topics.description: Balance laws express conservation or balance of physical quantities. General form: rate of change = production + flux. Mass conservation: Dρ/Dt + ρ∇·v = 0 (continuity equation). Linear momentum: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb (Cauchy equation), where σ is stress, b is body force. Angular momentum: σ = σᵀ (stress symmetry). Energy: ρDe/Dt = σ:D + ∇·q + ρr, where e is internal energy, q is heat flux, r is heat s

topics.name: Bodies, deformations, displacements
topics.description: Body B is collection of material points X occupying reference region. Deformation χ maps B to current configuration: x = χ(X,t). Bijective mapping ensures no material interpenetration or voids. Displacement field u(X,t) = χ(X,t) - X measures motion from reference. Deformation gradient F = I + ∇u (using ∇ = ∂/∂X). Smooth deformations require F continuous and det(F) > 0 (no volume collapse). Large d

topics.name: Cauchy's stress equations of motion
topics.description: Cauchy's equations of motion are differential equations governing continuum motion: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb, or component form: ρDvᵢ/Dt = ∂σᵢⱼ/∂xⱼ + ρbᵢ. Derived from linear momentum balance using stress principle and localization. Relate stress gradients and body forces to acceleration. Three scalar equations (i=1,2,3) for three velocity components. Supplemented by mass conservation and constitutive eq

topics.name: Cauchy's stress principle
topics.description: Cauchy's stress principle states that internal forces in continuum are transmitted through contact across surfaces via stress vector t(n) depending on position x, time t, and surface normal n. Stress vector: t(n) = σ·n, where σ is Cauchy stress tensor (second-order). On opposite face: t(-n) = -t(n) (action-reaction). Stress tensor is independent of n, fully characterizing internal force state. Com

topics.name: Change of coordinate frame
topics.description: Change of coordinate frame (basis transformation) relates tensor components in different coordinate systems. Rotation matrix Q transforms components: v'ᵢ = Qᵢⱼvⱼ for vectors, T'ᵢⱼ = QᵢₖQⱼₗTₖₗ for second-order tensors. Orthogonal transformations (rotations) preserve lengths and angles: QᵀQ = I. Tensors themselves are independent of coordinates; only components change. Active interpretation: rotatin

topics.name: Conservation of mass
topics.description: Mass conservation states material mass remains constant: dm = 0, or equivalently, mass density ρ evolves as Dρ/Dt + ρ∇·v = 0 (continuity equation). Integral form: d/dt ∫v(t) ρ dv = 0 for material volume. For incompressible materials (ρ = constant), reduces to ∇·v = 0 (divergence-free velocity). Alternative forms: ∂ρ/∂t + ∇·(ρv) = 0 (conservation form). Material derivative of J: DJ/Dt = J∇·v. Mass

topics.name: Determinants
topics.description: Determinants are scalar values associated with square matrices encoding volume scaling properties. For 2×2: det(A) = a₁₁a₂₂ - a₁₂a₂₁; for 3×3: expansion by minors or rule of Sarrus. Properties: det(AB) = det(A)det(B), det(Aᵀ) = det(A), det(A⁻¹) = 1/det(A). Geometrically, |det(A)| gives volume scale factor of linear transformation A. Zero determinant means singular matrix (non-invertible, volume co

topics.name: Elastic solids
topics.description: Elastic solids store deformation energy and return to original configuration when loads removed. Constitutive relation: stress depends on strain (not strain rate). Linear elasticity: σ = C:ε (Hooke's law), where C is fourth-order elasticity tensor. Isotropic linear elasticity: σᵢⱼ = λδᵢⱼεₖₖ + 2μεᵢⱼ using Lamé parameters λ, μ. Equivalently, use E (Young's modulus), ν (Poisson's ratio), G (shear mod

topics.name: Euler's laws
topics.description: Euler's laws extend Newton's laws to continua. First law (linear momentum balance): d/dt ∫v ρv dv = ∫s t dS + ∫v ρb dv, where t is surface traction, b is body force. Localized using transport and divergence theorems: ρDv/Dt = ∇·σ + ρb (Cauchy's equation of motion). Second law (angular momentum balance): d/dt ∫v x×ρv dv = ∫s x×t dS + ∫v x×ρb dv. Localizes to stress symmetry: σ = σᵀ (in absence of c

topics.name: Higher-order tensors
topics.description: Higher-order tensors extend second-order concept to more indices. Third-order tensor Tᵢⱼₖ maps two vectors to scalar or vector to second-order tensor. Fourth-order tensor Cᵢⱼₖₗ maps second-order tensor to second-order tensor. Elasticity tensor C relating stress to strain is fourth-order: σᵢⱼ = Cᵢⱼₖₗεₖₗ. Transformation rules extend naturally: T'ᵢⱼₖ = QᵢₘQⱼₙQₖₚTₘₙₚ. Symmetries reduce independent com

topics.name: Indicial notation
topics.description: Indicial notation (index notation) represents tensor components using subscripts and Einstein summation convention. Repeated index implies summation: aᵢbᵢ = Σᵢaᵢbᵢ. Free indices appear once and represent components; dummy indices appear twice and are summed. Kronecker delta δᵢⱼ (=1 if i=j, =0 otherwise) and permutation symbol εᵢⱼₖ are key tensors. Vector dot product: a·b = aᵢbᵢ; cross product: (a×

topics.name: Introduction to constitutive equations
topics.description: Constitutive equations relate kinematic quantities (strain, rate of deformation) to kinetic quantities (stress), characterizing specific material behavior. Balance laws alone are insufficient - need material-specific relations. Requirements: (1) Material objectivity (frame-invariance), (2) material symmetry (isotropy, anisotropy), (3) thermodynamic admissibility. Examples: Hooke's law for elastic

topics.name: Inviscid fluids
topics.description: Inviscid fluids have no viscosity (internal friction) - constitutive equation: σ = -pI, where p is thermodynamic pressure (function of density and temperature). Tangential stresses (shear) absent, only normal stress (pressure). Euler equations of fluid mechanics: ρDv/Dt = -∇p + ρb. Incompressible inviscid: ∇·v = 0 and ρ = constant. Bernoulli's equation for steady flow: v²/2 + p/ρ + gz = constant.

topics.name: Kinematics
topics.description: Kinematics describes motion and deformation of continua without considering forces. Maps material points X (reference configuration) to current positions x = χ(X,t) (current configuration). Displacement u = x - X. Deformation gradient F = ∂x/∂X = ∂χ/∂X relates material to spatial line elements: dx = F·dX. Jacobian J = det(F) gives volume ratio dv/dV. Velocity v = ∂χ/∂t (material derivative), accel

topics.name: Matrices
topics.description: Matrices are rectangular arrays of numbers representing linear transformations and second-order tensors. Operations include addition, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication (not commutative), transpose, and inverse. Matrix multiplication represents composition of transformations. Identity matrix I is multiplicative identity. Orthogonal matrices Q satisfy QᵀQ = I, representing rotations. Symm

topics.name: Motions
topics.description: Motion describes time-dependent deformation of continuum: x = χ(X,t) maps reference configuration to current at each time t. Material description follows particles: f(X,t). Spatial description observes locations: f(x,t). Velocity: material v(X,t) = ∂χ/∂t, spatial v(x,t). Acceleration a = ∂v/∂t + (v·∇)v using material derivative (convective acceleration included). Pathlines follow particles; stream

topics.name: Newtonian fluids
topics.description: Newtonian fluids have stress linearly proportional to rate of deformation: σ = -pI + λ(tr D)I + 2μD, where λ and μ are viscosity coefficients (Lamé parameters for fluids). Incompressible Newtonian: σ = -pI + 2μD with ∇·v = 0. Navier-Stokes equations: ρDv/Dt = -∇p + μ∇²v + ρb (incompressible). Dynamic viscosity μ resists shearing; bulk viscosity relates to compression. Most common fluids (water, ai

topics.name: Polar decompositions
topics.description: Polar decomposition uniquely factors deformation gradient F into rotation R and stretch U or V: F = RU (right stretch) or F = VR (left stretch), where R is orthogonal (RᵀR = I), U and V are symmetric positive-definite. U = √(FᵀF) is right stretch tensor, V = √(FFᵀ) is left stretch tensor, related by V = RURᵀ. Decomposition separates rigid rotation from pure deformation. Principal stretches λᵢ (eig

topics.name: Positive-definite tensors
topics.description: Positive-definite tensor A satisfies v·A·v > 0 for all non-zero vectors v, or equivalently, all eigenvalues positive: λᵢ > 0. Positive-semidefinite allows equality: v·A·v ≥ 0, λᵢ ≥ 0. Examples: metric tensor, right and left stretch tensors (U, V in polar decomposition). Positive-definiteness ensures physical realizability (e.g., positive strain energy). For symmetric positive-definite A, unique po

topics.name: Principal directions of symmetric second-order tensors
topics.description: Principal directions are eigenvectors of symmetric tensor: A·n = λn, where n is principal direction (eigenvector), λ is principal value (eigenvalue). For symmetric tensor, eigenvalues are real and eigenvectors mutually orthogonal. Principal axes provide coordinate system where tensor is diagonal - off-diagonal components zero. In principal coordinates: Aᵢⱼ = λ₁n₁ᵢn₁ⱼ + λ₂n₂ᵢn₂ⱼ + λ₃n₃ᵢn₃ⱼ (spectra

topics.name: Rate of deformation and vorticity
topics.description: Velocity gradient L = ∇v decomposes into symmetric rate of deformation D = sym(L) = (L+Lᵀ)/2 and antisymmetric vorticity (spin) W = skew(L) = (L-Lᵀ)/2. Rate of deformation D measures stretching and shearing rates. Vorticity W measures local rotation rate, related to angular velocity ω by W = -ω×. In component form: Dᵢⱼ = (∂vᵢ/∂xⱼ + ∂vⱼ/∂xᵢ)/2, Wᵢⱼ = (∂vᵢ/∂xⱼ - ∂vⱼ/∂xᵢ)/2. Vorticity vector: ωᵢ = εᵢ

topics.name: Rigid deformations
topics.description: Rigid deformation preserves distances between all material points - no stretching or distortion, only translation and rotation. Mathematically: x = c(t) + Q(t)·X, where c is translation, Q is proper orthogonal tensor (rotation) satisfying QᵀQ = I and det(Q) = 1. Deformation gradient F = Q (pure rotation). Material line elements: dx = Q·dX preserve length ‖dx‖ = ‖dX‖ and angles. Rigid motion repres

topics.name: Scalar products
topics.description: Scalar product (inner product, dot product) of vectors a and b yields scalar: a·b = aᵢbᵢ = ‖a‖‖b‖cosθ, where θ is angle between vectors. Properties: commutative (a·b = b·a), distributive, and bilinear. Orthogonality: a·b = 0 when perpendicular. For second-order tensors: A:B = AᵢⱼBᵢⱼ (double contraction). Scalar product measures projection, computes work (F·dr), defines norms, and tests orthogonali

topics.name: Second-order tensors
topics.description: Second-order tensors are linear transformations mapping vectors to vectors: y = A·x, with components Aᵢⱼ forming matrix. Transform under coordinate change: A'ᵢⱼ = QᵢₖQⱼₗAₖₗ. Symmetric tensors Aᵢⱼ = Aⱼᵢ have orthogonal eigenvectors; antisymmetric have Aᵢⱼ = -Aⱼᵢ. Decomposition: A = sym(A) + skew(A). Operations include addition, multiplication, transpose, inverse, trace, determinant. Physical exampl

topics.name: Some properties of the stress tensor
topics.description: Key stress tensor properties: (1) Symmetry: σᵢⱼ = σⱼᵢ from angular momentum balance. (2) Transformation law: σ' = QσQᵀ under rotation Q. (3) Principal stresses: eigenvalues σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ with orthogonal eigenvectors (principal directions). (4) Invariants: I₁ = tr(σ), I₂ = ½[(tr σ)² - tr(σ²)], I₃ = det(σ) are coordinate-independent. (5) Decomposition: σ = σmI + s into mean (hydrostatic) and deviatoric

topics.name: Tensor algebra and analysis
topics.description: Tensor algebra provides mathematical framework for continuum mechanics. Tensors are geometric objects independent of coordinate choice - scalars (rank 0), vectors (rank 1), and higher-order tensors (rank ≥2). Tensor operations include addition, multiplication, contraction, and transformation under coordinate changes. Tensor analysis extends to derivatives and integrals of tensor fields. Indicial n

topics.name: Tensor fields
topics.description: Tensor fields assign tensor value to each point in space: scalar field φ(x), vector field v(x), tensor field T(x). Spatial variation described by gradients, divergences, curls. Gradient ∇φ points toward maximum increase; divergence ∇·v measures outflow; curl ∇×v measures rotation. For tensor fields: gradient ∇T gives higher-order tensor, divergence ∇·T gives lower-order. Field equations (PDEs) gov

topics.name: Tensor square roots
topics.description: For positive-definite symmetric tensor A, unique positive-definite square root √A exists such that (√A)² = A. Computed using spectral decomposition: if A = Σλᵢnᵢ⊗nᵢ, then √A = Σ√λᵢnᵢ⊗nᵢ. In component form: √A operates by taking square root of eigenvalues while preserving eigenvectors. Applications: polar decomposition F = RU relates deformation gradient F to rotation R and right stretch U = √(FᵀF)

topics.name: The geometry of general deformations: strain
topics.description: Strain measures deformation intensity, quantifying stretching and angular distortion. Right Cauchy-Green tensor C = FᵀF and Green-Lagrange strain E = (C-I)/2 measure strain in material description. Left Cauchy-Green B = FFᵀ and Euler-Almansi strain e = (I-B⁻¹)/2 for spatial description. For small deformations, linearize: ε = (∇u + ∇uᵀ)/2 (infinitesimal strain). Principal strains are eigenvalues of

topics.name: Theorems from advanced calculus
topics.description: Key theorems relate volume and surface integrals, essential for continuum mechanics. Divergence theorem (Gauss): ∫v(∇·T)dV = ∫s T·n dS converts volume integral of divergence to surface integral. Stokes theorem: ∫s(∇×v)·n dS = ∫c v·dx relates surface integral of curl to line integral. Reynolds transport theorem: d/dt ∫v φ dV = ∫v (∂φ/∂t + ∇·(φv)) dV for moving volumes. These enable converting betwe

topics.name: The stress tensor
topics.description: Cauchy stress tensor σ is symmetric second-order tensor characterizing internal force state in continuum. Components σᵢⱼ represent force per area: j-direction indicates surface normal, i-direction indicates force component. Diagonal terms (σ₁₁, σ₂₂, σ₃₃) are normal stresses; off-diagonal are shear stresses. Stress vector on surface with normal n: t = σ·n. Principal stresses σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ (eigenvalues

topics.name: The transport and localization theorems
topics.description: Reynolds transport theorem relates time derivative of integral over moving volume to local rates: d/dt ∫v(t) φ dv = ∫v(t) (∂φ/∂t + φ ∇·v) dv, or d/dt ∫v(t) φ dv = ∫v(t) (Dφ/Dt + φ ∇·v) dv using material derivative. Localization theorem: if ∫v f dv = 0 for all volumes v, then f = 0 pointwise (assuming continuity). Transport theorem converts global conservation laws (integral form) to local field eq

topics.name: Vector products
topics.description: Vector product (cross product) of vectors a and b yields vector perpendicular to both: c = a×b with magnitude ‖c‖ = ‖a‖‖b‖sinθ and direction given by right-hand rule. In components: (a×b)ᵢ = εᵢⱼₖaⱼbₖ using permutation symbol εᵢⱼₖ. Properties: antisymmetric (a×b = -b×a), distributive, zero when parallel. Applications: torque M = r×F, angular momentum L = r×mv, curl (∇×v). Vector triple product iden

topics.name: Vectors
topics.description: Vectors are first-order tensors with magnitude and direction, represented by components in a basis: v = vᵢeᵢ. Vector operations include addition, scalar multiplication, dot product (a·b = aᵢbᵢ yields scalar), and cross product (a×b yields vector perpendicular to both). Magnitude: ‖v‖ = √(vᵢvᵢ). Unit vector: v̂ = v/‖v‖. Vectors represent physical quantities like displacement, velocity, force. Vecto

topics.name: Virtual power
topics.description: Virtual power principle (principle of virtual velocities) states: internal power equals external power for any kinematically admissible virtual velocity field. ∫v σ:D* dv = ∫s t·v* dS + ∫v ρb·v* dv, where * denotes virtual quantities, D* = sym(∇v*). Equivalent to Cauchy equation if holds for all v*. Provides weak form of momentum balance, foundation for variational methods and finite element analy

topics.name: Volume-preserving deformations
topics.description: Volume-preserving (isochoric) deformations maintain constant volume during motion: dv = dV, requiring Jacobian J = det(F) = 1. Material may distort but volume remains unchanged. Examples: incompressible fluids, rubber-like materials. Constraint det(F) = 1 reduces independent components of F. Pressure acts as Lagrange multiplier enforcing constraint. Decomposition F = J^(1/3)F̄ separates volumetric

TAM 470.name: Computational Mechanics
TAM 470.description: Modern computational mechanics: mappings and iterative methods; stability; convergence; consistency; numerical and symbolic solutions of ordinary and partial differential equations; finite-difference methods; the finite-element method; spectral methods. Applications to problems in solid mechanics, fluid mechanics, and dynamics.
objectives: Be able to select and apply numerical techniques appropriate for numerical approxi-mation of ordinary and partial differential equations that arise in mechanics.
Be able to analyze numerical techniques for accuracy, consistency, and stability.
Be able to devise and apply techniques for a posteriori estimation of numerical truncation errors.

Be able to design appropriate validation and verification procedures for a computational mechanics simulation in the context of the ethical responsibilities of the numerical analysis in engineering practice.

topics.name: Basic theory and numerical methods
topics.description: ODE theory covers existence and uniqueness (Lipschitz condition), stability (solution behavior), and stiffness. Numerical methods: Euler (explicit, first-order), implicit methods (backward Euler, trapezoidal), Runge-Kutta (explicit multi-stage, higher accuracy), and multistep methods (Adams, BDF). Key concepts: local truncation error (single step error), global error (accumulated), stability (boun

topics.name: Burgers equation
topics.description: Burgers equation ∂u/∂t + u∂u/∂x = ν∂²u/∂x² combines nonlinear advection with diffusion. Models simplified fluid dynamics, shock formation, turbulence studies. Nonlinearity u∂u/∂x causes wave steepening and shock formation. Viscosity ν∂²u/∂x² smooths shocks. As ν→0, approaches inviscid Burgers (develops shocks). Numerical challenges: capturing shocks without oscillations, handling nonlinearity, bal

topics.name: Computations as mappings
topics.description: Computational algorithms are mappings transforming input data to output results. Mathematical view: f: X → Y where X is input space (geometry, boundary conditions, material properties), Y is output space (displacements, stresses, velocities). Discrete approximation maps infinite-dimensional function spaces to finite-dimensional vector spaces. Algorithm properties include: existence (solution exist

topics.name: Diffusion equations
topics.description: Diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = α∇²u models heat conduction, mass diffusion, viscous effects. Parabolic PDE: infinite propagation speed, smoothing effect. Finite difference discretization: explicit (forward Euler in time) has stability restriction Δt ≤ Δx²/(2α) for 1D; implicit (backward Euler, Crank-Nicolson) unconditionally stable. Crank-Nicolson second-order accurate in time and space. Applications:

topics.name: Discussion of the advection equation
topics.description: Advection equation ∂u/∂t + c·∂u/∂x = 0 describes transport at velocity c. Exact solution: u(x,t) = u(x-ct,0) - wave traveling at speed c without change of form. Numerical solution challenging: centered schemes produce oscillations, upwind schemes introduce diffusion, explicit schemes have stability restriction (CFL condition: cΔt/Δx ≤ 1). Illustrates fundamental issues: numerical diffusion (smeari

topics.name: Examples from solid and fluid mechanics
topics.description: Solid mechanics ODEs: free vibration (ẍ + ω²x = 0), damped oscillation (ẍ + 2ζωẋ + ω²x = 0), forced vibration with resonance, multidegree-of-freedom systems (matrix equations), rigid body dynamics. Fluid mechanics examples: vortex dynamics, compressible flow equations in 1D, unsteady heat transfer, diffusion problems. These examples illustrate physical phenomena, mathematical structures, and numer

topics.name: Finite-difference methods
topics.description: Finite-difference methods approximate derivatives using function values at discrete grid points. Forward difference: f'(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-f(x))/h (O(h) error). Central difference: f'(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-f(x-h))/(2h) (O(h²) error). Higher derivatives: f''(x) ≈ (f(x+h)-2f(x)+f(x-h))/h². Applied to PDEs by discretizing spatial and temporal derivatives, creating algebraic equations. Structured grids simplify impl

topics.name: Finite element methods
topics.description: Finite Element Method (FEM) discretizes domain into elements with piecewise polynomial approximations. Weak formulation via weighted residuals or variational principle. Shape functions interpolate within elements; assembly creates global system. Advantages: handles complex geometries, systematic error control, mature theory and software. Process: mesh generation → element matrices → assembly → bou

topics.name: Finite volume methods
topics.description: Finite Volume Method (FVM) enforces conservation laws on control volumes. Integrates governing equations over cells: d/dt∫u dV = -∫F·n dS + ∫S dV, where F is flux. Fluxes computed at cell faces via interpolation and Riemann solvers. Inherently conservative - discretization exactly preserves conservation. Advantages: natural for conservation laws, handles shocks well, flexible geometries. Applicati

topics.name: Fourier transformation
topics.description: Fourier transform decomposes function into frequency components: F(ω) = ∫f(x)e^(-iωx)dx. Inverse: f(x) = ∫F(ω)e^(iωx)dω. Converts differentiation to multiplication: d/dx → iω. Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) for sampled data, computed efficiently via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) in O(N log N). Applications: solving PDEs in spectral methods, signal processing, filtering. Convolution becomes multip

topics.name: Functional expansions
topics.description: Functional expansions represent solutions as series of basis functions: u(x) = Σcₙφₙ(x). Basis functions: polynomials (Chebyshev, Legendre), trigonometric (Fourier), wavelets. Galerkin method: project residual onto basis functions to determine coefficients cₙ. Spectral convergence: exponential error decrease for smooth functions (vs. polynomial for finite difference/element). Advantages: high accu

topics.name: Introduction to computational mechanics
topics.description: Computational mechanics applies numerical methods to solve mechanical problems governed by differential equations. Bridges continuum mechanics theory with computer simulation. Enables analyzing complex geometries, nonlinear materials, and coupled phenomena intractable analytically. Major areas: computational solid mechanics (structural analysis, fracture, contact), computational fluid dynamics (CF

topics.name: Lagrangian and hamiltonian dynamics
topics.description: Lagrangian mechanics uses energy methods: L = T - V (kinetic minus potential energy). Euler-Lagrange equations: d/dt(∂L/∂q̇ᵢ) - ∂L/∂qᵢ = 0 for generalized coordinates qᵢ. Advantages: automatically incorporates constraints, coordinate-independent formulation, systematic derivation. Hamiltonian mechanics uses H = T + V: q̇ᵢ = ∂H/∂pᵢ, ṗᵢ = -∂H/∂qᵢ where pᵢ are conjugate momenta. Phase space (q,p) for

topics.name: Multistep methods
topics.description: Multistep methods use solution history for higher accuracy without multiple function evaluations per step. Adams-Bashforth (explicit): yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + h(b₀fₙ + b₁fₙ₋₁ + ... + bₖfₙ₋ₖ). Adams-Moulton (implicit): includes fₙ₊₁, more stable. Backward Differentiation Formulas (BDF): implicit, excellent for stiff problems. Predictor-corrector combines explicit prediction with implicit correction. Advantages

topics.name: Numerical and physical diffusion
topics.description: Physical diffusion: actual smoothing in physics (heat conduction, viscosity). Governed by diffusion equation ∂u/∂t = α∇²u. Numerical diffusion: artificial smoothing introduced by discretization errors. First-order upwind scheme for advection adds numerical diffusion O(Δx), acting like physical diffusion with coefficient ~cΔx/2. Dispersive errors cause oscillations. High-order schemes reduce numeri

topics.name: Numerical experiments
topics.description: Numerical experiments use computational models to study physical phenomena, parameter effects, and design variations. Analogous to physical experiments but using simulations. Advantages: cost-effective, safe (no physical failure), parametric studies easy, access to all field variables. Design of numerical experiments follows scientific method: hypothesis → simulation plan → execution → data analys

topics.name: Ordinary differential equations arising in mechanics
topics.description: Mechanical systems generate ODEs from governing laws. Examples: spring-mass-damper (mẍ + cẋ + kx = f), pendulum (θ̈ + (g/L)sinθ = 0), vibrating structures (Mü + Cu̇ + Ku = F), particle dynamics (mẍ = F). First-order systems: ẋ = f(x,t) arise from reducing higher-order equations or from inherently first-order physics. Initial value problems (IVPs) specify initial conditions. Stiff systems have wide

topics.name: Partial differential equations arising in mechanics
topics.description: Mechanics PDEs arise from conservation laws and constitutive relations. Elliptic: steady elasticity ∇²u = f, Laplace equation (steady heat, potential flow). Parabolic: diffusion ∂u/∂t = α∇²u, heat equation. Hyperbolic: wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∇²u, advection equation ∂u/∂t + c·∇u = 0. Navier-Stokes (fluid): ρ(∂v/∂t + v·∇v) = -∇p + μ∇²v. Elastodynamics: ρ∂²u/∂t² = ∇·σ + f. Classification determine

topics.name: Particle systems
topics.description: Particle systems model collections of discrete interacting particles. Governed by Newton's laws: mᵢẍᵢ = ΣFᵢⱼ + Fᵢ,ext. Forces include: gravity, springs, damping, contact, intermolecular (molecular dynamics). N-body problems computationally expensive: O(N²) for direct calculation, reduced to O(N log N) with fast multipole or tree methods. Applications: molecular dynamics, granular materials, astrop

topics.name: Physical interpretation.
topics.description: Physical interpretation extracts meaning from computational results, connecting numbers to engineering insight. Includes: stress/strain fields visualization, identifying load paths, understanding failure mechanisms, recognizing boundary effects, detecting numerical artifacts vs. physical phenomena. Sanity checks: equilibrium satisfaction, symmetry consistency, limit case comparison, dimensional an

topics.name: Physical interpretation of consistency and stability
topics.description: Consistency means numerical scheme approximates differential equation as grid refined: truncation error → 0 as Δx, Δt → 0. Physically: discrete equations approach continuous equations. Stability means numerical solution remains bounded and errors don't grow uncontrollably. Physically: numerical method doesn't amplify perturbations. Lax Equivalence Theorem: for linear PDEs, consistency + stability

topics.name: Problems, history, methodology
topics.description: Computational mechanics emerged from need to solve complex engineering problems. History: hand calculations → analog computers → digital computers → modern supercomputing. Key milestones: finite difference methods (1940s), finite element method (1960s), computational fluid dynamics development. Methodology follows scientific computing paradigm: problem formulation → mathematical model → discretiza

topics.name: Runge-kutta methods
topics.description: Runge-Kutta (RK) methods are single-step, multi-stage methods for ODEs. Classic RK4: k₁ = f(tₙ,yₙ), k₂ = f(tₙ+h/2,yₙ+hk₁/2), k₃ = f(tₙ+h/2,yₙ+hk₂/2), k₄ = f(tₙ+h,yₙ+hk₃), then yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + h(k₁+2k₂+2k₃+k₄)/6. Fourth-order accurate: O(h⁴) local error. No previous step history needed (single-step). Explicit RK for non-stiff problems; implicit RK for stiff. Adaptive RK (e.g., Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg) est

topics.name: Similarity solutions
topics.description: Similarity solutions reduce PDEs to ODEs by identifying self-similar structure. Occurs when physics independent of absolute scales, depending only on dimensionless combinations. Example: diffusion from point source uses similarity variable η = x/√(4αt), collapsing space-time PDE to ODE in η. Blasius solution for boundary layer, Sedov-Taylor blast wave solution. Dimensionless groups (Reynolds, Pran

topics.name: Spectral methods
topics.description: Spectral methods use global basis functions (Fourier, Chebyshev) for spatial discretization. Derivatives computed exactly in spectral space or via differentiation matrices. Spectral accuracy: exponential convergence for smooth solutions. Collocation method evaluates at specific points (Gauss-Lobatto). Applications: turbulence simulation (DNS), geophysical flows, stability analysis. Advantages: ver

topics.name: Stability and accuracy
topics.description: Stability: perturbations remain bounded, solution doesn't blow up. Analyzed via: von Neumann (Fourier modes), matrix eigenvalues, energy methods. Accuracy: discrete solution approaches exact solution. Measured by order: O(Δxᵖ) spatial, O(Δtᵍ) temporal. Trade-offs: explicit methods simple but conditionally stable (small time step required); implicit methods unconditionally stable but require solvin

topics.name: Systems with a finite number of modes
topics.description: Discrete systems have finite degrees of freedom, leading to systems of ODEs. Examples: lumped parameter models, mass-spring chains, structural finite elements, modal analysis of continuous systems. Equations: Mq̈ + Cq̇ + Kq = f, where M (mass), C (damping), K (stiffness) are matrices, q is displacement vector. Modal analysis decomposes motion into natural modes (eigenvectors) oscillating at natura

topics.name: The nature of computational solid and fluid mechanics
topics.description: Computational solid mechanics solves elasticity, plasticity, fracture, and structural dynamics problems. Lagrangian description (following material points) typical. Challenges include material nonlinearity, geometric nonlinearity, contact, and failure. Computational fluid mechanics (CFD) solves Navier-Stokes equations for incompressible/compressible flow. Eulerian description (observing fixed loca

topics.name: Vibrations
topics.description: Vibrations involve oscillatory motion about equilibrium. Single DOF: mẍ + cẋ + kx = F(t) with natural frequency ωₙ = √(k/m), damping ratio ζ = c/(2√(km)). Free vibration: undamped (sinusoidal), underdamped (exponential decay × sinusoid), critically damped (fastest return), overdamped (slow return). Forced vibration: resonance when driving frequency ≈ ωₙ, frequency response function H(ω). Multi-DOF

topics.name: Wave equation
topics.description: Wave equation ∂²u/∂t² = c²∇²u describes elastic waves, acoustics, electromagnetics. Hyperbolic PDE: finite propagation speed c, preserves discontinuities. D'Alembert solution (1D): u(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct) - superposition of left and right traveling waves.
